articles.title: Shrieking Toad and Dancing Ant
articles.author: John Samony
articles.description: Two campers learn the importance of treating others the way you want to be treated.
articles.intro: In this story, the narrator, Jay, gets a nickname at summer camp.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the important events in the story.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: A lot of kids who go to summer camp get nicknames.
I’d been at Camp Eggleton for only two days before I got mine.
Shrieking Toad.
Why couldn’t I have gotten a cool name like Running Bear or Soaring Eagle?
I guess it was my own fault.

A bunch of us campers were on our first nature walk. When we broke for lunch, we all sat down on rows of logs lining the path. I was on my third handful of trail mix when a tree frog dropped onto my shirt from the branches above.
I shrieked. It was totally involuntary. I didn’t even know I could make a sound like that.
Most kids laughed it off and forgot about it.
Except for Stan. He wouldn’t let it go.
Whenever I’d walk by, he’d shout, “Hey, look. It’s Shrieking Toad. Show us how you can hop!”
I corrected him that it had been an eastern gray tree frog, not a toad, but that just made things worse.

Three days later, Counselor Matt made the announcement that would change everything. “Gather round, Eggies. Last night, 10 treasures were hidden in the woods. The two-person team to find the most treasures gets something cold.”
Excited murmurs rippled through the camp. Thoughts of ice cream froze our brains.
Counselor Matt started reading off pairs of names. The longer he read, the more nervous I became. My nerves got completely fried when he said, “And finally, Jay and Stan.”
I hung my head, shaking it in disbelief.
Stan shouted, “Just great. Shrieking Toad’s going to give me warts!”
The counselors handed out sacks to hold our discoveries. Everyone started parading past me like an army of ants marching to the nearest picnic blanket.
“Hey, guys,” Stan called to the group, and I cringed at what might come out of his mouth. “If the mosquitoes are bugging you, just ask Shrieking Toad to eat them for you. Toads love bugs!”
One kid chuckled, but most of the campers were already concentrating on looking for hidden treasures.
Soon, all the teams had wandered off, leaving Stan and me alone.
Then I had a great idea, surprising myself that I hadn't thought of it earlier. “You know, Stan, if we split up, we’ll have a better chance of finding stuff.”
He stopped. “Yeah, that makes sense. So I guess I’d better do what I’m toad.” He laughed at his pun and wandered down the trail.
Ten minutes later, I heard something that reminded me of myself a few days ago.
I ran toward the sound. I never would have believed it if I hadn’t seen it with my own eyes.
Stan was jumping around like a monkey, shrieking and yelling something that sounded like “Dance! Dance!”
Seeing him scratching at his body and swatting at his clothes made me realize what he was really saying: “Ants! Ants!”
​​I did what had to be done.
I shuffled him about 15 yards and into a mud puddle. He was surprised at first, then he must have realized why I was doing it. The itch was going away and so were the ants.
Since Stan and I were both covered in mud, we decided to head back to the cabin.
“I guess you’re going to have a pretty good story for everyone later,” Stan said sheepishly.
“Yeah, I guess so. You’ll probably be known as Dancing Ant from now on.”

That night, we all sat around the campfire. Between the shadows that flickered on our faces, I could see Stan’s worry as clear as daylight. He was waiting for it. But I never said anything. I knew how it felt to be teased, and it wasn’t fun.
The next day was Game Day. When I was picked for a basketball team, someone called, “No fair! Shrieking Toad’s a natural jumper!”
Stan yelled out, “Knock it off, guys. His name is Jay.”

I'm not going to say I would have minded being known as Running Bear or Soaring Eagle. But for the last week of camp, being known as plain old “Jay,” and Stan’s friend, turned out to be pretty cool too.

articles.title: Walnuts and Watermelons
articles.author: Mehded Maryam Sinclair
articles.description: A boy wonders why small walnuts grow from big trees and large watermelons grow from small vines.
articles.intro: Mehded Maryam Sinclair has written for Highlights . In this folktale, Nasreddin Hodja wonders why watermelons and walnuts grow the way they do. Hodja's character is a repeating figure in folktales from the Muslim world.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens to Hodja.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Nasreddin Hodja was sitting under a great big tree. He saw tiny walnuts growing all over it. Then he looked at the garden. He saw a great big watermelon growing on a skinny little vine. He looked again. Tiny walnuts on the great big tree. A great big watermelon on the skinny little vine. “If I had made the world,” he said, “I would have put the great big watermelon on the great big tree and the tiny walnuts on the skinny little vine.” Hodja was tired. He took off his turban and went to sleep. "Walnuts and Watermelons" by Laura Jacobsen

He spied that watermelon in the garden. “Oh my!” he said. “It’s a good thing I didn’t make the world, or that watermelon would have 1. a head covering for a man 2. articles.title: Hothead
articles.author: Shelby Seim
articles.description: A friend helps a new soccer teammate by setting boundaries.
articles.intro: Shelby Seim is a teacher and writer from Washington, D.C. In this short story, a group of friends playing soccer learn an important lesson about what it means to be part of a team.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Luis feels.
articles.permissions: "Hothead" by Shelby Seim. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Luis scanned the field. The soccer ball was tucked between his feet. He searched and searched until… there! His friend Nicholas was wide open. He faked like he was going to pass to a teammate with a defender on him but at the last second passed the ball to Nicholas instead. Nicholas trapped the ball and started dribbling. Two defenders rushed towards him. But it didn’t matter. He was miles ahead of them, just like Luis had planned. Nicholas swung his leg back, kicked, and…
GOAL!!! Luis leaped up and down, celebrating. Nicholas grinned at him.
Just then, the teacher blew her whistle. It was time to head back inside for their next class.
“Thanks for the assist,” Nicholas said as he jogged up. “But if you’d seen me open earlier then I could have scored two goals in that time.”
“Yeah, yeah,” Luis said, “You’re such a pro, you could have dribbled circles around everyone before they even realized you had the ball.”

“You know it!” Nicholas said, totally unfazed by Luis’ sarcasm. The boys all grabbed for their Gatorade and water bottles, teasing each other about how they played and bragging about their own skills. Just then, Devon walked up, his face twisted in anger.

“What were you DOING out there Luis?!!” he yelled. Luis rolled his eyes. Devon had started playing soccer at recess with them a few weeks ago. He was a nice enough guy when he felt like things in the game had gone his way. But when he felt like they hadn’t… watch out.
“I was playing soccer. What were you doing?” Luis asked.
“Yeah, whatever, duh. But why didn’t you pass to me? You looked like you were about to and then you didn’t! I was WAY closer to the goal than Nicholas was.”
“Dude, you weren’t open. It’s just that simple.”
“I’m fast though!” Devon protested, his voice still loud and angry. “I could have lost Alex and Bayo like that!” He snapped his fingers loudly.
“Yeah, right!” jumped in Alex, who had been playing on defense. “You wish you could lose me!”
As the group walked in, Devon continued. Luis ignored him. He didn’t like being dragged into arguments. So that’s what he did with Devon most of the time — just ignored him. It almost always worked.

“I don’t like the way that Devon talks after soccer,” Nicholas said. Luis looked up from his phone. They took the same bus after school, although Nicholas got off at the first stop and Luis didn’t get off until the last stop.
“What do you mean?” he asked as Nicholas sat down.
“He’s always yelling at people and bragging about himself.”
“You brag about yourself,” Luis pointed out.
“Yeah, but I don’t yell at people,” said Nicholas. Luis nodded. That was true. “It isn’t right,” Nicholas continued. “He’s a bad sport. All that yelling. It makes soccer less fun.”
“So what do you want to do about it?” Luis asked. He could tell that Nicholas had a plan. His eyes had brightened, and he looked excited.
“I want to move to the other part of the soccer field. And then tell him that he can’t play with us anymore.”
“What? But isn’t that… isn’t that leaving him out?” Luis asked.
“Yeah, I mean I guess? Kind of.” Nicholas put his head down and started playing with his hands. “But come on. You know it was way more fun last year before he started playing with us.”
“Yeah… I mean, you have a point. But I don’t think he means to get so over the top. Why can’t we just ignore him? That’s what I’ve been doing.”
“Well I already talked to all the other guys,” Nicholas said. He looked up. His eyes were set in determination. “And they all agree with me.”
“All of them? You’ve talked to all of them?” Luis asked, startled. The bus started to slow down for Nicholas’ stop. Nicholas stood up, swinging his backpack back onto his shoulder.
“Yeah, I have. I know everyone has been getting sick of Devon ruining soccer for a while now. We all agree that it was more fun before he started playing with us. Even you said so.”
Luis gulped. He had said that. But still. Having all the boys who played soccer at recess get together tell Devon that he couldn’t play with them anymore? That seemed extreme.

He watched as Nicholas ran off the bus. He slumped forward in his seat. Now it was his turn to stare at his lap. He spent the rest of the ride trying to figure out what he should do tomorrow at recess.

By the time he walked in the front door, Luis still didn’t have an answer. His older brother, Omar, welcomed him with a wide smile from where he stood in front of the stove. Omar was the most weirdly cheerful person he had ever met. It was like nothing ever put him in a bad mood.

“What’s up, Luis?” Omar asked as he stirred a simmering pan of arroz con pollo. Omar went to college across town but saved money by living at home. He cooked dinner for Luis and his two younger sisters whenever their mom ended up stuck at work. Their dad always got home too late to make dinner anyway. Luis didn’t mind. He’d never tell either of his parents, but Omar could already cook circles around the both of them.
“Not much,” Luis said with a sigh, dropping his backpack onto the table. He unzipped it and a bunch of crumpled papers fell out.
“Bro, you gotta clean that out,” Omar said with a laugh. “That’s nasty!”
“It’s fine!” Luis shot back with more anger than he meant. He couldn’t help it though. He glared at Omar.
“Whoa, okay!” Omar said with his hands up. “Out with it. What’s wrong?”
It bugged Luis how Omar always knew if something was wrong. Stupid older brother telepathy.
“Nothing… it’s just… there’s this kid at school…”
Omar swung a chair around and pulled it up to the table. “What’s going on?”
Luis told him about Nicholas’ plan. Omar listened thoughtfully, and then said, “And you don’t want to leave him out?”
“No! I think that’s really mean. It’s just a game. Everyone should be able to play.”
“So tell Nicholas that it’s no big deal and that you want Devon to play.”
“I tried! But Nicholas wouldn’t listen. And also, well, I don’t want to tell Devon he can’t play with us. But Nicholas has a point. It isn’t fun when Devon yells at people.”
“Ahhhh,” said Omar, as he leaned back in the chair. “I have a question. Have any of you tried talking to Devon about this?”
“I don’t think so,” Luis said, scrunching his brow. “Maybe? I don’t know.”
“It sounds like what you need is a boundary,” Omar said. Luis rolled his eyes. Ever since Omar had started college, he’d been full of ideas and quick to lecture.
“Yeah, man, a boundary!” Omar leaned in. “Like alright, listen, when you play soccer, how do you know if someone is offsides?”
“When there are too many players across the line.”
“And what’s that line called?”
“... a boundary,” Luis mumbled begrudgingly.

“Exactly! So how can you expect Devon to know he’s crossing a line with how he talks to you if you don’t tell him?” Omar leaned back again, clearly thinking he had just solved all of Luis’ problems. Luis rolled his eyes again.
“Shouldn’t it be obvious that it isn’t fun if you yell at people?” he asked.
“Maybe it's obvious to you. But not to him. Just like it’s obvious to me that you should start setting the table for dinner. But apparently not to you.”
Luis sighed and started grabbing plates.

Luis thought about what Omar said. And he decided there wasn’t much to lose. Besides, it was the only thing he could think of that didn’t involve shutting Devon out or letting him keep yelling at people. So, the next day before school started he asked Devon if he could talk with him. His palms were sweaty, even though he’d already thought about what he was going to say.
“Listen Devon,” he started. “We like having you play soccer with us at recess. But none of the other guys like being yelled at and — ”
“I don’t yell at people,” Devon immediately interrupted.

“You yelled at me the other day because I didn’t pass to you,” Luis pointed out calmly. “And you are yelling right now.” Devon opened his mouth again to reply, but Luis cut him off. “Can you let me finish first?” Devon thought about it and nodded, his mouth a tight line.

“None of the guys like being yelled at. I know that it stinks to not get passed to, but there are other ways of dealing with it. And the other guys, well, they said that if you keep yelling at them, then they don’t want to keep playing soccer with you.”
Devon’s anger drained. “I love playing soccer with you guys,” he said, his voice much quieter now.

“Yeah. Which is why I’m talking to you. Listen, you can’t yell at us anymore. If you really need to yell, I don’t know, go kick the soccer ball really hard or something instead. But you can’t keep yelling at us if you want to keep playing with us. Being yelled at isn’t fun and playing soccer at recess is supposed to be fun.”
“Okay,” Devon said. “I’ll do my best.”
At recess that day, Luis hurried out onto the field. He caught up with Nicholas on his way over to the pitch and told him what he’d told Devon.
“Just give him another shot, okay? If he crosses the line and yells at someone today, then I’m okay with us not playing with him anymore. But he deserves a chance.”

The other guys were slowly walking up. Nicholas crossed his arms over his chest but didn’t say anything. They split into teams, just like always, and started to play. Luis could barely keep his eyes on the ball, he was watching Devon so much. Things seemed to be going well. Devon kept his word and didn’t yell at anyone.

But then — the ball rolled over the line towards a patch of trees. That meant there was a chance for their team to take a corner kick. Devon, who was close to the ball, started running to go and get it. So did Justin. They reached the ball at the same time.

“I’ve got it!” Justin called out, grabbing the ball. Luis watched as Devon slowed to a stop. He seemed to pause for a second. To Luis, it felt like minutes. Finally, Devon nodded. Justin put the ball on the line, wound up, and kicked. Everyone could tell that he meant to pass it to Nicholas, who was standing nearby. But the kick sent the ball about five feet to the right of Nicholas. Alex, who was on the other team, jumped in front of Nicholas, quick as a rabbit, and took control of the ball. Before anyone could react, Alex had dribbled up the field, wound up for a kick, and shot the ball.

It landed right in the center of the goal. The other team threw their arms up to celebrate. But Luis barely glanced at them. He was too busy watching Devon. Devon’s lips were tight and his fists were clenched. Luis watched him nervously and then started to jog closer to them. He had to get there before the explosion.
Just as he was close enough to say something, he saw Devon close his eyes and take a deep breath. Luis stopped in his tracks.
“That was a good kick,” he heard Devon tell Justin. “It was like Alex came out of nowhere.”
Luis released a breath he hadn’t realized he’d been holding.
After the whistle blew for the end of recess, Luis jogged over to Devon.
“Thanks man,” Luis said, “that was a fun game.”
“Yeah,” Devon agreed. “I had fun playing with you all.” They didn’t talk about anything else, but Devon was smiling, and Luis could feel himself start to grin, too.

Nicholas caught up to Luis, joining him as they lined up to go back inside. Luis glanced at him, suddenly feeling nervous. But Nicholas didn’t look upset. Actually, he looked happy. “What can I say? You were right. Devon deserved a second chance. And now we get to have one of the best players on our team without anyone getting yelled at.” At that, Luis practically beamed. He felt on top of the world.

Devon kept playing soccer with them for the rest of the school year. He yelled a couple of times, but then Luis reminded him of the boundary he’d set, sometimes with words, other times with a look. Devon would apologize to the guys he’d yelled at and ask to keep playing. Luis felt proud of himself for finding a way to include Devon. But he never did tell Omar how well his advice had worked out. He didn’t think his older brother’s head needed to get any bigger.

articles.title: Lazy Anansi
articles.author: Ghanaian Folktale
articles.description: A lazy spider learns an important lesson.
articles.intro: In this folktale from Ghana, a country in West Africa, a lazy spider named Anansi learns an important lesson.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Anansi interacts with his friends.
articles.permissions: Ghanaian folktale Author - Ghanaian folktale Language - English Level - Longer paragraphs © African Storybook Initiative 2014 Creative Commons: Attribution 4.0 Source https://www.africanstorybook.org/

blocks.text: There was a spider called Anansi. Anansi was too lazy to cook his own food. Instead, he used to visit his friends and eat the delicious food they had cooked.

One day, Anansi was passing Rabbit's house when he smelled green vegetables cooking. He was very excited. Rabbit said to Anansi, “They're not quite ready yet. You can help me to wash up while we wait.” Anansi replied, “Sorry, I have things to do. I'll come back later.”

“How will I call you when they're ready?” asked Rabbit. Anansi thought for a minute. “I'll spin a web,” he said. “I'll tie one end around my leg and one end to your pot. When the greens are ready, pull on the web string. I'll come right away.” So Anansi tied the web to the pot, and walked on.

Anansi saw Monkey and his wife, cooking beans in a large pot. “Come and join us! The beans are nearly ready.” Monkey said. Anansi replied, “Sorry, I have things to do. Let me tie one end of this web around my leg and one end to your pot. When the beans are ready, pull on the web string, and I'll come.”

As Anansi walked by Warthog's house, he smelled sweet potatoes. Warthog told Anansi, “My pot is full of sweet potatoes and honey! Come and share my food with me. Take this fork and help me to stir it.” Anansi replied, “I'll come back later. Let me tie one end of this web around my leg and one end to your pot. When the sweet potatoes are ready, pull on the web string, and I'll come.”

By the time Anansi arrived at the river, each of his eight legs was tied to a pot of delicious food. Then, Anansi felt a pull on one of his legs. “Rabbit's food is ready!” Anansi thought, licking his lips.

He felt a second pull. And a third. And a fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, eighth pull. Everyone was pulling on the web strings at the same time! “Stop! Stop!” he cried in pain, as his legs were stretched thinner and thinner. But no one could hear him.

Finally, the web strings could hold no longer. They snapped, one by one. Anansi rolled into the river to soothe his painful legs. But his legs would not return to their normal shape. Anansi was too embarrassed to go to any of his friends that day.

options.label: People who are lazy don't get to benefit from others' hard work.
Sometimes people take advantage of the kindness of their friends.
Sharing food with others is a good way to show that you care.
People are less likely to share with you if you don't help them in some way.

articles.title: The Artist's Challenge
articles.author: Pamela Love
articles.description: An artist holds a contest to see who is the best painter in Greece.
articles.intro: Pamela Love is a children's author. In this story, a man tries to prove he is the best artist in ancient Greece.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Zeuxis’s thoughts and actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Artist's Challenge" by Pamela Love, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: All Greece was talking about the gifted young artist, Parrhasius — or so it seemed to another painter, Zeuxis. “Bah!” he snorted. “I could paint better than he could when I was a baby smearing mud on the wall.”
Other Greeks felt differently. Officials were buying Parrhasius’s paintings to display in public buildings. Young artists were studying his works as they did those of master painters like Zeuxis.

Outraged, Zeuxis said, “I’ve worked long and hard for fame. Didn’t I invent new ways to make paintings more lifelike?” This was true. Zeuxis used color and shading that made his paintings of heroes and the gods look more three-dimensional.

Yet as the young artist’s recognition grew, Zeuxis believed that Parrhasius was becoming his rival. An upstart, he thought. He doesn’t deserve such acclaim so soon. Often Zeuxis visited places where he could examine Parrhasius’s work and search for flaws. To his annoyance, he never found any, but that didn’t stop him from rolling his eyes and stalking away in disgust whenever someone was watching.

Time passed, and Zeuxis grew so jealous of the young artist that he was having trouble sleeping at night. When he closed his eyes, all he could see was his rival’s work. This cannot go on, he thought.

One morning, he challenged Parrhasius to a contest. Each artist would paint one picture. Three other artists would judge which painting was the more lifelike of the two. To be fair, Zeuxis chose judges who were not his friends but were widely thought to be masters of painting. More importantly, they were even older than Zeuxis. They won’t be falsely impressed by someone just because he is new, he thought.
“Zeuxis greatly honors me with this challenge,” said Parrhasius. “Of course, I shall accept.”

The great day came. People from all over the city of Ephesus came to watch. The crowd parted to let Zeuxis through. He carefully carried his entry, which was covered with a beautifully embroidered veil.

“As challenger, I shall reveal my painting first.” Setting his work on an easel, Zeuxis proudly swept off its cover. Everyone was expecting another grand portrait — perhaps some great ruler or goddess.
Instead, Zeuxis had painted grapes.

A few giggles were quickly hushed as people looked more carefully at the picture. These grapes were so plump, purple, and ripe that it seemed as if they could be plucked from the wood panel as easily as from a vine.
Loud applause had barely begun before several crows swooped down and began pecking the picture.
“They’re trying to eat the fruit!” a judge shouted, waving at the birds. “Shoo! Shoo!”
Zeuxis shook his head. “Fret not! Even birds recognize my greatness.”
When the applause died down, Zeuxis held up one hand for silence. “Wait, my friends. The contest is not yet over.”
Briskly, he strode over to Parrhasius, with the judges trailing behind him. His face calm, the young artist stood beside an easel, his painting covered with a rough cloth.

“Well!” said Zeuxis, stopping inches away. “Behold the great Parrhasius. You say I honor you by this competition, yet how do you present your work? Covered by a wrinkled, paint-spattered rag! There’s even a spider in one corner. You insult the judges and me.” Sharply, Zeuxis commanded, “Unveil your painting! Let all of Ephesus judge its worth.”
Bowing, Parrhasius said, “Good Zeuxis, why don’t you unveil my work?” Someone looking closely might have seen a twinkle in his eye.

Trying to seize the rag, Zeuxis’s nails scratched wood, not cloth. His eyes narrowed. He slid his fingers lightly across the surface of what he now knew wasn’t a rag after all. The spider in the corner would never spin a web.
It was all a painting, and he, the finest painter in Greece (or so he thought), hadn’t known it until he touched it.
Slowly, he began to clap. Everyone else joined in. A long time later, when the applause died down, Zeuxis said quietly, “I fooled the crows. You fooled me.”

articles.title: The Mystery of Mary Celeste
articles.author: Meg Moss
articles.description: Meg Moss explores what might have happened to the Mary Celeste in 1847.
articles.intro: Mary Celeste was a ship that set sail in 1872. In this informational text, Meg Moss explores the mysteries surrounding the ship and its missing passengers.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what may have happened to the Mary Celeste’s passengers.
articles.permissions: "The Mystery of the Mary Celeste" by Meg Moss, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: In 1872, the ship Mary Celeste was found drifting with no one on board. Where did they go? It’s one of the great mysteries of the sea.
An Ordinary Voyage

In early November 1872, two small, sturdy sailing ships, the Mary Celeste and the Dei Gratia , set out from New York bound for Genoa, Italy. The autumn had been stormy, but both captains expected a routine voyage. Captain Benjamin Briggs of the Mary Celeste and Captain David Morehouse of the Dei Gratia were friends and dined together before they set sail. They would never see each other again.

The Mary Celeste had a crew of seven. She had just been repaired and was in tip-top shape. On this trip, the hold was packed with 1,701 oak barrels of nondrinkable alcohol for use in industry. Accompanying Captain Briggs was his wife, Sarah, and their young daughter, Sophia. Sarah brought along her sewing machine and a small piano. Their seven-year-old son had stayed home so as not to miss school. They expected to be gone a few months.
November 25, 1872

On the morning of November 25, after weeks of bad weather, the crew of the Mary Celeste found themselves near the Azores, islands in the eastern Atlantic. The cook prepared the first hot meal they’d had in days, and the first mate made the usual 8:00 a.m. entry in his log.
Then everyone on board disappeared.
December 5, 1872

The Dei Gratia left New York several days after the Mary Celeste . On December 5, the crew spotted a ship pitching around with its sails badly set. Captain Morehouse knew something was wrong. As they drew closer, the captain could see that it was the Mary Celeste .

A boarding party went to offer help. At first, everything looked normal. They found the ship clean and orderly. In the cabin, the log book’s last entry, dated November 25, was just a note of the ship’s course, nothing unusual. The crews’ belongings were all stowed neatly in their lockers. Food and water for six months filled the hold. Even the money box was intact. There was no evidence of fire, storm damage, or foul play. And no sign of the crew, or Captain Briggs and his family.

On closer inspection, they did find a few odd things. The ship’s compass was missing. One of the pumps used for getting water out of the hold had been taken apart and lay on the deck. And one lifeboat was gone. Hack marks in the deck suggested the crew had rushed to cut the lines that held it.
So it looked like the crew had left the ship in a hurry — but why?
It’s a Mystery

Crew members from the Dei Gratia sailed the Mary Celeste to Gibraltar in Spain. There, an official court of inquiry looked into the mystery of the ship and her missing crew. They combed over the ship searching for clues. But the court could not decide what had happened. Since then, many explanations have been offered, some more likely than others.
Mutiny!

The chief judge in Gibraltar was convinced that the crew had mutinied and murdered the captain and his family. But no evidence supported this idea. There was no blood or any sign of a struggle, and Briggs was well liked and a fair captain.
Waterspout!
Did a seaquake (an underwater earthquake) or a waterspout (a tornado over the water) strike fear into the crew? Maybe, but these are rare, and someone else would have noticed it.
Pirates!
Pirates had been known to attack ships and kill the crew. But nothing, not even money, was missing.
Foul Play!

Even the crew of the Dei Gratia came under suspicion. Abandoned ships belong to whoever finds them, so they stood to make some money by claiming she had been adrift. But the captains were friends. And in the end, they were rewarded only a small amount.
The Truth?
The mystery of the Mary Celeste endures to this day. But modern historians think it’s most likely the crew left the ship because they were afraid that it was about to sink or blow up.
We’re sinking!

The Mary Celeste had recently had a lot of repairs to her wood. And her last cargo was dusty coal. It’s possible that sawdust and coal dust had clogged the ship’s pumps — which might explain the pump parts found on deck. The captain may have feared that his ship was filling with water with no way to pump it out.
We’re about to explode!

But the most popular explanation involves the cargo, those 1,701 barrels of undrinkable alcohol. When this alcohol evaporates it makes fumes that can explode with a spark. Captain Briggs was nervous about carrying such dangerous stuff. The original investigation found that nine of the alcohol barrels were empty. It’s possible that they leaked, filling the inside of the ship with dangerous fumes.

At the back of the Mary Celeste , the Dei Gratia boarding party found one more odd thing — a long, frayed rope trailing into the water. When ships were in trouble, sailors sometimes got into a lifeboat and tied it to the ship on a long line to wait out the danger at a safe distance. If alcohol vapors filled the hold, the captain may have ordered the hatches open to air the ship out and then commanded everyone into the lifeboat in case of explosion. The dangling line suggests that Briggs did just that, hoping to return to his ship.

But it wasn’t to be. The line unfortunately broke, and the Briggses and their crew watched the Mary Celeste sail away without them. At the mercy of the waves, the little lifeboat would not have lasted long.

options.label: Many passengers on boats go missing for unknown reasons.
Mary Celeste and Dei Gratia were boats that sailed from New York in 1872.
It is important to be prepared for the many challenges that may come up while sailing.
There are still many questions about what happened to the passengers of Mary Celeste.

articles.title: Milly and the Great Seattle Fire
articles.author: Carissa Sumner
articles.description: A cat lives through the Great Seattle Fire of 1889.
articles.intro: In 1889, Seattle, Washington was a busy logging town. This time period in American history is called Westward Expansion, because many people from the east coast were moving west in hopes of finding more land to live on. Many of these people settled in Seattle. Seattle is home to the Duwamish, a Native American tribe. The Duwamish are the original, or first, people to live on this land. Many Duwamish had their homes taken by force as settlers moved west. During this time, many buildings in Seattle were made of wood and logs. On June 6, 1889, a fire began at a local woodworking shop. The fire destroyed 25 blocks of businesses and homes. In this short story, a cat named Milly experiences the Great Seattle Fire.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Milly feels about her home in Seattle.
articles.permissions: "Milly and the Great Seattle Fire" by Carissa Sumner, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: A pickier cat might have turned her nose up at licking sawdust out of her fur. But to Milly, Seattle was home. Where else could she feast on the trash left behind the Queen City Chop House, play with the mice at Yesler’s Mill, nap beside the whir of the Seattle Times printing press, enjoy a show at Frye’s Opera House, and end the day with a salmon dinner on the Elliot Bay waterfront?

There was also the prestige of living in a woodpile beside the first steam-powered sawmill on Puget Sound. Old Henry Yesler couldn’t have done it without Milly. The wharf where he turned logs into lumber would be overrun by rats without her.

June 6, 1889, was a warm, dry day. Milly had checked a few naps off her to-do list and was ready to round up some mice for a game of tag when she smelled something that made her nose twitch and her fur prickle. Smoke!
Was it the Washington Iron Works blacksmith shop? A Duwamish tribe cooking fire? It seemed to be coming from Front Street.
Curious, Milly decided to save the mice for later. She tiptoed daintily over the sawdust to investigate.
Victor Clairmont’s woodworking shop was on fire!
Kaboom! The liquor store next door exploded.

Milly scrambled away as the fire leaped from building to building. She looked for a way back to the wharf, but by now the crowd was as thick as the smoke. The clanging bells competed with the roaring wind as the volunteer firefighters raced by Milly, pulling their hose cart. It was no use. The tide was out, and the firefighters could not get enough water from Elliot Bay. Milly yelped in indignation as people carrying beds, barrels, and newspaper files trod over her paws.
“The Opera House is on fire!”
Milly turned to look and noticed something else on fire — her tail! How humiliating. A cat with its tail on fire.

Milly, tail lit like a torch, ran by the courthouse where a bucket brigade had gathered. The first man handed a bucket of water to the second man. The second man handed the bucket of water to the third man. The third man was supposed to hand the bucket of water to the fourth man, but when he saw Milly’s tail, he turned that bucket over and doused her with water.

Drenched (but no longer on fire), Milly wondered which way to turn. On one side of her was the Occidental Hotel and on the other side was Trinity Church. Both were falling to the flames. At Henry Yesler’s house, she could see men with wet blankets slapping at sparks on the windows.

Gathering what little dignity she had left, Milly held her singed tail high and stepped gingerly across James Street and Cherry Street. She tried to keep an air of confidence while she searched for a place to rest, but her tail drooped a little more with each step.

To make matters worse, she had to watch out for rats. Flushed from their homes by the flames, every rat in Seattle was scurrying about the streets. On a different day, Milly might have taken advantage of this situation and put a few rats in their place. But not today.
Milly needed somewhere to be alone and rest. The sawmill was destroyed, the woodpile she used as a bed gone.

She followed all the people seeking shelter at the armory, where women bustled to and fro, converting a place to store weapons into a place to serve dinner. A makeshift sign outside said “Tacoma Relief Bureau.” Relief was just what Milly needed. She even caught herself wishing one of those bustling women would take her home and make her a pet. Milly had never longed to sleep indoors before, but now she imagined what it might be like to eat from a dish and sleep on something soft.
She crept past the militiamen keeping watch over the relief efforts, left a trail of gray paw prints up the stairs, and paused at the open door of a quiet room.

Milly slipped inside and peeked out the window. The embers were dying. Wood buildings had been reduced to piles of rubble. Ashes had replaced sawdust. Now that Yesler’s Mill was burned down, where would she live? The fire had eliminated the need for her mouse and rat services. What else could a mill cat do?
Exhausted, she rested her head on her paws without even bothering to lick off the sawdust and ashes.
When Milly woke up, a militiaman was staring at her, the brass buttons on his coat winking at her in the sunlight. Milly stared back.
“The cat lives!”

Other militiamen crowded around. “Poor thing! Look at its tail!” All these militiamen staring at her and not even a chance to tidy up. Milly got to work immediately, licking her matted fur, erasing the evidence of what she had endured to find safety.
Salmon on a tin plate. “Here, pretty kitty!”
Pretty kitty! Now, that’s more like it. When she finished the meal, Milly washed again, spreading her toes to lick the tufts of fur between each one.
Now, where to go. Where to go. No need to look lost, like one of those newcomers from back East.
“We’ll make this cat our mascot!”
Yes, that was it. Someone needed to care for the militia while they maintained order in the ashes of Seattle. Milly was the cat for the job!

She graciously accepted a second helping of salmon and showed her appreciation by allowing the militiamen to pet her. Curling up in a militiaman’s cap, Milly thought it was a good thing she wasn’t picky. Other cats would turn their noses up at the ashes. But not Milly. Seattle was home.

options.label: Fires are easy to put out.
There is no place like home.
Cats can be grumpy animals.
Be kind to animals large and small.

The Great Seattle Fire caused damage to Seattle, but afterwards the city was rebuilt in a way that was much safer. Can you think of other examples of times when a bad event led to good things in the future? This can be from your own experience, or events you have read about or seen in art, history, movies, etc.

In the end of the story, the militiamen make Milly their mascot. Do you think it is important for teams or groups to have mascots? Why or why not? What is your favorite mascot? Why? What does it represent?

articles.title: MOON Rain
articles.author: Tiare Solorzano
articles.description: A brother makes a creative gift for his sister's birthday on the moon.
articles.intro: This short story is set in a future where people are living on the moon. In this story, two siblings, Collin and Carina, talk about plans for celebrating Carina's birthday on the moon.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how life on the moon is different from life on earth.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Collin was lying on his back, tossing a baseball toward the ceiling when his bedroom door opened. His sister, Carina, barged in and perched on the edge of his bed. Collin could tell she’d been crying.
“What’s wrong?” Collin asked. “Homesick again?”
Carina nodded.
“Yeah, me too.” Collin sighed.
Their family had moved to the Moon colony nearly four months ago. It still didn’t feel like home. The dwellings were cozy, but outside everything was gray and rocky and lifeless.
“I miss my friends,” Carina said gloomily. “And green grass and swimming, and — and everything. I even miss the sound of rain on the roof.”
The colony was housed inside a huge transparent dome. There was no weather there at all — no rain or snow or even wind.
“Worst of all,” Carina continued, “my birthday is in two days, and I don’t think Mom and dad are planning anything special. They’re too busy.”

She’s probably right , thought Collin. Life on the Moon was hectic for scientists like their parents. He doubted they had time to plan a party. And how could they anyway? Things such as balloons and birthday candles didn’t exist on the colony.
“Don’t worry.” Collin put an arm around Carina’s shoulder.
The next day, after school, Collin stayed behind to talk to his teacher, Mr. Shaw.
“A birthday party?” Mr. Shaw frowned. “I’m not sure anyone on the Moon has ever had one. We don’t even have the rations to make a cake.”

Collin sighed. If he couldn’t plan a party, maybe he could give his sister a gift. But what? Moon rocks? He and Carina had a bunch of those already. He could give her one of his toys, but all he had was his baseball and a bag full of Legos. Room in their transport ship had been limited, so each child had been allowed to bring only two toys from Earth.
Collin wandered toward the agricultural center, where his mom worked. Some days, he helped her take measurements or harvest crops. Today he found her taking notes in a patch of tall tube-like plants.
“What are those?” he asked.
“Bamboo plants,” said Mom. “They’re strong and grow fast. They make good building material.”

Collin remembered something made of bamboo that a classmate had brought in for show-and-tell on Earth. It gave him an idea. He spotted a one-foot section of bamboo that had broken from a larger stalk. “May I have that piece?”
“Sure.” Mom handed it to him and made a note on her chart.

The next day, Collin asked Mr. Shaw for permission to use the school’s tools after class. As his teacher watched, Collin drilled tiny holes into the piece of bamboo and inserted small nails into each hole. He poured a handful of Moon pebbles inside the tube and taped heavy-duty fabric scraps over the open ends.

Collin held the bamboo piece carefully as he hurried home. He hid it behind his back and slid open the front door. To his surprise, Mom and Dad were home early from work. Mom was carrying something that looked like a cake.
He looked closer and realized Mom had topped a flat brown oatcake with dried apples and cinnamon sugar. A real treat, on the Moon.
After the family had sung to Carina and Dad had cut the cake, Collin said, “And now I have a present for you.” He handed his sister the bamboo rod. “Happy birthday!”
“What is it?” Carina asked, looking at it curiously.
“A rain stick,” said Collin. “Whenever you turn it over, the Moon pebbles fall around the nails and it sounds like rain.”
Carina turned the stick, and the room was filled with a sound like rain drumming on a roof.
“It’s Moon rain!” she said. “It makes me feel like I’m home.” She smiled at Collin and turned the rain stick over again.

options.label: Quick-thinking can help you solve problems.
Everyone loves to celebrate their birthday.
Someday people will live on the moon.
Family can help you feel less homesick. E. The moon is not a fun place to live. F. It is important to hear the rain.

articles.title: Career Day at Pixie Academy
articles.author: Bradford H. Robie
articles.description: In this short fantasy story, a young pixie chooses a different career.
articles.intro: Pixies are playful magical creatures. They are usually small and human-like, with pointed ears and wings. In this story, a young pixie attends Career Day, a day when adults come in to speak to students about their jobs.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes Faylie different from the other pixies.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “You’re 12 years old,” Faylie’s mother said, fretting. “Most pixies your age know what they want to do by now.”
“I’m not sure Career Day will help,” Faylie said.
“It’s free, and you’re going,” Mom insisted, nudging Faylie into the classroom.
Faylie landed in a seat and folded her wings against the back of her chair with a sigh. She nodded to the other pixies, who all seemed more excited to be there than she was.

“Good morning!” said Mr. Impish, the school counselor, as he darted through the window, leaving behind a shimmering trail of pixie dust. “I hope you’ll all have fun hearing about different career opportunities.”
Faylie shifted uncomfortably in her seat. Pixies were expected to choose a profession before they turned 13, but Faylie didn’t have a clue what she wanted to do.

“First and foremost, we are pixies,” Mr. Impish said. “And what pixies do is play tricks on humans. Even the humans’ dictionary defines us as ‘mischievous elfish creatures.’ It’s just a matter of choosing your specialty.”
Faylie noticed several older pixies hovering eagerly outside the classroom.

“I see our guest speakers have arrived,” Mr. Impish said, beckoning the visitors inside. After introducing them, he explained they would give short presentations about their jobs. “Then you’ll get a chance to follow one of them through a workday and decide if his or her job interests you.”
Faylie did her best to listen to the presentations. Surely there would be a job she liked.
First was Mr. Gremmerlin, who specialized in hiding humans’ belongings, such as keys, phones, and homework. “Annoying people” was what he said he liked best about his job.
Next was Ms. Elfstein, whose job was to knock things over when people weren’t looking and to make irritating noises. “I turn faucets on just enough so they drip,” she said gleefully.

By far the most creative was Ms. Sprightly. “When people store their Christmas lights away and wonder why they don’t work the next season, it’s because of me,” she said proudly. “I burn out the light bulbs over the summer.”
Mr. Impish himself had a part-time job mixing up pairs of socks and putting things in the wrong places. He laughed as he described putting sugar in the saltshaker.
Faylie raised her hand. “I don’t think I’d like being annoying,” she said.
“But we’re supposed to be annoying,” Mr. Impish said. “Why don’t you give it a try? You can accompany Mr. Gremmerlin one day and Ms. Elfstein the next.”

Faylie really didn’t have a choice, so she agreed. But after she helped Mr. Gremmerlin hide a boy’s math homework, she felt guilty when she realized the boy had to do it all over again. And after she and Ms. Elfstein let a mouse into a house, Faylie felt sorry for the people who were kept awake all night listening to the mouse scurrying in the walls. So she shooed the mouse outside.
“I feel bad playing pranks on people,” Faylie explained to Mr. Impish afterward.
“But it’s all in good fun,” Mr. Impish said reassuringly.

That’s when Faylie had an idea. What if she did things that helped humans? What if she played un -tricks? To experiment, she moved keys and eyeglasses into plain sight if their owners happened to leave them where they might forget them. She tied people’s shoelaces so they wouldn’t trip. She made sure coffee lids were on tight.
“It isn’t normal,” Mr. Impish said when he heard about it. “You’re supposed to be mischievous.”
Faylie smiled. “According to the dictionary, mischievous means being ‘playfully’ irresponsible.’ I’m just being playfully responsible.”
“That’s changing the very definition of what we do!” Mr. Impish said.
“Will you come along with me and see?” Faylie pleaded.
Mr. Impish reluctantly agreed. But the next day, upon noticing the look of relief on a girl’s face when Faylie led home the girl’s lost dog, he understood.
“I still think it’s an odd occupation,” he remarked. “But I can see you’ve found your niche.”
Faylie had a long, successful career after that. In fact, many of the younger pixies began to imitate her. Soon there was a whole generation of pixies playing un-tricks on people.
But that was only the first half of her career. After Mr. Impish retired many years later, Faylie enjoyed a second job — as the new counselor at Pixie Academy.

options.label: Teachers and parents are very helpful when you need to decide something.
It is important to be true to yourself and do what makes you happy.
You should know what you want to be when you grow up.
Playing tricks on people is a fun way to spend time.

Mr. Impish changes his mind about Faylie's career choice.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The story lists some of the things that pixies like to do to annoy humans, like moving their keys or making the faucet drip. Have any of these things ever happened to you? What are some things that annoy you? Do you think pixies are to blame? Why or why not?

articles.title: The Dragon's Scales
articles.author: Pamela Love
articles.description: A girl working in a magical supply shop searches for a dragon's scales.
articles.intro: Pamela Love is a children's magazine and book author. In this story, a young girl must help a dragon look for his scales.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Althea feels about Torquil.
articles.permissions: "The Dragon's Scales" by Pamela Love, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “Three years I’ve been waiting, when Torquil promised he’d return them in three days. I’m not waiting three more days to get back what’s mine!” The dragon punctuated his remarks with a smoky snort and a lashing tail.

I sighed. This wasn’t Mr. Finnegan, the leprechaun scheduled to pick up a basket holding a dozen pounds of extra-lucky four-leaf clovers. (My boss, Torquil, had weighed the clovers before he left.) Should’ve looked before opening the door, Althea, I told myself.

Not that it mattered. The dragon’s huge, scaly wings looked plenty strong enough to knock down the door (and maybe the whole building) with one flap aimed in the wrong direction. Its claws and teeth were equally impressive. “May I help you?” I’d asked politely, the way I’d been taught. (And in this case, very politely.)
To be fair, the dragon had been every bit as courteous at first. “Years ago, Torquil the Wise borrowed my scales and forgot to return them. However, I require them back now.”
“I’m afraid he’ll be away for the next few days, but if you’d like to leave a message — ”
That’s when the dragon began complaining loudly about how long he’d been waiting already. No, he didn’t want to leave a message. He wanted to leave with his scales. Immediately.

Handing them over would be the sensible thing to do. So why didn’t I? Not because I was scared I’d lose my job. While it’s true I needed the work, being fired by Torquil had to be better than getting “fired” by a dragon.

My problem was finding the scales. See, my boss sells magical ingredients: troll tears, beans for giant beanstalks, demon dandruff, and such, to witches, wizards, and whoever else can pay his prices. No doubt the dragon scales were tucked away somewhere in Torquil’s enormous storehouse where I worked. Floor to ceiling, it’s filled with thousands of bottles, flasks, boxes, and other containers. Torquil knows what’s in every one. He just goes to the fourth shelf of the third rack, grabs the second jar, and there it is, magic-mirror polish or whatever. Unfortunately, as I’d already explained, my boss would be away for three days, and I had no way to reach him.

To make things worse, almost none of those bottles and boxes were labeled. Nobody can read Torquil’s handwriting anyway. Which is why I was here. The Magical Council got worried that if anything happened to Torquil, they wouldn’t be able to use any of his supplies. (He’s nearly three hundred years old, after all, and his Fountain of Youth water must run out sometime.) So they made him hire a clerk.
“Torquil,” said Serena the Sorceress, “Althea is just the person you need. She can label every item here. She can even reorganize the products in a more logical manner.”
“Don’t need her,” Torquil grumbled.
Serena’s eyes narrowed. “The Council thinks you do. We will even pay her salary, but you will hire her.”

That was that, as far as she was concerned. But Torquil? Ha! Oh, he gave me plenty of chores, so I was earning my pay (what little there was). He pointed to a closet. “All you’ll need is in there.” Paper and pens?
Try dishpan and mop.

I hadn’t spent years becoming a clerk to do housework. Torquil didn’t let me label anything. By the time the dragon showed up, I suspected that if Torquil had his way, I never would. Just my luck to have to deal with a dragon before I could quit.
Before I could explain, the dragon demanded, “Will you fetch my scales? Or shall I find them myself?”
That did it. Just the thought of the damage he’d do weaving his way around, over, or through the shelves made shiver, despite the heat from his snout.
“Just please give me some time to find what you want.”

The dragon grumbled, and I knew his idea of “some time” would be closer to minutes than hours, so I started scanning the shelves. After all, there was a tiny chance the scales were nearby. Then I thought I heard a clock ticking, even though Torquil uses a sundial. “Uh-oh,” I whispered. It had to be the dragon tapping his claws. Not a good sign.

What would I do if I didn’t find his scales? If the dragon did come in, he’d quite likely accidentally destroy what had taken Torquil centuries to collect. (Not to mention the danger of stuff swirling together in a way that might cause some magical disaster—at least to me. That was all I needed, to turn into a toad.)
“How can you lend your own scales anyway?” I wondered out loud. “Dragons are reptiles, so I suppose they shed their skins, scales and all. But why not just sell them?” It didn’t make sense.
Keep looking, I thought. Then I thought another word I won’t repeat.

Nervousness was keeping me from using my brain. That dragon’s scales weren’t all the same size. I needed to ask how big they were so I could narrow down my search to the right possible containers. Eyelid scales might be as small as my fingertips, but those from his back could serve as a shield —
A shield! Nothing was tougher than dragon’s scales, and Torquil’s work can be dangerous. (Ever try plucking a feather from a burning phoenix?) That had to be it.

“And I know where the shield is!” I said, breaking into a run. Torquil had gone herb gathering, not on some dangerous quest, so he’d likely left it behind. Sure enough, it was leaning against the side of his wardrobe, next to his armor. Dark green, smooth and glossy, it did look like dragon’s scales sealed together.
Relieved, I headed for the door with the shield. “Here, um, Your Dragon-ness.”
Instead of the thanks I’d expected, there was an ominous rumbling deep in the dragon’s throat. No time to run. All I could do was crouch behind the shield and hope for the best.
But the air filled with laughter, not flames. “I like a good joke. You should become a jester. Now, bring me my scales so I can be going...unless, of course, you’re trying to stall.”
Which wasn’t a bad idea. After all, a powerful leprechaun was on the way. If I was really lucky, he’d arrive in time to chase away the dragon.

Lucky...maybe I could improve my luck. Not caring what my boss thought, I dashed back to the work room. Reaching into the four-leaf clover basket Mr. Finnegan had ordered, I wondered how much I’d need. “A pinch? An ounce?” Suddenly, I spotted something out of the corner of my eye. Then it was my turn to laugh.
Once more, I returned to the door, this time carrying the device Torquil had used to weigh the four-leaf clovers. “Are these the scales you’re looking for?”

Letting out a smoky sigh, the dragon said, “Finally! My last clerk preferred to use his own scales to weigh my gems and gold. When he retired, he took them with him. That’s when I remembered Torquil never returned mine.”
Casually, I asked, “So...are you looking for a new clerk?”
“Yes, indeed.”
Turns out I was lucky after all. The dragon is a generous employer. Now I’m working happily ever after.

And I don’t feel guilty about leaving the labeling job undone. I told Mr. Finnegan (who’s on the Magical Council) I was quitting when he finally showed up. “Good luck getting Torquil to cooperate with the next clerk,” I added. “Not that you’ll need luck from me.”
The leprechaun grinned as I handed him the four — leaf clovers he’d bought. “To be sure I won’t.”

articles.title: Lewis and Clark: American Explorers
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: This text describes explorers Lewis and Clark's 1804 journey around the western United States.
articles.intro: The Lewis and Clark Expedition, also known as the Corps of Discovery Expedition, was the first effort by Americans to explore what is now the western United States. President Thomas Jefferson had just completed the Louisiana Purchase in 1803, in which the United States bought a great deal of land from the French. He wanted American travelers to map and characterize the newly acquired territory and establish an American presence there.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the difficulties that Lewis and Clark faced on their journey, and how they overcame them.
articles.permissions: "Lewis and Clark: American Explorers", © 2005, Barbara Radner. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: More than 200 years ago, in 1804, two explorers made an important journey. They were named Meriwether Lewis and William Clark. Today, people know a lot about the places they visited, but 200 years ago there were no maps of that part of the United States. They would travel by boat most of the way and they would make the first maps of that part of our country. They were going to trace where a great river went. The river they were mapping is a very big one called the Missouri River. They wanted to find out where it went. They hoped it would take them to the ocean.

They took many people with them to help with the exploration. There were more than 40 people on the trip. They also carried many supplies, including a lot of food. They hoped they would find food along the way, but this was long ago and they did not know what the territory would be like. The explorers had three boats to carry them and their supplies. It was summer when they started on this long trip.

They traveled slowly, each day traveling a short distance because they had to row their boats on the river. They would only travel a few miles every day. They traveled for months and were still far from their destination. In winter it was difficult to travel, so they camped along the river. There they would wait for spring when traveling would be easier. Snow and ice made it very hard to travel in winter.

Native Americans helped them along the way. They helped them get food, and they showed them where places were. The explorers had never been to this area before, so they were not sure where to find food or even where the river went. The Native Americans had lived there for many years, so they knew the area and how to survive there, even in the hard winter.

A Native American woman named Sacajawea helped them travel. She became their guide, and she traveled with them for months. It was hard work for everyone, including Sacajawea. The explorers needed her help to find their way to the West. They wanted to find out how to get to the ocean.

As they traveled, they made maps. Their maps showed the way the river went. It passed through grasslands, and then they were in mountains. When they got to the mountains, they had to leave their boats and walk.

It took more than a year for the explorers to get to the ocean. When they got there they had made maps that would help many people. But they had to bring the maps back. It had taken more than a year to make this first part of the trip. It also took a long time to get back. When the explorers came back, in 1806, they had been gone two years, and people said they were heroes. They would not make such a great journey again. They had done their job.

Their maps would help people settle in the new land. Long after their trip, people would build roads to the west. They would travel quickly by car. Today people can travel their route by plane. If you look out the window from the plane you will see those high mountains, you will see what a difficult journey it was.

articles.title: Why Do We Sleep?
articles.author: Trudee Romanek
articles.description: Trudee Romanek explains how sleep keeps the body healthy.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Trudee Romanek explains what scientists know about sleep.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens in your body while you sleep.
articles.permissions: "Why Do We Sleep" by Trudee Romanek, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Humans spend about one third of their lives asleep. By the time you’re 15, you will have slept for 5 years! But why do we do it?
Sleep has puzzled people for a long time. Why do we lie down and zone out for hours every night? Isn’t sleep just a giant waste of time? Could we learn to do without it?

Now scientists are beginning to answer some of those questions. They’ve discovered that brains and bodies don’t just shut down at night. Quite the contrary! At night, your body repairs itself and grows strong. Your brain is busy making memories and learning. Sleep is so useful that all animals do it. And without it, waking life would be impossible.
All Through the Night
What happens when you go to sleep each night? The sun goes down. You take a bath and read a book. Your eyelids start to droop. Time for bed!
After lying down, most people take about 20 minutes to fall asleep.

In the twilight zone between asleep and awake, your brain begins to drift. It muffles signals from the muscle and senses, ignoring information from the outside world. That can give you the odd sensation of falling. You aren’t really—you’re just falling asleep.

What makes you fall asleep? The first trigger is your body clock, an internal time-keeper. At around the same time every day, this clock releases a special “night” signal chemical. This tells the whole body to slow down and get ready for sleep.

That heavy feeling that you really need to close your eyes is triggered by a build-up of old nerve messenger chemicals. These are made as you think and move during the day. When the clutter gets too much, the brain senses that it’s time for a tidy-up. Time to sleep! While you’re asleep, your body will break down the old message stuff and clear it away, ready for a new day.

As you slip into sleep, your heart slows down and you breathe more slowly. Your eyes shut, and your brain ignores ordinary sounds. That’s why when you’re sound asleep, you don’t hear small noises or other things that might wake you up. The part of the brain that keeps you aware of time passing also switches off, so in the morning it feels like the night takes no time at all.
But your brain does not shut down at night. In fact, it gets quite active. It’s got important jobs to do.
Sleep, Dream, Repeat
You might think sleep is just...sleep. But in fact, you do several different kinds of sleeping, cycling through them several times through the night.

First is a light sleep. After about half an hour, your brain sinks into a deep sleep. During deep sleep, long, slow waves of electrical impulses move through the brain. These slow waves move all the important things you learned and did today from short-term memory into long-term memory. At the same time, you forget unimportant details like putting on your socks. That frees up space to learn new things tomorrow.

After about 20 minutes or so of slow-wave sleep, your sleeping brain shifts gears. It starts to look more awake—though it isn’t. In the next stage of sleep, your muscles go completely slack. Your body doesn’t move at all. But your eyes dart back and forth behind closed eyelids. This motion gives this kind of sleep its name — REM, for “rapid eye movement.”

During REM sleep, the brain is making connections. It links new experiences to old memories. It also bundles together actions you’ve been practicing, such as the motions of riding a bike, into a new, single muscle memory: ride bike. All this connecting triggers dreams—which can get pretty weird.

You cycle through stages of light, deep, and REM sleep every 90 minutes, over and over, all night long. At the beginning of the night, you do more slow-wave sleeping, while the brain cleans house. Toward morning, you get more REM sleep, as it builds new knowledge. That’s when the most vivid dreams happen.
Dream Yourself Smart
Why do we dream? Scientists aren’t completely sure, but they have some ideas.

Dreams might be a side-effect of memory making. While you sleep, your brain sorts through everything that happened during the day. It tries to link new experiences to old memories that are similar in some way. As it connects things, the “story-making” part of your brain turns them into a story, and you get a dream.
Dreams may also help regulate emotions. At night, the brain can replay upsetting events in a safe dream space, with less intense emotions. This can help make the memory less painful.

When it’s sorting through the day’s memories, your brain pays special attention to anything that made you feel extra happy or scared or excited. In dreams, the brain can try out solutions to problems that are bothering you. Finding even a weird dream solution can make you feel more in control the next day.

Dreams can even help you learn. In one test, volunteers learned how to play a new game. That night, some of them were awakened whenever they entered REM sleep. The others were awakened the same number of times, but only during non-REM sleep. The next day, the people who got their REM sleep did much better at the game. The players who started out being worst at the game had the most dreams about it — and they improved the most.
So if you want to do well on that test, don’t stay up all night studying. Instead, get a good night’s sleep. That’s when your brain learns best.
The Night Gym
Your brain gets a good workout at night — but your body is not idle, either. Sleep is your body’s time to repair and grow.

At night, your body releases chemicals that help create new bits of skin, muscle, and other parts of you. Sleep is also when your body makes the hormones that help you grow. So, even though you may not do much running while you’re asleep, your muscles are busy growing stronger.

Your immune system gets super busy at night, clearing away germs and making more germ-fighting antibodies. People who don’t sleep enough get sick more often and take longer to get well. That’s also why you often feel extra sleepy when you’re ill. Your body needs more sleep to fight germs and get well.
Sleep On It

Scientists still have many questions about sleep. They’d like to know why some people need more sleep than others, and how some animals get by with a lot less sleep. They’d like to find ways to help people sleep better.
But they know one thing for sure: getting enough sleep is the most important thing you can do to stay healthy and smart.
Think of sleep like a free magic potion that arrives every night. It makes you strong, helps you grow, and fights germs! It makes you smarter, happier, and more creative! To get some, just lie down.

articles.title: Cracks of Gold
articles.author: Elizabeth Donnelly
articles.description: A boy must find a way to fix a mistake.
articles.intro: Kintsugi is the Japanese art of repairing broken pottery by fixing the broken areas with a glue that is mixed with gold or silver. In this story, a boy must find a way to fix a mistake.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Benjamin’s feelings change throughout the story.

blocks.text: Everything about moving day is a puzzle. I don’t know where the boxes should go or anything about our new building on a new street. Our old life is broken into pieces and packed up in piled boxes. I wonder if it will ever fit back together again.

Mom introduces me to our new neighbors, the Satos. Mrs. Sato works with Mom at the job she just started. Mr. Sato helps me push our heavy recliner down the hall. He asks questions about the school I’ll be transferring to, but I don’t know any of the answers. Mom says it’s a fresh start, but it feels broken to me. I just want things to go back to how they were before.
After the first day at my new school, I walk home alone. Mom told me she had to work. I was still hoping she’d meet me after the bell.

I wiggle the key into our stiff lock, twisting and turning it until the door pops open. Leaving my backpack on a stack of boxes, I flop down on the couch and reach for the remote. As I flip back and forth between boring grownup shows, my stomach rumbles. I wander into the kitchen, ripping tape from the packed boxes while I fumble for a clean plate. My fingers slip, and something goes crashing to the ground. Smash! Porcelain flies from the folded newspaper and spins across the floor.

“Benjamin!” cries Mom from the doorway. I didn’t hear her come in. “What just broke?” she asks, kneeling to pick up the shards. In her hands, I recognize the shattered pieces. It’s Grandma’s bowl, the one Mom puts out for special occasions. “Why couldn’t you have waited for me to unpack? I can’t believe you broke this,” she says.

Words burst from my mouth and spill across the cramped room. “Well, I can’t believe we have to live here now! I can’t believe I have to switch schools when there are only a few weeks left before summer, and I can’t believe you didn’t even meet me after my first day!” I run into my room, slamming the door behind me.

The broom swishes across the kitchen floor. Then the click of Mom’s bedroom door closes behind her. I sneak out into the kitchen and sift through the fragments in the dustbin. There are so many pieces in here, but maybe I could put them back together. Turning the shards, I try to match the pieces, but this bowl will never look the same again.
“I’m sorry I yelled,” says Mom, as she opens her door. “Please just throw out the pieces. We can’t make a fresh start with broken things.”

As I wander down the hall, Mr. Sato is emptying his garbage down the chute. “Are you sure you want to throw that out?” he asks, gesturing toward the bowl. My throat twists into a knot. “It’s broken,” I whisper. “This is all my fault.”
“Things break, Benjamin. That isn’t anyone’s fault. Besides, this is a simple repair. Come. I’ll show you.”

In their apartment, Mrs. Sato makes me a sandwich while Mr. Sato spreads the broken pieces out like a jigsaw puzzle. “In Japan, where we come from, there is a beautiful way of mending things. Cracks are not something to hide. You’ll see.”
Taking a thin knife, Mr. Sato spreads lacquer across the sharp edges. He finds two parts that fit and begins to delicately rebuild the curve of the bowl. “Now you try,” he says.
I reach for a long, smooth section and place it at the rim. Slowly, gently, we twist and turn the pieces, finding each match. It’s tricky but also kind of fun.
When our puzzle is complete, the bowl is together again. “This needs to dry. Come back on Saturday, and we will finish it,” says Mr. Sato.

Saturday morning, I sneak out early and knock on the Satos’ door. “Now comes my favorite part,” says Mr. Sato, as he mixes a tray of lacquer and shimmering gold powder. “This is an ancient technique called kintsugi. In Japanese, the word means ‘golden joinery.’” He offers me a tiny brush. “Take your time, and fill in the crack lines.”

At first my hand is shaking, but then I paint with smooth strokes. When I’m finished, the bowl looks completely different. Instead of the cracks blending in and disappearing, now you can see exactly where it was broken.
“This bowl has another layer to its history — it has been broken, repaired, and is stronger because of it,” says Mr. Sato.
I’m a little nervous to show Mom the bowl, but that night I bring it into her room. She runs her fingertip over the golden veins. “Mr. Sato helped me fix it,” I say.
“It’s so beautiful,” she replies. “A new beginning.”
I sit on the edge of her bed, and Mom reaches out to me. Our hands join with crisscrossed fingers. Maybe it finally does feel like a fresh start.
“The bowl is stronger like this too,” I say. “Now the cracks are what’s holding it together.”
