articles.title: Forces and Motion
articles.author: India James
articles.description: India James teaches about how forces and motion impact our everyday lives.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James explains the science behind how things move.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the different ways people experience forces and motion.
articles.permissions: "Forces and Motion" by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Ouch! Why does it hurt so much when you stub your toe? Banging into a wall is painful. Your toe hurts because something heavy and solid made it change speed very quickly. The wall put a force on your toe. A very painful force!

Forces are around us every day. Every time something starts to move or stops moving, that change happens because a force has acted on it. But what are these forces? And how are they related to motion?
Forces and Motion

A force is anything that pushes or pulls on an object. You feel forces all the time. When you walk, your feet push on the ground to move you forward. When you hold a pencil, your fingers push against the pencil to keep it in place. Even when you’re lying down, you’re still feeling the force of gravity. Gravity is the force that pulls you down to Earth.

Sometimes, forces act when two things touch. When you stub your toe on a wall, the painful force happens through touch. Other forces can act over a distance. These forces don’t need to touch anything to be felt. Gravity is a force that doesn’t need to touch something to make it move. The force from a magnet is another type of force that can act over a distance.
Forces and motion are related through changes in speed. These changes in speed are called accelerations . Even something slowing down is called an acceleration.

Larry Giles is the Vice President for Engineering at Busch Gardens, an amusement park in Williamsburg, Virginia. “Accelerations can be any direction,” he explained. Accelerations can come up, down, left, right, forward, backward, or any combination of those directions.

Your stubbed toe hurts because it came to a sudden stop against the wall. When something changes speed, the force on the object changes, too. That acceleration is because the wall pushed on your toe. That push — or that force — hurts!
But forces aren’t always painful. Sometimes, forces can be a lot of fun.
Forces in Different Directions

People all over the world love spinning, swooping, and screaming their way through amusement parks. Many people think it’s worth standing in long lines to feel the different ways that rides push and pull on our bodies. Acceleration and forces that happen in unusual directions are what make amusement park rides so fun.

Our bodies are used to moving in certain ways. We don’t think of riding in a car as scary because we’re used to the accelerations — and the forces that go along with them — caused by those motions. But rides in amusement parks are designed to change speeds in directions that we don’t usually experience. “That’s where everyone says, ‘oh my gosh, this is so scary!’” said Larry.

Most roller coasters and amusement park rides go more slowly than a car on the highway. We think the rides are exciting because of the different ways they change speeds and apply forces to our bodies.
Whether they are fast or slow, constant or sudden, forces are all around us. They put our lives in motion!

options.label: Forces and motion remain a mystery to scientists.
Forces and motion are a new and useful discovery.
Forces and motion affect the human body in many ways.
Forces and motion help our body and our brain work together.

articles.title: Flying with Arctic Terns
articles.author: Rebecca Hirsch
articles.description: Rebecca Hirsch describes the life of the arctic tern.
articles.intro: Rebecca Hirsch is the author of over 70 children's nonfiction books. In this text, she describes small birds called arctic terns. Over the course of their lives, arctic terns migrate, or move, from the Arctic to Antarctic, which are the icy areas at the top and bottom of the planet.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why arctic terns migrate.
articles.permissions: "Flying with Arctic Terns" by Rebecca Hirsch, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The journey is about to begin. Hundreds of white seabirds fill the air. As the Arctic sun dips low over the ocean, they depart. Like many birds, they are migrating, moving south to escape the coming winter. But this is no ordinary migration. These tiny birds, Arctic terns, will fly across the world, all the way from the Arctic to Antarctica.

Arctic terns look like small seagulls. They have long, swept-back wings and tails, with black caps and bright red beaks. Every year they complete the longest migration of any animal on earth. Not bad for a bird that weighs no more than a lime!
The Nesting Grounds
For an Arctic tern, life begins as a speckled egg, laid in a nest on the bare ground. The mother and father take turns sitting on the egg. Nearby, other terns tend their own eggs.

It is June in the Arctic tundra, on an island off the coast of Greenland. The long winter has passed. The snow and ice are melting fast. The sun shines day and night. Soon a gray chick hatches. His parents bring him food: insects, fish, and small ocean plants and animals called plankton. He hides in the tall grass while his parents fish. Some chicks around him have nowhere to hide, for not much grass grows on the nesting grounds. Instead, the chicks lie very still on the bare ground to avoid predators.

One day, an Arctic fox sneaks into the colony. The adults work together to keep the chicks safe. One bird sends up an alarm, squawking loudly with a shrill call that sounds like kee-kee . Soon everyone is squawking. The birds band together and swoop down upon the fox. They dive and peck and scream at it. They even poop on it! The fox does not like all this attention and noise. Finally, he leaves. The chicks are safe once more.
As the weeks pass, the little chick grows bigger. He is no longer gray but white. Soon he is flying. He plucks fish and plankton from the surface of the ocean and snaps insects from the air.
The Long Journey

In September, cold winds start to blow. The Arctic summer is ending. Soon the snow and ice will return, and there will be little food to eat. The terns leave their Arctic nesting grounds, departing at sunset. Under the stars, they fly over the dark sea.

The terns swoop low over the waves and dive for fish. In some places, the ocean is filled with food. In others, there is little to eat, so they keep flying south. Their light bodies, mostly feathers, allow them to travel without much need for rest. On and on they fly, across the wide, wide Atlantic Ocean.

One day the terns encounter a flock of gulls. The larger gulls chase young terns and steal their food. Later a storm blows the terns off course. But when the weather is calm, the wind helps the colony travel fast and far.

In October and November the Arctic terns arrive in Antarctica, just in time for the southern summer. The sun shines day and night. Now the birds molt. Their worn feathers fall out. The young tern rests on a chunk of floating ice and waits for his new feathers to grow.

In May, most of the colony will return north for the Arctic summer. But the young tern will stay behind. He is not yet ready to return to the nesting grounds to have chicks of his own. Antarctic winters are brutal, so he will wander the southern seas to survive. In two years, he will fly north with the rest of his colony. He will soar over the waves, making his way to the other end of the world, to the very place he was born. There he will choose a mate and have chicks of his own under the Arctic sun.

options.label: Arctic terns should be studied more carefully.
Arctic terns are tough but not friendly birds.
Arctic terns should be protected by humans.
Arctic terns are small but amazing birds.

articles.title: City Systems
articles.author: Barbara Radner
articles.description: Barbara Radner discusses different government jobs.
articles.intro: No matter what city you live in, your community depends on the help of the systems in place. In this informational text, Barbara Radner further explores the systems that help a city run.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the different systems that are in place in a city.
articles.permissions: "City Systems" from Center for Urban Education at DePaul University by Barbara Radner. Copyright © 2007. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Thousands of people work in Chicago’s government. You probably see the mayor on television, and you see other government workers in your community. If you see a police officer or a crossing guard, they are government workers. They are safety workers, and they are part of a system that helps everyone in the city. Thousands of people work every day to keep you safe, make sure you have water and other services. "Untitled [Explore #18]" by Logan Campbell is There are other safety workers you don’t see, licensed under CC BY-SA 2.0. but they keep you safe. There are food inspectors. They go to restaurants, not to eat but to check for problems. They make sure the restaurant is clean so that customers won’t get sick. You may have seen a sign on a restaurant that it was closed by the health department. The inspectors have found serious problems. The restaurant will be inspected again before it is allowed to open. Sometimes a person gets sick because of food at a restaurant. That person usually takes a day to recover. Some people even have to go to the hospital after they get food poisoning. There are many workers who help run the city’s systems. One of the systems you don’t see is mostly underground. It is the sewer system. That system carries the dirty water from homes and businesses. The water goes to a sewage treatment site. There it is cleaned. Workers have to make sure the sewers are working. They need to check for leaks. They may need to replace a part of the system. If you have ever seen a hole in the road and workers digging deep underground you know they’re fixing a sewer. When a sewer breaks it is urgent to fix it. If they 1. a leader of a city that the people vote for 2. illness caused by bacteria or other toxins in food 3. Urgent(adjective)requiring immediate action or attention don’t fix it right away, it could become a health problem. There’s another system that involves water. It is the water system. People in Chicago have very clean water. The water you drink comes from the lake. About fifty years ago, it would come right from the lake and go into people’s homes. Pipes carried it to the homes. People might turn on the faucet and get water and a little fish. Yes, a fish could come into the home from the pipes. It would have traveled from the lake to the home. Pipes still carry water to the homes, but now it is filtered first. There is a very big filtration plant along the lakeshore. Water is pumped into the plant. Then it is cleaned. Then it is pumped to homes and businesses through the system of pipes under the city. There are hundreds of miles of pipes that carry the water.

So every day when you get water, cross a street, or go to a restaurant, you’re being helped by the government. The systems that the government runs help people stay safe and healthy. The thousands of people who work for the government all work for you.

options.label: The government doesn't provide as many services as you might think.
While there are many city systems, the most important are sewer and water.
City systems have changed over the years to focus mainly on keeping people safe.
There are many systems in place in a city that you might not realize are helping you.

articles.title: The Tides of Change
articles.author: Victoria Marie Lees
articles.description: A girl helps her sister accept change while battling the tides of the ocean.
articles.intro: This story is set in Hereford's Inlet in Cape May, New Jersey. In this area, the Atlantic ocean meets the sound, or smaller body of water, which is called a confluence. The military reserves is a special part of the military where service members keep up with their training in a long-term, part-time agreement to support their country if needed. In this story, two sisters find out their mother will be deployed in the army reserves.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Marie and Evelyn’s actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Tides of Change" by Victoria Marie Lees, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Marie trailed along the ripples of the cool Atlantic as she and her little sister walked the beach in Stone Harbor, New Jersey.
“Why does Mom have to go?” Evelyn asked for the umpteenth time.

It dawned on Marie why Dad had told her to take Evelyn for a walk at the point. It was a quiet stretch of sand beyond the short sea wall, without beachgoers and lifeguard whistles constantly blaring. This was the perfect place where Evelyn could talk to her away from their parents.
“Mom’s a nurse in the army reserves,” Marie said. “When the army needs more nurses, they call her up.”

That was the easy answer. It didn’t stop the nightmares from happening, especially now that Marie was getting older and understood more about what was going on in the world. Sometimes she hated current events. Her trouble sleeping started again after Dad had explained Mom’s mobilization at her last swim meet of the season, right before vacation.
“Evelyn was only three when Mom was last activated five years ago,” Dad had reminded Marie. “You’re older now and you understand. I need help convincing your little sister that Mom will come back.”

No, Marie didn’t understand. If only she could get Dad to see that being thirteen didn’t make her any less scared for Mom. How could she convince her sister when she needed convincing herself? Marie sighed and scanned the ocean. The water was still chilly, but she searched for dolphins to distract Evelyn. No fins in the water today.

The breeze blew in off the ocean, making the afternoon’s heat bearable. Marie was glad she and Evelyn wore their swim shirts. They wouldn’t have to keep putting sunscreen on. The shirts kept their shoulders from getting sunburned like last time.

They walked on in silence, just the gulls calling to each other, and the sandpipers rushing in and out of the water’s edge, plucking tiny clams out of the sand. Marie peeked over her shoulder to study Evelyn’s face beneath the sun hat as they kicked at the ripples on the beach. The bit lip, the eyes darting back and forth, troubled her.
“Let’s walk down to Hereford’s Inlet and watch the confluence where the ocean meets the inlet,” Marie said. “It’s high tide. The walk’s shorter.”
Evelyn only nodded.
“The inlet has warmer water. Remember?”
Her sister just shrugged.

Marie stopped walking, and Evelyn bumped into her. Marie turned around and took hold of Evelyn’s shoulders. “Mom will come back. She did last time.” Marie knew she desperately needed to focus on that herself.
“But where’s she going?” Evelyn’s lip quivered.
Marie understood. If they knew where Mom would be stationed, it wouldn’t seem as scary.

Last time Mom was called up, Marie was the eight-year-old. She was the one asking all these questions. Dad finally got tired of answering them. And then the nightmares started, even though Dad had changed the news channel on the television each time Marie walked into the room.

Marie clenched her fists now, remembering back. She didn’t want her sister going through that. The nightmares came because Marie didn’t know what was going on. She shook her head. Kids need their questions answered. She swallowed her fear to help her sister.

“I don’t know,” Marie told Evelyn. “But when I talked to Mom yesterday, she said she thinks it will be stateside instead of over where they’re fighting.” Marie clung to this belief and needed to help her sister do the same.
Once they reached Hereford’s Inlet, Marie pointed out where the waters met like a seam.
“See the waves smacking into each other?” she asked Evelyn.
Evelyn nodded.

“That’s where the ocean meets the inlet.” They stood on a sandbar at the tip of the beach just inside the water’s edge. Marie felt the tug of water rushing against her ankles from the inlet. “Feel the warmer water on your feet? That’s from the inlet. The tide’s going out now. Want to feel the difference in the temperature of the water?”
“OK.”
Marie took two steps to her left into the ocean side of the confluence, and the water chilled.
Evelyn giggled. “Can we go in?” she asked.
For the first time since they heard Mom was going active again, Marie saw a smile slip onto Evelyn’s face.
“Dad warned us about the strong current. Let’s get out of the swift water.” Marie backed onto the beach. She took Evelyn’s hand. “We can’t go deep. Let’s float by the water’s edge of the inlet.”
They tossed their hats onto the beach and stepped down the sandbar into the calm waters of the inlet. They went in as far as Evelyn’s knees, the sand soft and squishy between their toes.
“Now lie back, keeping your chest and butt up like I showed you when you were trying the backstroke,” Marie said. “I’ll keep holding your hand.”
Slowly, Evelyn spread her body on top of the water.

“You’re doing great!” Marie said. She lay back and allowed the water to support her. They turned away from the sun to watch the gulls soar overhead. Marie squeezed Evelyn’s hand. She turned her head slightly to see her sister floating calmly alongside her, a grin plastered to her face.

Water always relaxed Marie, whether she was swimming in it or floating on it. She was a water girl. She breathed in the salt air, filling her lungs. Mom would come back, she knew. If Mom’s stateside, she could call them a little more often. Marie squeezed Evelyn’s hand again. It was time to help her little sister understand this.

Suddenly, Marie’s bottom bumped into a sandbar. She stood up and pulled Evelyn to her feet. Looking around, she realized they had turned the corner of the point toward the ocean. The tide was indeed rushing out, pulling the warm water with it.
Evelyn raced back to their hats on the beach. She plopped hers on and came running back to give Marie hers.
“Thanks.” Marie smiled and pulled her wet ponytail through the back opening in the ball cap. Evelyn was smiling now, and Marie was determined to do the same.

Marie led the way back toward their blanket on the main beach. They trudged through knee-deep ocean, splashing each other and pointing to the dark cormorants overhead diving into the deep ocean for their dinners.
“I wonder what we’ll have for dinner,” Evelyn said.
“You’re always hungry.” Marie rolled her eyes.
Evelyn laughed.
“I want to swim first,” Marie said, “once we get back to the lifeguards. Now that you can float, let’s work on your backstroke.”
“OK!”

Marie could make out the lifeguard stands on the other side of the sea wall now, as they tripped along through the surf. With Evelyn older this time, Marie thought, maybe she wouldn’t feel so lonely with Mom gone.

Not paying attention to the pull of the ocean, Marie stumbled into a hole in the sand under the water, where the beach had been scoured out by the tide. She turned to warn Evelyn to be careful but couldn’t find her. Instantly, Marie realized the pull of the water was wrong. The waves parted around her. A rip current. Immediately, she turned her gaze out to sea. There in turbulent waters full of sand was Evelyn, floundering about fifty feet out. With no time to think, Marie dove into the water and swam with all her might to her sister.

Evelyn was coughing and crying. Treading water, Marie grabbed her arm and pulled her sister’s face up out of the water. “Float on your back!” she instructed as she flipped over and did the same. Marie kept hold of Evelyn’s arm as her mind raced. They were shoulder to shoulder, lined up with the beach. The current continued to pull them out to sea.
Evelyn started coughing again. Marie looked over. Her sister was sinking and flailing. “Relax. Keep your butt up. Kick a little to keep your legs from sinking.”
Evelyn evened out above the water line.

Marie had to remain calm. Drownings happened when people panicked. She tried to slow her breathing. Then she remembered the signs posted at the beach about rip currents. She had to swim out of the current’s grip by swimming to the side and paralleling the beach until she felt the strong outward pull of the water lessen. Only then could they swim toward the beach.
She glanced over at Evelyn. The fear etched in her little sister’s face probably mirrored her own.

“Hold onto my swim shirt here at my hips,” Marie instructed, “and stay on your back.” As long as Evelyn spread her body on the surface of the water, she should float. Marie took several deep breaths. All she had to do was parallel the beach.

Evelyn bumped against Marie’s left hip, clutching the shirt. Marie could hear her sister crying again. She glanced at the empty beach. Slowly, she began to backstroke, reaching back with her left arm, then her right, kicking just below the surface of the water. She kept her sights on the beach, heart thudding loudly in her ears.
People started gathering, watching her and Evelyn.

They barely moved, yet Marie continued stretching her arms back, kicking forcefully with her legs. Her muscles ached. She glanced left one time to be sure Evelyn was still floating beside her. She was.

The pull of the water outward finally began to lessen. Marie swam a little further to be sure they were out of the rip current. Then slowly she dipped her right side into the water to sidestroke toward the beach.
“Stay on your back and kick, Evelyn. We’re heading for shore!”
Marie’s arms tensed as she pushed the water aside to cut the distance to the beach. The waves helped her reach the shallows.
Mom and Dad were rushing toward the water’s edge with towels as Marie dragged Evelyn out of the sea.
“Rip current,” Marie swallowed as Mom draped the towel over her shoulders, “swept Evelyn out.”
“It’s OK,” Mom said, wrapping Marie in her arms.

Dad scooped up Evelyn in a towel and held her tight. “The lifeguards noticed the change in the water and alerted everyone on the beach. That’s when we looked toward the point to find you girls and saw you out in the ocean. We rushed over to help, but you were coming in.”
“Marie’s going to teach me to swim.” The color was coming back to Evelyn’s lips.
“Right! At home. In the pool,” Marie said, a smile tugging at her lips.
Maybe that’s why Mom serves. When others need help, someone has to step up and do it. She glanced over at her little sister.
Until Mom comes back, I’ll be here to take care of Evelyn.

options.label: It is important to help others when they need it.

You should always listen to your parents because they know you best.

articles.title: Seacoast Secret
articles.author: Nancy Dawson
articles.description: An imaginative girl sees something strange while traveling with her brother.
articles.intro: Southern sea otters are an endangered species, which is a type of animal that is in danger of no longer living on Earth. In the 1900s, they were hunted because their thick fur was used to make coats and hats. In this story, an imaginative girl discovers a secret while traveling with her brother.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Kate’s actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Seacoast Secret" by Nancy Dawson, Cricket, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: One June morning in 1935, my brother and I halted our horses at the edge of the sandstone cliffs and looked out at the Pacific Ocean. A wave slammed into the black rocks in the distance and shot a spray of white foam skyward. The wave continued coming in, shattering against the rocks beneath the cliffs. The churning foam scampered back out and was lost in the collision with the next wave crashing shoreward.
I raised my binoculars. “Tim, look! There’s something swimming out by the rocks. It just dove under. Now I can’t find it.”
“Probably a sea lion. Come on, Kate, let’s go. I want to get in some fishing before the sun sets.”

I didn’t want to leave the ocean, but Tim, at sixteen, was older by two years, and today he was the boss. I was feeling quite grown-up, as this was the first time our parents had agreed to let us ride over to the ocean from our cattle ranch and camp out overnight without them.

We turned inland and rode a mile north before stopping on the banks of Bixby Creek to set up camp. Tim went upstream to fish. My passions pulled me the other way — back to the ocean and its unending watery horizon.
As I rode Star Blazer along the creek toward the ocean, my mind filled with the creatures of the sea. In my imagination, stingrays, sharks, and octopuses battled with slimy sea monsters and mermaids.

My imagination. Dad thought it was a problem, past time for me to put my fantasies and sketch pad aside and focus on the real world. Momma wanted me to work harder on my studies and spend more time with her in the kitchen, baking bread and canning vegetables. Tim liked my fanciful drawings well enough, but he pestered me to make more of Star Blazer, the pigs, and the barn. But I like to draw what is not there, the unseen.

The whoosh and whir of the waves and the kur-kree! of the sea gulls broke into my thoughts. Bixby Creek cut a path through the sand and emptied into the ocean. I slowed Star Blazer to a walk and breathed in the salty air and bitter odor of seaweed marooned by high tide and now rotting under the sun.
I stared hard at the kelp beds in a bay near shore. Something long and brown floated there. Driftwood maybe or something alive. I raised my binoculars and scanned until I had the kelp bed in view.

Then I saw it: a brown furry creature floating on its back. It had a round head with chubby cheeks and tiny dark eyes and a large brown nose surrounded by white hair and whiskers. And hands! The creature had furry hands with fingerlike claws. I sucked in a deep breath. Had I really seen hands? Now the hands lay hidden under the water as the creature swam on its back, propelled by strokes from hind flippers. The creature rolled over and dove beneath the waves.

I panned my binoculars back across the kelp bed. There! Three more of the creatures back-floating, each wrapped in kelp, with only its head protruding. Why? Were they cold? Trapped by the huge seaweed? Dead, and this was their watery tomb? I imagined the wrapping rite, kelp spun round and round by tiny hands.

But as I watched, two of the creatures rolled over until they were free of the kelp. They swam and chased after each other, nipping and nudging, as if they were playing a game of tag. Finally they dove out of sight.
Star Blazer whinnied, and I noticed the sun was setting. Time to go.
When I got back to our campsite, I jumped down and tied Star Blazer’s reins to the twisted limb of a cypress tree.
“Tim! I saw the most amazing sea creatures! They were round and brown and furry and looked like bear cubs floating on their backs.”
“Yeah right! How about cleaning these fish while I build a fire?” He handed me his pocket knife, and I slit open a steel-head trout.
“They chased each other and dove under the waves and...”
“Probably sea lions,” he said.
“No, they weren’t sea lions!”
“Kate, bear cubs floating in the ocean? Sounds like your imagination played tricks on your eyes in the fading light.”
“No, these are real! I’ll show them to you tomorrow before we ride home.”

But the next morning, our campsite was wrapped in a layer of fog so thick that the horses looked like ghosts though they were only twenty feet away. No point in trying to go to the ocean. The damp fog made my hair frizzy and wild. I tied it up with a scarf and managed to get a fire going for breakfast. Then we packed up camp, saddled our horses, and rode slowly into the fog, inland toward our ranch.

Tim and I rode close together, singing songs and telling jokes to keep from getting separated. After an hour of riding, the fog thinned. Near the top of a hill, we broke through to sunshine. I turned in my saddle and looked back west. Fog still blanketed the hills and ravines. We rode on east, into the warmth.
We arrived home in time for Tim to milk the cow and me to set the table for supper. While we ate, I told my parents about the sea creatures.
“Probably sea lions,” said Dad, siding with Tim.
“No! They were rounder and furrier and had the cutest little hands,” I said.
“You can look for them in the encyclopedia,” said Momma. That’s Momma, always making something into a school lesson, even in the summer.
“Or you can look for them in your fairy tale books,” said Tim with a smirk. I shot him a dark look and got even by cutting him a particularly small piece of strawberry-rhubarb pie.

After washing the dishes, I opened the glass doors of the bookcase and pulled out a few volumes of Compton’s Pictured Encyclopedia . There wasn’t a listing for “marine life,” so I looked up “ocean” and found pictures of snails and corals and seaweeds and drawings of eels and jellyfish.

I looked up “sea” and found sea anemones, with a lovely illustration of the straggly, flowerlike animals. I got out my sketch pad and began to draw them, attached to rocks and waving their long tentacles in the ocean currents. Then it was bedtime.
The next morning as Momma and I kneaded bread dough and shaped it into loaves, I told her about my futile search through the encyclopedia.
“Well,” she said, “you could write a letter to the biologists up at Stanford University. Surely they would know.”
While the loaves rose, I wrote the letter and drew a picture of what I’d seen at the bottom.

My summer days after that were filled with weeding the garden, baking bread, washing mounds of dishes, and, when I had time, riding Star Blazer around the ranch or drawing in my sketch pad. I was not allowed to ride the fifteen miles over to the ocean by myself, and Tim and my parents were too busy to go with me.
A month passed before Dad drove into Carmel to buy supplies and pick up our mail. He had a letter for me when he returned.
Dear Miss Kate,
Thank you for your letter, which was forwarded to me. I am going to be in your area doing some research and will stop by your ranch on 20 August.
Until then, it is of the utmost importance that you and your family do not talk to anyone about what you saw. I will tell you all about it when I come to visit.
Sincerely,
Dr. Philip Backus
Hopkins Marine Station of Stanford University
Pacific Grove, California
“I’m going to be famous! It’s a matter of utmost importance!” I danced around the kitchen and read the letter out loud to my family.
The wait for Dr. Backus was agonizing. Momma said the time would go faster if I kept busy, her excuse for giving me more chores.

Dr. Backus finally drove up just before supper on the twentieth. After brief introductions, he turned to me. “Your drawing is a good likeness,” he said. “Look at these pictures.” He handed me a thick book opened to a double spread of illustrations.
“Yes! That’s it!” I shouted. “It says they’re California sea otters!”
“Sea otters? But they’re extinct,” said Momma.

“Not quite. They were heavily hunted in the nineteenth century, but a few survived,” Dr. Backus replied. “I’ve been observing the raft of otters over by Bixby Creek for the last two days. I counted twenty-four adults but no pups.”
“Why doesn’t anybody around here know about them?” asked Tim.

“The few scientists who know are keeping quiet, going along with the extinction story. Sea otters have been protected by law since 1911, but we’re afraid that poachers would hunt them if the word gets out,” Dr. Backus said. “That’s why it’s so important that you tell no one.”
“Why do they wrap up in kelp?” I asked.
“So they don’t drift away while they’re sleeping. The kelp fronds are attached to the ocean floor,” explained Dr. Backus.

Before leaving the next morning, Dr. Backus asked us to send him information about any otter sightings: the number, date, location, and their behavior. He had each one of us solemnly swear that we would not reveal the sea otter secret.

The next Saturday, Dad left the hired hands in charge of the ranch. My family saddled horses and rode over to the ocean to look for sea otters. We took turns with the binoculars and rode in pairs up and down the beach, our eyes always scanning the ocean. We saw a few sea lions sunbathing on the rocks, sea gulls skimming just above the waves, black cormorants flying low and diving for fish, but no sea otters.
On Sunday morning, we made one more ride down the beach.
“There!” shouted Tim, pointing at a sea otter swimming on its back. I looked through the binoculars.
“A baby! There’s a baby riding on the otter’s stomach!” I passed the binoculars to Momma.
“Mother and child,” she whispered.
We watched until they swam out of sight.
“Time to go,” said Dad reluctantly. “Thank you, Kate, for bringing us the sea otter secret.”
“Hey, Kate, you should draw a new picture and send it to Dr. Backus,” said Tim. “He’ll want to know about the baby.”
“Right,” said Dad. “Good thing we have an artist in the family!”

articles.title: Hazel Down the Rabbit Hole
articles.author: Rebecca Agiewich
articles.description: A girl hopes to be the lead in the school play.
articles.intro: Rebecca Agiewich is an American author. "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland" is a story written by Lewis Carroll in 1865. It is about a young girl named Alice who falls through a rabbit hole into a fantasy world. The book has turned into a widely popular and well known movie. In this story, the new girl in school hopes to be the lead in the school play, "Alice in Wonderland."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Hazel’s feelings change throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Hazel Down the Rabbit Hole" by Rebecca Agiewich, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Alice in Wonderland is my favorite story of all time. And acting is my number one passion.

So when my sixth-grade teacher announced during my first week at Newland Elementary that we’d be performing Alice in Wonderland for the school play, I practically squealed out loud with excitement. I’d always wanted to play the role of Alice!
Luckily, I stopped the squeal before it really escaped. The girl with purple glasses sitting next to me gave me a funny look. So I put my hand on my stomach and pretended to have a stomachache.

At my old school, Poulsbo Elementary, I’d starred in every play from third grade on. Before that, gymnastics was my thing. But then I played my first role as Tinkerbell in Peter Pan . And after that, backflips just couldn’t compare.

All my friends were in the plays, too, and we’d had so much fun together! Because I missed them so much, I’d been crying a lot, just like Alice does when she first goes down the rabbit hole. Down there, she eats a little cake marked “Eat me,” grows into a giant, and cries so much that a huge pool of salty tears forms around her. Then she shrinks down to a tiny size and almost drowns in her own tears.

Nothing that weird had actually happened to me, of course. But I could relate to Alice more than ever now that I was going to a new school. And I just knew that if I could star as her, life at Newland Elementary would be a lot more fun.
A week later, Mrs. Pearson announced, “The role of Alice goes to Denise Taylor!” My head buzzed. My stomach churned. But I clapped along with everyone else and pasted a fake smile on my face.
It took Mrs. Pearson forever to get to my name. When she finally did, she read, “Hazel Woods, Tyler Ames, and Ella Cottrell — hedgehogs!”

She announced this as if we’d just won a million dollars. But the hedgehogs had no lines. They just got batted around with croquet mallets and then disappeared forever. Mrs. Pearson called the hedgehogs up after class. I realized that Ella was the girl with the purple glasses who sometimes sat next to me. She had purple sneakers on today, too. Tyler had long hair and really baggy jeans.
“Hedgehogs,” she said, “your roles are going to be a lot of fun! But you might also consider helping out with costumes. We’ve never done this play before and might need some new ones.”
Mrs. Pearson looked at us expectantly. I snuck a glance at the other two. Ella looked as if she were about to cry. Tyler looked as if he’d just eaten a piece of moldy cheese.
I had to admit, the fact that they were miserable, too, made me feel just a tiny bit better.
“Well, think about it!” said Mrs. Pearson. “See you tomorrow at rehearsal!”
The next day we had our first rehearsal in the gym.
Only the hedgehogs didn’t actually get to rehearse. After everyone had assembled on the basketball court, Mrs. Pearson called the hedgehogs aside again.

“Hedgehogs,” she said, “I have a special job for you. Could you please get the custodian to let you into the storage room where we keep the costumes, and find as many costumes as you think will work? You might even need to go out shopping to Goodwill at some point, or get creative!”
The three of us glanced at each other. Had we actually agreed to help with costumes? I didn’t think we had.

“Great, thank you, hedgehogs!” said Mrs. Pearson. As she walked toward the basketball court, loud laughter filled the air. Alice and the Cheshire Cat were clowning around while the other actors cracked up.
My stomach started to ache. My legs got heavy. I wanted to lie down all of a sudden.

“Come on, hedgies, no time to waste!” said Ella. I looked toward the gym door, where the other two were waiting. “We have lots of important, creative things to do,” said Ella, rolling her eyes behind her purple glasses.
I giggled, which was weird because I was also about to cry. But I followed them out of the gym.
“Gross!” said Tyler, pulling a purple velvet robe out of the storage trunk. It was crawling with moths. He threw it to the ground.
Ella had one hand against the wall and appeared to be doing ballet warmups. “I really wanted to be the Queen of Hearts,” she said, pointing her left foot as she swept her left hand into the air.

“Yeah, well I wanted to be the Cheshire Cat,” said Tyler, kicking the moth-eaten robe out of the way. He twirled around after he did it with his hands clasped together above his head. Then he peered back into the trunk.

“I really wanted to be Alice,” I said, slumping against the wall. This stinky storage room with no windows wasn’t helping my stomachache. Maybe I should just go back to gymnastics, I thought. Forget about stupid acting.

Ella swept her left arm up into the air again. “Since you just moved here, you probably didn’t know that Denise Taylor has gotten all the starring roles since kindergarten. The rest of us just have to take what’s left.”
“Oh,” I said, startled. Ella rose onto both toes in her purple sneakers.
In Poulsbo, I’d been the one who’d gotten every starring role since kindergarten. I’d never even wondered if maybe the other kids had felt bad about that.

“What does a hedgehog even LOOK like? Is it like a porcupine?” said Tyler, as he pulled a fake gold crown out of the costume trunk. He put it on and stuck out his tongue. Then he did this cool backward turn, where he ended up bowing in front of us as he took the crown off and held it to his chest. It was like something from Fred Astaire movies my grandmother liked to watch.

“That will work nicely for the Queen of Hearts,” said Ella glumly not even noticing Tyler’s graceful dance move. She raised her left leg so that it was almost parallel to her body. I remembered hearing that she danced the Nutcracker every year. Both of these guys seemed like really good dancers.
Suddenly I had an idea.
“Guys, I know what we should do,” I said.
They looked at me.
“We should —”
I stopped. I needed to show them what I had in mind.

“Why don’t you come over to my house this weekend, and I can tell you about it? I know something that will make this play way more fun.” The invitation escaped my mouth before I had time to think about it.
There was a pause that lasted about two days.
Then Ella said, “OK, I’m in.”
“Me, too,” said Tyler.
My heart was pounding like crazy all of a sudden. But at least I didn’t have a stomachache anymore.
“Are you guys ready?” I whispered as we stood backstage at the first dress rehearsal. Ella and Tyler nodded. Then we did our special hedgehog handshake.
The three of us had on awesome hedgehog costumes my uncle had designed. We’d taken three trips to Goodwill to find other costumes for the show. Mrs. Pearson had called our costume work “fantabulous.”
But that wasn’t the best part about the hedgehogs.
Then came our cue. “Let the games begin!” said the Queen of Hearts.
One… two… three,” I counted in. Then we ran onstage in a tight single file, steps synchronized and arms pumping in unison.
We stopped in front of the Queen of Hearts and curtsied.
She was so surprised she couldn’t say her next line.
“What was that?” said the Queen.
“It’s the Hedgehog Shuffle!” I said, smiling and trembling at the same time.
We all looked at Mrs. Pearson, who was sitting in the first row. Her mouth formed a big “O.”
Oh no, I thought. She’s going to get mad at us.
Then suddenly she clapped. “That was fantastic, hedgehogs!” she said. “Where did you learn to choreograph like that?”

The three of us smiled and shrugged. My older sister Trina had agreed to help us come up with the dance after I said I would donate two weeks’ allowance to her AND scoop our dog’s poop from the yard the next time it was her turn.
“Well, Queen, what are you waiting for?” said Mrs. Pearson. “Your next line, please!”
I glanced at Tyler and Ella. They were trying to hide smiles of anticipation, just like I was.

“Hand me my mallet!” said the Queen, sounding unsure of herself. Then she aimed at Hedgehog #1 — Ella. When the mallet touched her, Ella pirouetted and jumped across the stage using moves from the Nutcracker .
“Ooh,” said everyone.
The mallet came down on Hedgehog #2, Tyler. Tyler glided across the stage doing a move he called the Sideways Moonwalk.
“Aah,” said the cast, even louder than before.
The Queen looked excited as she prepared to hit Hedgehog #3 — me. When the mallet tapped me, I did a series of three somersaults, a cartwheel, and finally a backflip.
Everyone erupted into applause.

Ella and Tyler surprised me with a T-shirt after our first performance. On the front it said “Alice in Wonderland 2020, Newland Elementary.” On the back it said “Go, Hedgehogs, Go!” and showed three hedgehogs dancing in sync like cheerleaders. Ella and Tyler each had one, too.
One of the parents saw me wearing the T-shirt after the show and said, “Oh, are you selling those T-shirts? Those are adorable!” The three of us looked at each other.
“They’ll be for sale starting tomorrow,” said Ella.
The T-shirts that Ella and Tyler designed helped raise $300 for the spring play, which Mrs. Pearson promised would be used to mothproof the costumes, among other things.

We don’t know what the spring play will be yet. Probably Denise Taylor will get the starring role in that, too. But that’s OK, because now Tyler and Ella and I all kind of want small parts so we can surprise everyone by making them totally cool. And, of course, we’ll all do costumes together again.
I still miss my friends in Poulsbo. But the good thing is that Seattle feels a lot less like the rabbit hole now, and a lot more like home.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Hazel is disappointed that she does not get to play Alice, but eventually is excited by her smaller role. Can you think of a time when you did not get something you wanted? Were you able to find a way to make the best of it?

articles.title: The Child
articles.author: J. Patrick Lewis
articles.description: A speaker describes the experiences of Civil Rights activist Sylvia Mendez, as a child.
articles.intro: J. Patrick Lewis is an American poet and prose writer known for his children's poetry. In this poem, the speaker describes the experiences of Sylvia Mendez, an important Civil Rights activist of Mexican–Puerto Rican heritage. Mendez played an important role in the desegregation of schools in California, meaning that white and Mexican American students were no longer forced to go to separate schools based on their race.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Sylvia’s perception of the different schools.
articles.permissions: "The Child" from When Thunder Comes by J. Patrick Lewis. Copyright © 2013 by J. Patrick Lewis. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Sylvia pushed into the wind,
Septembering the trees,
and hurdled over a railroad track
to a two-room shack
that never read “Browns Only.”
It did not have to.
Under the billion-acre sky,
she wondered, Did white girls
at 17th Street Elementary really
wear rainbow necklaces?
Aunt Sally took her there once.
Eyes sharp as icepicks pierced
the windowpanes as if seeing
a Mexican for the first time.
Every door was locked with a
secret combination of frowns.
How can anyone ever get in?
Sylvia asked. Someone must know
who has the right key…
She looked up at her mother.
Maybe me?

options.label: Children can often be crueler than adults.
One person has the ability to cause widespread change.
Some people will always have access to better education than others.
People are often excluded from organizations based on the color of their skin.

discussionQuestions.prompt: According to the poem, Sylvia Mendez was not welcomed at 17th Street Elementary School. How did prejudice contribute to her exclusion from the school? How were students disadvantaged by being placed in separate schools because of their race? Does this type of prejudice occur in schools today? Cite examples from the text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

According to the poem, Sylvia Mendez is the one with the ability to create change. What challenges do you think she encountered as she helped to desegregate schools in California? What characteristics do you think Mendez needed to possess in order to overcome these obstacles? Have you ever been in a situation that required you to overcome an obstacle? What personal characteristics did you use to get through the situation?

articles.title: The Impossibles
articles.author: J. Patrick Lewis
articles.description: A speaker describes how it is possible to do impossible things.
articles.intro: J. Patrick Lewis is an American poet and prose writer known for his children's poetry. In this poem, a speaker describes doing impossible things.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what type of things the speaker says you cannot do.
articles.permissions: "The Impossibles" from If You Were a Chocolate Mustache by J. Patrick Lewis. Copyright © 2012 by J. Patrick Lewis. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: You cannot seed a garden
With wheelbarrows of dreams.
Unless you first plant wishes, how
Cucumbersome it seems.
You cannot climb a rainbow
Unless the winds agree
To blow in one direction — up —
Toward Curiosity.
You cannot live in castles
Suspended in the air,
But your imagination takes
Long vacations there.
You cannot reach Forever —
Forever’s much too far —
But fortunately Now and Then
Were built right where you are.
You cannot catch Tomorrow
Until you catch your sleep.
Can you recall how many Yes-
terdays you wish to keep?
You cannot snatch Sir Shadow —
He’s such a clever chap!
Until — at night — he snatches you
And dozes in your lap.

options.label: People often dream of accomplishing what is impossible.
Nothing is impossible if you truly plan to accomplish it.
While dreams are fun, they are often not realistic.
It's better to be safe and secure than to pursue your dreams.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the poem?

options.label: "You cannot seed a garden / With wheelbarrows of dreams. / Unless you first plant wishes" (Lines 1-3)
"But fortunately Now and Then / Were built right where you are." (Lines 15-16)
"Can you recall how many Yes- / terdays you wish to keep?" (Lines 19-20)
"You cannot snatch Sir Shadow — / He's such a clever chap! / Until — at night — he snatches you" (Lines 21-23)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the poem supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: sleep
desire
nightmares
fear

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes "Sir Shadow" (Lines 21-24)?
What does the speaker mean when they say that your imagination can take "long vacations" in castles in the air (Lines 9-12)?

discussionQuestions.prompt: The speaker in the poem "The Impossibles" believes your imagination can make anything possible. What are some of the things that you dream of or imagine? How do you think you can use your imagination to make these things possible?

In the poem, the speaker describes accomplishing seemingly impossible things. What steps does the speaker suggest readers take to accomplish impossible things? What are the steps that friends or members of your family have taken to achieve things you thought were impossible?

articles.title: Missing Mom on Mother's Day
articles.author: Lissa Rovetch
articles.description: Advice columnist "Ask Arizona" helps a reader navigate their feelings around Mother's Day.
articles.intro: This text is part of an advice column for kids called "Ask Arizona" in Highlights . An advice column is part of a newspaper or magazine where people going through a hard time can write a letter to someone asking for help. In the answer, the author gives them ideas on how to solve their problem. In this letter, "Confused in Concord," writes to Arizona looking for help on how to handle their feelings about spending Mother's Day without their mother.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, pay attention to the story Arizona shares about what happens during Writing Club.
articles.permissions: "Missing Mom on Mother's Day" by Lissa Rovetch. © Highlights for Children, Inc., Columbus, Ohio. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: ASK ARIZONA
Mother’s Day is always a confusing time for me because my mom is not around. Any thoughts?
-Confused in Concord
Dear Confused,

I had no idea how confusing Mother’s Day could be until something happened during Writing Club at the library. Our first project was to write mysteries. The next week, we made comic books. And the third week, our librarian, Mr. Sendak, piled stacks of cookbooks on every table.
“Yes!” my friend Ollie said, grabbing a book of chocolate recipes. “This is my kind of cookbook!”

“Today we’re going to write down food memories,” said Mr. Sendak. “Let’s see if the pictures in these cookbooks remind you of anything you especially loved to eat or drink growing up. What did it smell like? Can you remember a time when it made you feel good to cook it, eat it, or share it?”
I started turning the pages of a breakfast cookbook and stopped at a recipe for Belgian waffles.
“Oooh!” I said. “This makes me think of the time my dad made fancy blueberry waffles for my fifth birthday and my parents let me pour my own syrup.”
“Syrup is the best part!” said James, a new friend Ollie and I had met at Writing Club.
“That’s exactly what Cow thought!” I giggled.
“You have a cow?” James asked.
“Arizona’s cat is named Cow,” Ollie explained.
“Yep,” I said. “Cow walked right through the puddle of syrup that I spilled, and our house was covered in sticky little paw prints for days. I’m going to write about that!”

“Chocolate cake, chocolate brownies,” Ollie said as he drooled over the pictures. “Oh! Mexican hot chocolate with marshmallows. That’s it! I remember the first time I ever played in the snow. We went to this cabin in the mountains, and I was sledding down a hill, and I went flying!”
“That sounds awesome,” said James.

“Yeah, the flying part was pretty cool,” said Ollie, “but the landing part really wasn’t! I was so sad and sore until my abuela made me some special cinnamon hot cocoa. Now, whenever things get tough for either of us, we make that hot chocolate together. I’m going to write about that.”
James just kept flipping through the books.
“What’s your favorite food memory?” I asked.
James shrugged.
“Come on,” said Ollie. “We want to hear about it!”

James sighed, put down his cookbook, and said, “Saturday night was always breakfast-for-dinner night. My mom used to make the best french toast with apple butter, and sizzling turkey bacon on the side.”
“Yum!” I said. “How did you start that tradition?”
But James didn’t answer. He just picked up his pen and started to write.
Half an hour was up before we knew it, and Mr. Sendak asked for volunteers to share their writing.

When it was time to pack up our things, Ollie said, “Hey, I just had a genius idea. If we put our waffles, french toast, bacon, and hot-cocoa recipes together, we could make an epic Mother’s Day brunch for our families.”
“Yes!” I shouted way too loudly for being at a library. “I bet my parents will let us have it in the backyard.”
James slung his backpack over his shoulder. “Sorry. I’m booked up that day.”
Ollie laughed. “Do you know the future? We didn’t even choose a day yet.”
James took a deep breath. “Actually,” he said, “my mom doesn’t live with my dad and me right now.”
“Oh, your parents are divorced?” said Ollie.
“It’s not that,” said James. “My mom has to be in prison for a while, and I hardly ever get to see her.”
“I’m sorry,” I said. “That sounds hard.”
“Really hard.” Ollie nodded. “My mom isn’t around either, and sometimes I miss her so much.”
“Do you live with your dad too?” James asked.
“No,” said Ollie. “I live with my grandmother.”
“Abuela is the best!” I said.
“And she definitely loves parties,” said Ollie. “How about I bring my grandmother to our brunch, and you bring your dad?”
“I guess that could be all right,” said James. “But I should probably see what my dad says first.”
Luckily, James’s dad said yes. Also luckily, my parents agreed to have the brunch at our house. Unluckily, they said I’d have to sweep the patio and help with a humongous garden cleanup first!
It was a lot of work, but on the day of the party, the backyard looked fantastic. The food turned out great, and everyone had a deliciously good time.
Oh, also, James sent his mom a beautiful card that he made, photos from our brunch, and his breakfast-for-dinner memories. He said his mom loved them.
So, dear Confused, I realize that not having your mom around has to be extremely tough. The biggest thing I want to say is that there’s no right or wrong way for you to feel.

Ollie said that it almost always helps him to talk about his feelings with grown-ups and friends he trusts. And James told us that even though his mom wasn’t there at the party, it felt good to think about her on Mother’s Day.
I also want to thank you so much for your letter. It reminds me that it’s important to be as kind as possible, because you never really know what other people are going through.
Ciao for now,
Arizona

articles.title: Great Splashes
articles.author: Joan Bone
articles.description: A young girl and her family celebrate the Thai New Year's festival of Songkran.
articles.intro: In Thailand, New Years is celebrated with a water festival called Songkran. In this story, a young girl and her family celebrate the holiday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people celebrate Songkran.
articles.permissions: "Great Splashes" by Joan Bone, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: In a small wooden house nestled under coconut trees, Fa turned over on her sleeping mat. The sound of jingling silver cups had awakened her.

Fa knew the sound well. She could picture Mother reaching up to the high shelf in the kitchen and lifting down the large silver bowl with the small cups inside. The bowl, a yaikhan , and the cups, the leks , were decorated with curly designs and used only for the special ceremonies of the Songkran water festival.

Maybe, just maybe, today Father would let her be first to pour the scented water from the lek into Grandmother’s hands. “Am I old enough this year?” The words seemed to chant inside her with each beat of her heart. But she knew that maybe she was still too young.
Fa squealed with excitement as she hopped off her woven mat. The humid morning air was already making her skin sticky.
“Time to go!” called Mother. Fa ran outside. Her father and mother were already on the motor scooter. Fa climbed on behind her mother, hugging her waist tightly.

Her father zigzagged quickly along the busy village street. The motorcycles, taxi tuk-tuks , and the trucks flowing out to the main road were like a river of colors. The sweet smell of jasmine blossoms in Mother’s cloth bag teased Fa’s nose as they headed to Grandmother’s house a few miles away.

Fa ducked her face into her mother’s hair as some older boys ran out toward them, carrying pans of water. Splash! The cool water on her back made her shudder, but she laughed and so did Mother and Father.

Laughing, shouting boys and girls in the back of a pickup truck pulled up close beside another truck carrying their friends. Both groups of kids flung water back and forth with pans and cups. This was the most fun part of Songkran — everyone, everywhere throwing water to celebrate the New Year.

As they grew close, Fa smelled Grandmother’s special sticky rice in the air, which made her stomach rumble. In all the excitement, Fa could still feel the words beating within her heart: “Maybe, maybe this is the year.”

Grandmother sat on the bench by her door in a colorful skirt. She smiled, showing her crooked teeth. Father and Mother and Fa got off the scooter. They were all dripping wet, but it did not matter. It was Songkran.

Putting their palms together in front of their faces, bending their knees in a little bow, they honored Grandmother with the traditional greeting. Bringing her tiny hands together, her dark eyes sparkling, Grandmother returned the greeting.
Father poured water from a bottle into the yaikhan. The sweet fragrance of jasmine filled the air as Mother sprinkled white blossoms into the water.
Grandmother held out her palms. Father turned slowly toward Fa. “You may go first, Fa,” he whispered, his eyes dancing. He handed her the first lek.

Carefully, trembling just a bit, Fa dipped the lek into the yaikhan and poured the jasmine water into Grandmother’s cupped hands. As she did, Father said to his mother, “We honor you, dear one. Khab Khun Krap , thank you, for taking care of us and loving us.”

Father dipped his fingers in the water and sprinkled it on Grandmother’s cheeks. He lifted her white hair from her face. Gently, his fingers smoothed some water over her forehead. “ Khab Khun Krap, Ma .” Father, Mother, and Fa then knelt on the ground, palms pressed together in front of their faces. Grandmother gently patted each of their heads in blessing.
“I’ve made your favorite sticky rice,” she said, rising from the bench. “Come, I have peeled the early mangoes, and there is coconut milk, too.”

Fa took a little rice from the basket steaming over the earthen charcoal burner. She tucked a piece of mango into her rice, rolled the mixture into a ball, and then dipped it into the cool coconut milk Grandmother offered.
“Grandmother, it’s so warm and soft and chewy, but cool and sweet at the same time. It tastes like all the best parts of summer.” Her sigh was a happy one.
Grandmother chuckled. The soft sound reminded Fa of wind blowing through bamboo stalks on the first day of the New Year.

articles.title: Dancing for Mamá
articles.author: Joanna Lukens
articles.description: Margarita makes her mother proud when she dances in the Festival de Las Flores.
articles.intro: The Festival of Flowers is an important celebration in El Salvador. In this story, a young girl is selected, or chosen, to dance in the festival.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Margarita feels throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Dancing for Mamá" by Joanne Lukens, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Margarita sat on the porch rocker. Next to her lay a basket filled with cloth and trims. She was finishing the costume she would wear on Saturday for the Festival de Las Flores . She had been selected to be one of the cargadoras , dancers who carry palm branches covered with colorful flowers.

The festival was always a special day, but this year it was even more so because the president of the United States, who was visiting her country, would be attending. Margarita knew she should be excited about the festival, but deep down she was sad. Her mamá had been so pleased when Margarita had been chosen as one of the dancers. As a girl, Mamá had also danced with a palm at the festival, and she had taught Margarita the steps herself.

They had begun to sew the costume Margarita would wear at the festival together, but before it was finished, Margarita’s grandfather had fallen ill. Her mamá had to leave for a job in the United States to help pay for his medicine and to make money for the family.
“Think of me, and I will be with you, mi chiquita ,” Mamá had whispered, hugging Margarita goodbye.

Margarita’s favorite uncle was taking care of her while Mamá was gone, but Margarita still missed her very much. So while she sewed, Margarita thought of her. Pulling a strip of bright green fabric from the pile, she thought of her mamá’s beautiful, green eyes. The pink trim around the bodice reminded her of Mamá’s round cheeks when she smiled. And her mamá’s silky, black hair was like the thread she used to sew. Margarita’s spirits had already begun to lift when the telephone rang.
“Mamá!” Margarita shouted happily into the phone.
“Hola, mi chiquita. I don’t have much time to talk. Each minute I use on the phone card today is one less I will have to hear your precious voice next week,” her mamá said. “How is your grandfather?”
“Much better with the new medicine,” Margarita told her.
“Good,” her mamá said. “And your uncle?”
“He leaves early every morning for the sugarcane fields.”
“Yes, spring is the time to plant,” her mamá said, “and it is the time for flowers. Is your costume for the festival finished?”
“Almost, Mamá,” Margarita answered, “and it is so pretty! I wish you could be here to see me dance.”
“I will, mi chiquita. When the bells of Santa Cruz chime at noon, think of me,” her mamá said, “and I will be watching you in my heart.”

On Saturday, Margarita put on her green blouse with bright trims and her red, ruffled skirt. She braided her long, black hair, weaving colorful ribbons into it, and tied her dancing shoes. After reading a good-luck note her uncle had left her, she kissed her sleeping grandfather and hurried to the festival.
When she arrived, her spirits sank. All the other girls’ mothers were there, and they fussed with the girls’ costumes and smoothed their hair. Margarita missed her mamá more than ever.

At the signal, Margarita lifted the palm with its rows of colorful tropical flowers and joined the parade with the other dancers. Her feet felt as heavy as the palm in her hand. Even seeing the grandstand that held the American president didn’t seem to help.

When the dancers neared the grandstand, the bells of Santa Cruz began to chime. As they rang out, Margarita imagined her mother watching. Suddenly, her feet felt lighter. Holding her palm high, Margarita dipped and twirled, clicking her heels to the beat of the music. She pictured her mother nodding and smiling, and Margarita smiled happily back.
After the parade, a man in an official uniform came up to her. “The American president wishes to meet the talented dancer in the beautiful costume,” he said.
Margarita was led up to the grandstand. As she reached to shake the hand of the American president, there was a sudden click, click, click of photographers’ cameras.
Early the next morning, the telephone rang. Yawning, Margarita was surprised to hear her mamá’s voice at the other end. “Mamá! You already called this week. Is everything all right?” she asked.
“Yes, Margarita. I’m calling about the festival — ”
“Oh, Mamá. I wish you could’ve seen me,” Margarita said before her mamá could finish.
“That is why I’m calling. I see you, mi chiquita,” her mamá said.
“I know, Mamá. When the bells at Santa Cruz chimed, I felt you thinking of me and I danced, holding my palm straight and high. I wish you could have seen me.”
“I see you, Margarita,” her mamá insisted.
“I mean really see me, Mamá.”
“I see you, Margarita,” her mamá said. “I see you everywhere today — on the street corner, at the coffee shop, even on the kitchen table.”
“The kitchen table?” Margarita said, confused.
“Yes.” Her mamá laughed. “You are here in your pretty costume, with colorful ribbons in your hair and a big smile on your face. I even see the flowers on your palm.”
“Oh, Mamá. I’m worried. Are you sick, like Grandfather?”
“Don’t worry, mi chiquita,” her mamá assured her. “I am well and happy today. The American president is shaking the hand of my beautiful daughter on the front page of every newspaper I can find.”

articles.title: Marie Maps the Sea
articles.author: Liz Huyck
articles.description: Liz Huyck describes Marie Tharp's important contributions in mapping the ocean floor.
articles.intro: Marie Tharp is a famous geologist, or someone who studies the earth and what it is made of. In this text, Liz Huyck describes Marie Tharp's important work.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Marie Tharp discovered.
articles.permissions: For cricket: "Marie Maps the Sea" by Liz Huyck, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Young Marie Tharp thought her dad had the best job ever. He traveled around the country, making maps. His maps were special — they revealed hidden things. They showed where different kinds of soil were, and wet and dry places. That helped farmers know what to plant.
He taught Marie to draw maps too. They moved around a lot. Marie changed schools often. But she loved to see the country, and draw, and play her violin.

In college, Marie tried lots of different subjects. Her father always told her, “When you find your life’s work, make sure it is something you like to do.” She took art classes in art, and music, and math, and teaching, and geology. She couldn’t quite make up her mind.

Then one day, her geology teacher pointed to a big map of the earth. Almost three-fourths of it was plain blue ocean. What was under all that water? Was it flat, like a beach? Or were there mountains and valleys, as on land? No one knew. That got Marie’s attention. All that blue blank space — waiting to be mapped.
Exploring with Numbers

Marie became a geologist. She got a job with a group studying the oceans. But Marie wasn’t allowed to go to sea. The Navy did not let women on ships. Marie’s job was to stay in the office and do math and draw. But that didn’t mean she couldn’t explore.

Part of Marie’s job was to keep track of all the data that the ships sent back. One number she often wrote down was the depth of the water. Ships measure this with sonar — sending out a sound ping and timing the echo. Often they made many measurements each day. In the old days, sailors lowered a weight on a string until it hit the bottom, then measured the string.

The office had huge books listing how deep the ocean was along routes where ships sailed. Over the years, many sailors had noticed that the ocean got shallower down the middle of the Atlantic. Were there mountains under the sea?
All those books filled with depth measurements gave Marie an idea. Could she use them to make a new kind of map — of the whole sea floor?
She plotted the numbers on graphs. Each graph made a picture of the depth along a line east to west, like a slice of the sea floor.

She put the slices together to make a 3-D map. Where the soundings showed a deep bottom, that was a valley or canyon. Where it stayed the same for a long way, that meant a flat plain. The numbers revealed underwater mountains. And lots of blank places.

With the help of her fellow geologist Bruce Heezen, Marie set out to fill in the empty places. Bruce went out on ships and collected data, and together they made the maps. Ocean scientists from other countries shared their measurements. It took many years. But eventually, all those thousands of depth readings fit together to make the very first map of the ocean floor.
Sea Floor Surprise

The new map showed that the bottom of the ocean is not flat at all. It is filled with mountains and valleys, just like the land. There is indeed a long line of mountains down the middle of the Atlantic Ocean. It continued on across the whole globe, like the seam on a baseball.
In the middle of these mountains was another surprise — a long crack or rift, right down the center. That rift helped solve an old puzzle.

In 1915, a German geologist called Alfred Wegener had come up with a truly wild idea. He suggested that the continents move. He said that the reason South America and Africa look like they should fit together is because they once did. The continents had drifted apart.
This idea seemed so preposterous that not many people believed him. After all, how could huge pieces of Earth’s crust move? Geologists had been arguing about Wegener’s idea ever since.

But Marie’s map proved Wegener right. Its undersea mountains traced the edges of big, rocky plates. Where two plates are pulling apart, magma bubbles out of the crack, making underwater mountains. At other edges, plates are crunching together. This makes deep trenches and triggers earthquakes and volcanoes.
Modern satellites can now actually measure the continents moving — very slowly, a few centimeters a year. And Marie’s map showed the way.

options.label: The ocean floor is very deep and dark.

Marie Tharp worked with her father to make maps.
The ocean floor is impossible to know more about.
