articles.title: How Dinosaurs Started to Fly
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler explains what paleontologists have learned about the earliest birds.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes what scientists have learned about how dinosaurs evolved, or changed, into modern birds over millions of years.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists have learned about Archaeopteryx.
articles.permissions: "How Dinosaurs Started to Fly" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: When you see a tiny hummingbird flitting around a flower, you probably don’t say, “Wow, what a cute dinosaur!” But you should. Today’s birds are descended from a group of two-legged, meat-eating dinosaurs called theropods.

Probably the most famous theropod is the huge Tyrannosaurus rex , but birds evolved from smaller theropods — ones that were ostrich or pony-sized, not school bus-sized like a T. rex . Today, the connection is so well understood that scientists refer to birds as “avian dinosaurs.” (“Avian” means something related to birds.)

For many years, birds’ origins were a great mystery. But in the 1860s, a very curious fossil was discovered in Germany. It was of a strange creature, about the size of a large crow, and it had feathers along its arms and tail.

Many people didn’t know what to make of it. The fossil dated back about 150 million years to the Jurassic period, when dinosaurs ruled the earth. No birds were known to have lived back then. (Some people even thought the fossil might be of an angel!) But scientists who studied the fossil classified it as a bird — the earliest bird — and named it Archaeopteryx (ark-ee-OPT-er-ix), which in ancient Greek means “ancient feather or wing.”

Not all scientists were convinced that Archaeopteryx was a bird. Yes, it had feathers and wings. But it also had teeth, claws, and a long, bony tail like a dinosaur. It turns out that this half-dinosaur, half-bird fossil was a huge find — the first discovery of a transitional creature linking modern, living animals with distant ancestors that looked very different from them.
The discovery came at a perfect time.

Just a couple of years earlier, scientist Charles Darwin had explained how animals and other living things slowly change, or evolve, over time through a process called natural selection. It works like this: Living things pass along various traits — how they look, are built, and behave — from one generation to the next through their genes. As genes get copied, sometimes mistakes are made, creating variations in traits. Some changes don’t matter much, others are harmful, and some are helpful — like making an animal faster, so it can catch its next meal more easily or escape being eaten itself.

When a new trait gives an animal an advantage over others of its kind, that animal has a better chance of surviving, reproducing and passing its genes along to its offspring. That’s why Darwin’s theory is often called “survival of the fittest,” with the “fittest” organisms having the traits best suited to their environments. And if an environment changes, the traits that give an organism an advantage will gradually evolve, too.
Over time, a series of small changes adds up, often resulting in an organism that looks nothing like its distant ancestors — like modern birds and their reptilian ancestors, the dinosaurs.

In the past three decades, scientists — especially paleontologists (PALE-ee-on-TOL-uh-jists), who study life in the past — have found additional fossils and learned more about the dinosaur-bird transition than they had in the previous two centuries. They discovered that all the important features of modern birds evolved one by one, often for different purposes, and that the transition from dinosaur to bird was so smooth that it is hard to identify the actual “first bird.”

For many years, people thought that only birds had feathers. But in the 1990s, paleontologists discovered that feathers emerged long before the evolution of birds — and early feathers didn’t even help the animals fly. Like all evolution, it took a series of gradual changes to create flight feathers.

At first, some small theropods 125 million years ago grew short, hair-like feathers on their heads, necks, and bodies — mainly to keep them warm. These feathers were different colors and arranged in various patterns, so they also may have helped the dinosaurs hide, recognize their own kind, or find mates. The small, non-avian theropod Sinosauropteryx , for example, rocked reddish-orange and white stripes!

Over time, some theropods grew fluffy or downy feathers, while others evolved a central quill — the hollow shaft or stalk of a feather — with fuzzy branches shooting off the sides. The hollow shafts may have dispersed heat, keeping dinosaurs from getting too hot. Later, the feathers filled in, becoming larger, lighter and more flexible. Even then, feathers were not just for flying. Fossils showed that 75 million years ago, Oviraptors spread their feathered arms around their egg-filled nests, much like chickens today keep their eggs warm.

Eventually, feathers helped dinosaurs get airborne. The early feathers probably just helped little dinosaurs leap into the air to catch flying insects. But larger feathers made flight possible — first helping the dinosaurs scramble up into trees, then glide from branch to branch. By the time Archaeopteryx came around, it sported large flight feathers on its wings, similar to those on modern birds. Though Archaeopteryx could not soar like an eagle, it was capable of short bursts of powered flight, like today’s pheasants. These abilities gave avian dinosaurs a huge advantage, because they could reach new habitats and food sources and escape predators more easily.

Feathers alone did not help the avian dinosaurs take to the skies. Their bodies also had to change. Here again, the changes probably served different purposes at first. The avian dinosaurs’ wrist joints changed so they could flex sideways — great for whipping their arms around to snatch prey and essential, later, for tucking their wings at rest and flapping more powerfully during flight. Meanwhile, their bodies grew smaller and their bones lighter, their arms became longer than their legs, and they evolved feathers on a shortened tail to help in flight.

Despite the wonder of wings, it may have been the development of beaks that helped some birds to survive the mass extinction 65 million years ago. Non-avian dinosaurs, toothed birds, and pterosaurs — the huge, flying reptiles that dominated the skies for millions of years — all went extinct then. Why did the beaked birds survive? It was not flight that gave them the crucial advantage then, but food. With their beaks, they could pluck up nuts and seeds from the ground — probably the only foods that survived the destruction caused by a massive asteroid impact, the cause of the mass extinction. Animals that needed to eat leafy vegetation or meat were out of luck.

Beaked birds were just one tiny twig on the dinosaurs’ branch of the tree of life, but they were the only dinosaurs to survive. Over time, they have evolved and diversified into around 10,000 species of modern birds today.

articles.title: Fossils: Keys to the Past
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler describes different types of fossils and what scientists can learn from them.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes the important information we can learn from different types of fossils.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what scientists can learn from different types of fossils.
articles.permissions: "Fossils: Keys to the Past" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Fossils reveal the secrets of Earth’s past: the animals and plants from long ago, how they lived, and what their world was like. For scientists, especially paleontologists who study the history of life on Earth (including dinosaurs!), finding a good fossil is like finding hidden treasure.

Fossils are the preserved remains, impressions, or traces of animals and plants that lived long ago. On rare occasions, they include the actual remains, or bodies, of ancient animals or plants, which managed to survive the passage of time pretty much unchanged — maybe the hard shell of a scallop, the armored exoskeleton of a beetle, the tooth of a woolly mammoth, or a little lizard entombed in amber (fossilized tree resin).

The vast majority of the time, however, fossils are rocks. Paleontologists divide fossils into two main groups. Some fossils, called body fossils , show the structure of the plant or animal. They form directly from the remains of plants and animals. These fossils include such things as hardened dinosaur bones, petrified wood, or a dark film that a leaf or insect left behind. Other fossils, called trace fossils , record signs of animal or plant activities, such as walking, burrowing, feeding, scratching, or even resting. Trace fossils do not include the actual bodies. Instead, they form from impressions left behind by plants and animals, such as bird tracks, an insect’s burrow, or even dinosaur poop.

Most animals and plants don’t become fossils after they die. They simply decompose, decaying or breaking down into little bits, or get gobbled up by a scavenger. But some remains get buried too fast for those things to happen. An animal might die near a body of water and sink to the bottom, where its remains get covered in sediment. The animal’s soft parts usually still decay, leaving only hard bits like bones and teeth. As sediment builds up, mineral-rich water seeps into the remains, depositing minerals in the tiny spaces of the bones and even replacing the original bone. The newly deposited minerals react with those in the animal’s remains and, thanks to that chemical reaction, harden into fossils. These hard fossils, called cast fossils , take the shape of the original animal parts, like dinosaur bones.

Sometimes fossils form in a different way. As sediment builds up, it hardens around the animal’s remains. Water seeps into the area where the animal’s remains are decomposing. But instead of leaving behind mineral deposits that harden in the shape of the animal, the water washes the animal’s decaying remains away. The animal’s shape is left in the surrounding, fossilized rock. These fossils are called mold fossils .

Both cast and mold fossils are 3D and can show all the details of the original animal (or plant). The big difference is how they form. An easy way to remember the difference is to think about making Jell-O! You fill a fancy pan with liquid Jell-O and let it get firm. The Jell-O ends up shaped like the fancy pan; it is cast in the same shape. The fancy pan itself is a mold. Both body and trace fossils can form as either cast or mold fossils.

Most fossils are buried deep in the Earth. But over time, land changes in ways that bring fossils closer to the surface. The minerals in rock break down through a process called weathering. And rock, sediment, and soil may be cleared away by wind, water, and ice — processes known as erosion. As the Earth’s surface changes, scientists can dig up new fossils and learn more about past life and Earth’s history. (In fact, the word “fossil” comes from the Latin word fossilis , which means “dug up.”)

Over hundreds of millions of years, layers of sediment built up on the Earth, burying animals and plants specific to certain time periods. As scientists study older and older layers, they find fossils from different times in Earth’s history. In rock that formed before a certain time, roughly 2.8 million years ago, scientists will not find human fossils.If they keep digging back further, they will find layers with no modern birds, then no mammals, then no reptiles and so on. The layers represent a timeline of life on Earth.

Knowing what lived when helps scientists identify the age of new discoveries. Scientists know, for example, that ammonites, cool-looking spiraled marine animals, lived in the Paleozoic and Mesozoic Eras roughly 408 to 65 million years ago. So if they dig up an ammonite, they know the surrounding rock is that old, too. And if they discover a new fossil in that same sedimentary layer, they know it is from the same time period.

Fossils are our keys to understanding prehistoric life and Earth’s history. By studying fossils, we learn about the great variety of plants and animals that lived in the past — not just what they looked like ( body fossils ), but also how and where they moved and what they ate ( trace fossils ). By comparing fossils from different time periods, we can track the evolution of a species, see how it adapted to changes in its environment, and understand more about the climate and environment where the fossils were buried. Some of today’s deserts, for example, were lush forests or vast oceans long ago. How do we know? Paleontologists found fish fossils in the desert.

articles.title: Our Creek
articles.subtitle: This is the stream that flows near our school. It's called Standing Stone Creek. People walk by it every day without even noticing it. But we think it's pretty special.
articles.author: Celina Seftas and the Standing Stone Creek crew
articles.description: The Standing Stone Creek is a small stream in Pennsylvania that runs into the Susquehanna River.
articles.intro: Standing Stone Creek is a stream that runs into the Susquehanna River in Pennsylvania. In this informational text, Celina Seftas and the Standing Stone Creek crew discuss the small stream.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, takes notes on what the authors study about the stream.
articles.permissions: "Our Creek" from Ask by by Celina Seftas and the Standing Stone Creek crew. Copyright © 2018 by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced by permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://cricketmedia.com/info/licensing2 for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Where Does the Water Come From? Where Is It Going?

The water in our creek comes from rain and snow falling on land for miles around. Standing Stone Creek is one of many small streams that all flow into the Susquehanna River. Eventually, all that water empties into the Atlantic Ocean.

Standing Stone Creek may look small, but it’s important. It carries water to forests, towns, and farms. Lots of animals, birds, and insects live in the stream and along the banks. It’s a fun place for people to boat and fish. And we get our drinking water from it! So it’s important to keep it clean and healthy.
Today we’re going to get to know it a little better.
Along the Banks

First stop is the woods beside the river. Healthy streams need healthy banks. Trees, grasses, and shrubs soak up pollution, keeping it out of the water. Plant roots also hold onto soil so the stream doesn’t get too muddy.
Shade from trees keeps the water cool for fish and other water animals. And many bugs, snails, and other small water creatures eat the leaves that fall in the water.
The Streambed

In streams and rivers, the water is always moving. How fast? We measure this by throwing floats into the water. We count how long it takes them to pass a certain point. We discover it’s moving at different speeds! It’s faster in the middle and slower near the banks.
The bottom and sides of a stream are called the streambed. It’s full of sand and rocks that the rushing water carries along.

Fish and water bugs like rocky riverbeds. They hide out in nooks and crannies between the rocks. Too much sand and mud can clog the living spaces. So a healthy stream needs rocks of different sizes and not too much sticky mud.
What’s in the Water?

The water might look clear, but it’s full of lots of stuff. Wouldn’t you like to know what? We would! So we’re going to do some tests. The first test is just to look. Is the water cloudy or clear? Does it smell funny? A weird smell can be a sign of pollution. Next we do some tests with special kits. Chemicals in each kit change color depending on what’s in the water. Each kit checks for something different.
Dissolved oxygen

The first test measures how much oxygen is in the water. Oxygen is a gas that we breathe in the air. Fish and bugs breathe oxygen too. But the oxygen they breathe is dissolved in the water, sort of like sugar is dissolved in a glass of lemonade. Healthy streams should have plenty of oxygen.

For the oxygen test, we mix drops of different chemicals into a jar of stream water. First eight drops of one, then eight drops of another, then another, then another. First the water goes all thick and cloudy. Then it goes light yellow, and then it turns red! We count how many drops from the last bottle it takes to turn it clear again, and that tells us how much oxygen is in the water.
Good news! Our stream has plenty of oxygen. That might be thanks to the shady trees. Cool water can hold more oxygen than warm water.
pH
The pH test measures how acidic the water is, or how much it wants to dissolve things. A tester strip turns color depending on how acidic the water is. Our water looks good.
Hardness

Then we measure how much mineral stuff is in the water. As water flows through earth and over rocks, it dissolves some of the rock. That fills the water with minerals, the ingredients of rock. Most are harmless, like calcium and iron. But too many minerals can clog water pipes. Our creek has some, but not too much.
Nitrates

Last, we check for nitrates. This is a chemical found in fertilizers. It helps plants grow. That’s good on a farm. But in streams, it can poison fish and make too much algae grow. Our stream has barely any — good!
What Lives in the Water?

Now for our favorite part! What lives in our creek? Stream water is full of living things. Some are tiny swimming things you can only see with a microscope. There are fish, frogs, and salamanders in our creek. We also have snails, crayfish, and freshwater mussels. And lots of bugs and larvae! Some insects, like dragonflies, spend the first part of their lives completely underwater.
Some water creatures are very sensitive. They won’t live in polluted water. Others don’t mind dirty water. If we find bugs and animals that don’t like pollution, that’s a sign our stream is healthy.

Healthy streams also have lots of different species, not just a few. So we’re doing a bug count. We wade out into the stream with cloth nets. Two people hold the net while one kicks up the mud. Small creatures get caught in the net, while the water flows through.

Then we use a handy chart to identify what we find. We find some stonefly and mayfly larvae. That’s good! They only live in very clean water. We count up all the different critters. Each “sensitive” bug gets 3 points. Medium-sensitive kinds are 2 points, and the ones that don’t mind pollution get 1 point. Streams with more than 22 points are very healthy. Standing Stone Creek scored 28!
Finny Friends

Fish live in the stream too. People care about fish — when a stream isn’t healthy enough for fish, people notice. Fish need lots of different water bugs to eat, and cool water with plenty of oxygen. Big rocks, logs, and roots from trees make good places for fish to hide and lay eggs.

We already know a lot about what kinds of fish live in our stream, from what people catch. We aren’t going to catch any today. Instead we’ll learn about them by making fish prints. We paint rubber models of local fish, then press them on paper.
Caring for Your Stream
We’ve learned a lot about Standing Stone Creek today. It’s very healthy, with cool water, lots of rocks for bugs and fish, good oxygen, and not too much pollution. That’s great!

We write down all our test results to send to the water scientists. This helps them keep track of how the watershed is doing. It also lets them see the effect of conservation projects, like planting more trees around rivers.

On our way out, we pick up some stray trash. After all, anything that goes into our little stream could eventually end up in the ocean! It’s fun to think we can help the whales right from our own backyard.
Do you have a creek near your house or school? What’s it like? Could it use a nice friend like you?

articles.title: Carrie, Navajo Fancy
articles.subtitle: A conversation with a young Navajo dancer
articles.author: Nancy Bo Flood
articles.description: Carrie, a young member of the Navajo nation, explains what makes the fancy shawl dance special.
articles.intro: The Navajos are a Native American people of the Southwestern United States. They refer to themselves as Diné, meaning "the people." Powwows are gatherings held by Native American communities, or groups of people with similar backgrounds, that often include singing and dancing. In this interview, Carrie, a young member of the Navajo nation, explains what makes one of these dances special.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the important details of the shawl dance.
articles.permissions: "Carrie, Navajo Fancy" by Nancy Bo Flood, AppleSeeds , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “My name is Carrie. I am Navajo, born into the Bitterwater Clan. I live in Arizona on the Navajo Nation near Tuba City. This part of the reservation is called Tsh Naees Dizi, or ‘the place of many springs.’ While attending grade school in Tuba City, I decided to become a fancy shawl dancer. Dance is important to my people. Dance is a way of praying, healing, celebrating, and even laughing. The traditional dances…were given to us by our ancestors. The oldest dances are for prayer, not for performing. Social dances, like the shawl dance, are for powwows and celebrating.” Carrie sat across from me, her dark eyes shining with pride as she showed me her photo, dressed to dance at a powwow. She "2005 Powwow" by Katherine Fogden is also brought the beaded cedar bag, licensed under CC0. necklaces, and hairpieces she wears when she dances. 1. an area of land set aside for Native Americans by the U.S. government 2. handed down from age to age 3. Social(adjective)done in a group, not alone 4. a special gathering of Native Americans “I chose to learn the shawl dance, a fancy dance. At a powwow, the drums start beating fast, and we start singing. Soon the arena is filled with men and women, girls and boys, of all ages. Everyone is wearing colorful outfits (called regalia). A fancy dancer carries a long-tasseled shawl. We spin and twirl, stepping light and fast. We circle…the arena, lifting our arms and spreading our shawls open, appearing as butterflies. I feel the quick beat, beat, beating of the drums. I sing the words sung by our ancestors…in old languages, so few know their exact meaning. But I…feel what the music is saying. Sometimes I end up crying…” Carrie showed me the special clothes she wears when she dances. From the embroidered shawl to the white buckskin moccasins, everything was a gift from someone in her family.

“Most of my dance regalia is old, treasures handed down from my aunt. When she saw that I was determined to be a dancer, she gave me her shawl embroidered with turquoise flowers…the border of fringe swirls around my moccasins. She also gave me the jewelry she used when dancing. My mother made matching dresses. My favorite was blue, the color of the desert sky . . .beaded with flowers. My grandmother surprised me with her old, special cedar bag. This bag holds sweet-smelling cedar offered with prayers. . . .When I am ready to dance, I tie her cedar bag to my waist belt. “Becoming a dancer means first watching. During the day-long powwows, I sat next to the drummers. Five or six sat around a huge round drum, beating and singing the songs. As I learned the words, I watched how the dancers move their feet, their heads, and their arms. I knew one day I would have to step out and join the circle. Would I ever be brave enough?” Carrie smiled. “I was! There’s no test. You just step out and do it. I wore the gifts from my family and felt the ancient words in my heart. I stepped into the arena. Was I ever surprised! I loved it.” "Carrie, Navajo Fancy" by Nancy Bo Flood, AppleSeeds, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

articles.title: The Woman and Her Bear
articles.subtitle: A Play Based on an Inuit Legend
articles.author: Pat Betteley
articles.description: In this Inuit play, a lonely woman cares for a polar bear.
articles.intro: The Inuit are a group of native, or first, people of the arctic areas of northern Alaska, Canada, and Greenland who have lived there for over a thousand years. The word Inuit means "the people." In this play, a lonely woman cares for a polar bear.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the Old Woman’s actions.

blocks.text: Characters :
-Narrator 1
-Narrator 2
-Narrator 3
-Old Woman
-Nukilik: Villager
-Aput: Villager
-Ikiaq: Little Boy

Narrator 1 : In the cold, dark north, an old woman lived all alone in a little hut at the edge of an Inuit village. She had no husband or sons to hunt for her, so she sometimes had to depend on her neighbors for food.
Ikiaq : My mother sends salmon for your dinner.
Old Woman : Thank you. Tell her I am lucky to have such a generous neighbor. And she is lucky, too — to have such a kind, strong son.

Narrator 1 : The old woman often walked along the shore, staring out at the sea, praying to the gods that she might also have a son. One day, she saw a little white polar bear in the distance. She walked out on the ice to get a closer look.
Old Woman : Where is your mother? Has someone killed her? It seems we are both alone in the world, then. Come, little one.
Narrator 1 : She took him back to her home and shared what little food she had with him.
Old Woman : I will call you Kunik, my son.

Narrator 2 : Kunik and the old woman were happy together. He was a round, friendly, little bear. The children of the village loved to play with him, sliding on the ice and tumbling in the snow together. Kunik grew quickly over the spring and summer. During the long, dark winter, the children taught him to hunt and fish. By spring, he was bringing salmon home to his mother every afternoon.

Old Woman : Thank the gods for you, Kunik. Now I have plenty of meat and fish to eat, furs to keep me warm, and blubber for cooking. Good neighbors, come and share Kunik’s catch with us. There’s more than enough.
Nuklik : She speaks the truth. That bear brings home the biggest seals and the fattest salmon.
Aput : Yes. He makes the rest of us look bad.
Nuklik : And he has grown so strong, he is a danger to our families. For the sake of our children, we must kill him.
Ikiaq : (overhearing) Oh, no! I must warn the Old Woman.
Narrator 2 : When she heard of her neighbors’ wicked plot, the old woman visited each of them to plead for her son’s life.
Old Woman : Kunik is my son. Please do not take him. Kill me, instead.
Nuklik : That bear is getting too strong and dangerous. Tomorrow, he must die. Then the whole village will have a fine feast — and use his fur to keep us warm.
Narrator 2 : The old woman hurried home. She called Kunik to sit beside her.
Old Woman : My son, you must leave at once. The villagers have become jealous and plan to kill you. Run quickly and do not come back.
Narrator 3 : With tears in his eyes, the bear did as his mother asked.
Old Woman : Do not forget me. . .

Narrator 3 : From time to time, the broken-hearted old woman felt very lonely and hungry. Then she left her hut early in the morning to walk far out on the ice, calling Kunik’s name. Soon, a great polar bear came running to meet her.
Old Woman : My son! How are you? Let me check your fur to make sure you have not been injured. No, you look big, strong, and sleek!
Narrator 3 : When he saw that she was hungry, Kunik ran to get her fresh meat and fish. They ate some together, and she took the rest home.

And so it happened for many years. The villagers realized the love between the old woman and her bear was strong and true. And, to this day, the Inuit tell the tale of the faithful bear Kunik and the old woman who brought him up.

articles.title: Smooth Sailing
articles.author: Bonnie Katz
articles.description: A boy must think quickly to save his father while sailing.
articles.intro: Sailing uses wind and sails to move a boat over the water. In this story, a boy goes out sailing with his father.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Sean’s actions while sailing with his father.
articles.permissions: "Smooth Sailing" by Bonnie Katz, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “Prepare to come about!”

Sean turned to look at his father, who was standing confidently at the helm of his newly purchased sailboat. The boat was used but in perfect condition, dark blue with a bright white sail. His dad had named it Smooth Sailing , which was painted in white lettering on the side of the boat.

Sean hated that he couldn’t remember all of the sailing terms his dad tried to teach him. He was afraid to ask, anticipating his father’s reaction, but pushed himself anyway. “Which way are we turning?”

He saw his father frown. “We’re coming about ,” his father said sternly. “That means we are turning into the wind. That way,” his father said after a pause, pointing to the right side of the boat, his frown deepening.

Sean tried to think of a way to remember this so he would not disappoint his father next time. Like the way he remembered the difference between port side and starboard side of the boat. Port had four letters and so did left , so it was easy to remember that port was the left side of the boat. But he could think of no easy way to remember coming about . He hated that he was always disappointing his father.

Sean ducked as he pulled in the sail and the boom swung across the deck of the boat over his head. At least he knew how to do this correctly. He looked back at his father, hoping for a smile or nod or some sort of indication of approval. But his dad was looking up at the sail, not at him.

Sean had mixed feelings about these sailing outings with his father. Ever since his parents divorced two years ago, he only got to see his dad every other weekend. He liked spending time with his father, but when they were together, Sean was reminded of how different he and his dad were. His father was handsome, confident, and outgoing. Sean was shy and had few friends. He hadn’t gotten his father’s good looks and he certainly didn’t have his self-confidence. He enjoyed being on the boat but at the same time always felt nervous. He couldn’t stop thinking about the sailboat race two years ago, when a fierce storm appeared unexpectedly and capsized one of the boats. Two experienced sailors fell overboard and drowned. There was always the potential for sudden extreme weather out on the lake.

He looked up at the sky. Just an hour ago it had been sunny, the water turquoise blue and sparkling, with just a slight breeze blowing. He had started to relax. But now the sky was a menacing gray, clouds were gathering, and the wind had begun gusting angrily. Whitecaps were forming on the water. He tugged nervously on his life jacket and was once again reminded how different he was from his father, who had been swimming and sailing his entire life but never wore a life jacket. He knew his father was disappointed that Sean was not a better swimmer and did not share his passion for being on the water.
“Hey, Sean, can you take the helm for a minute?” his father asked.
Sean hesitated. He didn’t want to, with the weather changing, but he also didn’t want to see that disapproving look on his father’s face, which had become all too familiar.
“Uh, yeah, sure,” Sean replied. He locked the sail in place and went back to take the wheel.
“Just keep her going straight. I’m going to run up to the bow to check something.”

Sean clutched the wheel tightly and looked around for other boats. The wind whipped his hair into his eyes, and he had trouble seeing. But he did not want to risk taking one of his hands off the wheel to brush it back. He looked toward the bow for his father, but the sail blocked his view.

Suddenly, the wind shifted direction, catching him off guard. The boat heeled so sharpy on its side, spraying water in his face, that it felt like it might capsize. Sean knew he had to let some air out of the sail to help right the boat, but in order to do that, he would have to let go of the wheel for a moment. His heart was pounding in his ears. Where was his father?
The boat began to heel even more. “Dad!” he called, but the wind swallowed his voice.

Sean knew he had to act fast. He kept hold of the wheel with his left hand and stretched forward with his right, reaching for the mainsail line, trying to dislodge it from its locked position. After a few yanks, it finally came loose, and he was able to let the air out of the sail. The boat quickly righted itself.

Sean let out a sigh of relief, then looked around the flapping sail for his father. He didn’t see him anywhere. “Dad!” he yelled frantically, but there was no sign of his father. He looked out at the huge waves crashing against the boat. There was nothing but water as far as he could see.

Then he saw it — just a flash of something in the water, about thirty feet from the boat. But then a huge wave crashed, and whatever it was disappeared. Then he saw it again. It was his dad! Another wave crashed over his dad’s head, and once more Sean lost sight of him. A few moments later his dad’s head popped up again, and he tried to wave at Sean. He must be hurt, Sean thought, or he’d be able to swim over to the boat.

Sean had to think fast. How could he get his dad back into the boat? Then he had an idea. As the boat rocked back and forth violently, he reached under the seat for the rope he knew his dad kept there — the rope his father had used trying to teach Sean dozens of sailing knots that he could never remember. Sean knew the most important one was the bowline knot, and he needed that now to save his father.

He pulled the rope into a loop large enough to go over his dad’s shoulders as he thought back to the silly mnemonic his dad had taught him to remember how to tie the knot. “The rabbit comes out of its hole, runs around the tree, and goes back down the hole,” Sean recited, forming the knot and pulling it tight. He quickly tied the other end onto the boat with the same knot, then threw the loop end to his dad.

His first throw wasn’t far enough, so he hauled the rope back in and heaved it again. This time it landed just a few feet from his dad, who was able to reach out and grab it. Sean watched as his dad, using only one arm, struggled to pull the loop over his head and under his shoulders. Sean then used all his strength to pull his dad slowly toward the boat.
When his dad finally drew near, the water settled down a little, and it was not quite as rough. As Sean helped his dad climb back into the boat, he saw that his father’s right arm was clearly broken.
“You saved my life, son,” his dad said. He lifted the rope over his head with his left hand and fingered the knot. “Nice bowline,” he said smiling, looking Sean right in the eyes.

Sean felt a rush of relief and pride. But he knew there was more to do and stepped quickly to take the wheel. He had to get his dad to the hospital, and to reach shore they were going to have to change direction. He looked at his dad from the helm, smiled, and called, “Prepare to come about!”

options.label: Never judge a book by its cover.
Stay away from people who do bad things.
Working through fear can help you succeed.
Treat others the way you want to be treated.

articles.title: Katherine Johnson
articles.author: Britannica Kids
articles.description: Katherine Johnson was a Black female scientist who helped NASA's early space missions succeed.
articles.intro: Katherine Johnson (1918-2020) was a Black woman who worked at the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), the government group in charge of studying air and space. In this biography, Britannica Kids describes Johnson's life and her work as a mathematician and scientist.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Katherine’s successes.
articles.permissions: Excerpted with permission from Britannica Kids, © 2022 by Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.

blocks.text: Introduction

Katherine Johnson was an American mathematician. She made important contributions to the United States space program during her career at the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA). Her work helped send astronauts to the Moon.
Early Life

Katherine Coleman was born on August 26, 1918, in White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia. Her intelligence and skill with numbers became obvious when she was a child. She was in high school by the time she was 10 years old. Katherine graduated from West Virginia State College in 1937 with highest honors and then took a teaching job in Virginia.

In 1939 Katherine was selected to be one of the first three African American students to enroll in a graduate program at West Virginia University. She studied math but soon left to take care of her family. She had married James Goble that year. He died in 1956. (She later married James Johnson.)
Career

In 1953 Johnson began work at the West Area Computing unit of the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics (NACA). The West Computers, as they were known, were a group of African American women. They studied data from tests and provided mathematical computations that were essential to the success of the U.S. space program.
The West Computers were segregated from white workers. They were forced to use separate bathrooms and dining facilities. That changed when NACA became NASA in 1958.

At NASA Johnson was a member of the Space Task Group. She calculated the flight path for the spacecraft that put the first U.S astronaut in space in 1961. The year before she had coauthored a paper with an engineer. It was the first time a woman in her division received credit as an author of a research report. She authored or coauthored 26 research reports during her career.

In 1962 John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth. However, before he left the ground, he wanted to make sure the electronic computer had planned the flight correctly. He asked to have Johnson double check the computer’s calculations. Johnson was also part of the team that calculated where and when the rocket would be launched that would send the first three men to the Moon. Johnson also worked on the space shuttle program. She retired from NASA in 1986. Johnson died on February 24, 2020.
Honors and Awards

Johnson received many awards and honors. In 2015 she was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the highest civilian honor in the United States. NASA honored her in 2016 by naming a building, the Katherine G. Johnson Computational Research Facility, after her. That year the book Hidden Figures: The American Dream and the Untold Story of the Black Women Mathematicians Who Helped Win the Space Race was published. It tells the story of the West Computers, including Johnson, Dorothy Vaughan, and Mary Jackson. A motion picture based on the book was also released in 2016.

options.label: Katherine Johnson was a scientist who did important work for NASA.

articles.title: Egyptian Pets: Falcons, Monkeys, and Crocodiles, Oh My!
articles.author: Summer Blais
articles.description: Summer Blais explains how the ancient Egyptians cared for their pets.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Summer Blais explains how the ancient Egyptians treated their pets.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the ancient Egyptians’ actions toward their pets.
articles.permissions: Egyptian Pets: Falcons, Monkeys, and Crocodiles, Oh My! By Summer Blais. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Ask someone if they have a pet and they might tell you they have a cat, a dog, maybe a pet rabbit — something small, something kept in a cage. But imagine taking your pet hippo for a walk around the park? Well, the ancient Egyptians — a civilization that existed from around 2700 BCE to 343 BCE — kept all sorts of animals as pets. They had cats, dogs, fish, and birds like we do today. But they also kept baboons, monkeys, gazelles, lions, mongoose, and crocodiles as pets.
Falcons, Monkeys, and Crocodiles, Oh My!

Okay, so some of these animals are different from what we consider to be “pets” today. But just like we love our excitable dogs, moody cats and pet gerbils, the ancient Egyptians deeply loved their animal companions. In fact, they considered pets to be gifts from the gods.

You see, many of the pets ancient Egyptians kept represented the power of gods. The falcon represented the power of Horus — a sky god in ancient Egyptian mythology, or Montu — a god of war. Pharaohs (a pharaoh was a king or queen in ancient Egypt) kept falcons for hunting but also as a symbol of divine power. The ibis was another popular bird for richer citizens. It represented wisdom and the god Thoth — the Egyptian god of writing, magic, wisdom, and the moon.

Crocodiles were kept as representatives of the god Sobek, the crocodile god. Sobek was considered a creator god in certain periods of Egyptian history. Pet crocodiles were often fed choice cuts of meat and honey cakes — better food than most Egyptians ate at the time!
Doggone It
Okay, so even though the ancient Egyptians kept baboons, monkeys, hippos, and falcons as pets, they also loved dogs and cats as much as we do today.

The dog was considered a very important member of the household. But because they required more care than cats, dogs were more often kept by the upper-class citizens, who were better able to afford them. The dogs of ancient Egypt were mostly breeds we know today, including the Basenji, Greyhound, and Whippet. People named their dogs after loved ones, or even honored them with the names of gods.

Did you know the Egyptians are credited with the invention of the dog collar and leash? The early collars were simple leather bands but became even more fancy as time went on. Early pictures from ancient Egypt show men walking dogs in these collars attached to cords.
The Purrrrfect Pet
Although dogs were common pets for ancient Egyptians, the cat is far better known as the most popular pet at the time. Most households had a pet cat — often more than one.

Cats were popular because the goddess Bastet was thought to look like a cat. Statues and illustrations of Bastet show her as having the body of a woman and the face of a cat. She was the original Cat Woman! Bastet was also the Egyptian goddess of the home, women’s secrets, and childbirth, and she protected the home from evil spirits and disease. It was believed that keeping cats honored Bastet and would inspire her to protect the house. But even if cats didn’t actually keep away evil spirits or disease, they did keep away other unwanted visitors such as rats and snakes.

Cats were considered so valuable that the penalty for killing a cat in Egypt — even by accident — was death! And if a building was on fire, men would save the cats first, even before putting the fire out or saving themselves or their loved ones!

Cats were so important to the ancient Egyptians that they lost a war because of them. In 525 BCE the Persian general Cambyses II invaded Egypt. Cambyses II, knowing how the Egyptians felt about certain animals, herded cats (yes, it can be done!) along with dogs and sheep, and led them in front of his soldiers as they marched into battle. They also painted an image of cats and Bastet on their shields.
The Egyptians did not want to hurt the sacred cats and anger the goddess Bastet, so they surrendered, and the Persians won the war without even much of a battle.
A Pets Passing

In ancient Egypt, when a beloved pet died, the family would have the pet mummified with as much care as they would for a human member of the family. They often decorated the body with jewels and gold. To show how sad they were, the family members would shave the hair from their bodies completely, including their eyebrows.
It’s clear that even though the pets ancient Egyptians kept were not always the same types of animals we keep today, they loved their pets just as much as we do.

options.label: Pets were an important part of daily life in ancient Egypt.
Everyone should keep pets like the ancient Egyptians did.
Ancient Egyptians owned pets to stay safe from other civilizations.
All pets in ancient Egypt were named after gods and other people.

articles.title: Fly High, Bessie Coleman
articles.author: Jane Sutcliffe
articles.description: Jane Sutcliffe discusses the first African American woman to receive her pilot license.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Jane Sutcliffe discusses the first African American woman to receive her pilot license. Bessie Coleman was born in 1892 and received her pilot license in 1921.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what challenges Coleman faced as she worked towards becoming a pilot.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Two thousand people sat with their faces turned to the sky. High above the airfield, a pilot had just finished carving a crisp figure eight in the air. Suddenly, the plane seemed to stumble. Twisting and turning, it began to fall from the sky. The crowd watched in horror. Had something happened to the pilot?

But the woman in the cockpit of the plane on October 15, 1922, was in perfect control. Only two hundred feet above the ground she straightened out the tumbling aircraft and soared back into the sky. By the time she landed her plane, the crowd was on its feet, roaring with delight. Everyone cheered for Bessie Coleman, the first licensed black pilot in the world.
Growing Up

Bessie Coleman was born on January 26, 1892. She was a bright girl and a star pupil in school. In Waxahachie, Texas, where Bessie grew up, black children and white children attended different schools. Each year Bessie’s school closed for months at a time. Instead of studying, the children joined their parents picking cotton on big plantations. Bessie’s mother was proud of her daughter’s sharp mind. She didn’t want Bessie to spend her life picking cotton, and urged her to do something special with her life.
Learning to Fly

In 1915, when she was 23, Bessie Coleman moved to Chicago. She found a job as a manicurist in a men’s barbershop. Coleman loved her job and the interesting people she met there. After the United States entered World War I in 1917, soldiers returning from the war often came to the shop. Coleman was fascinated by their stories of daredevil pilots. She read everything she could about airplanes and flying. She later recalled, “All the articles I read finally convinced me I should be up there flying and not just reading about it.”
Bessie Coleman asked some of Chicago’s pilots for lessons. They refused. No one thought that an African American woman could learn to fly.

In desperation, Coleman asked Robert Abbott for help. Abbott owned Chicago’s African American newspaper, The Chicago Defender . He had often promised to help members of the black community with their problems. Abbott told Coleman to forget about learning to fly in the United States. Go to France, he said to her, where no one would care if her skin was black or white.

So she did. First Coleman learned to speak French. Then she applied to a French flying school and was accepted. On November 20, 1920, Coleman sailed for France, where she spent the next seven months taking flying lessons. She learned to fly straight and level, and to turn and bank the plane. She practiced making perfect landings. On a second trip to Europe, she spent months mastering rolls, loops, and spins. These were the tricks she would need if she planned to make her living as a performing pilot.
Performing in Airshows

Coleman returned to the United States in the summer of 1922. Wherever she performed, other African Americans wanted to know where they, too, could learn to fly. It was a question that made Coleman sad. She hoped that she could make enough money from her airshows to buy her own plane. Then she could open a school so everyone would have a chance to feel the freedom she felt in the sky.

By early 1923, Coleman was close to her goal. She had saved her money and bought a plane. Then, as she was flying to an airshow in California, her engine stalled. The brand-new plane crashed to the ground.
Coleman suffered a broken leg and three broken ribs. Still, she refused to quit. “Tell them all that as soon as I can walk I’m going to fly!” she wrote to friends and fans.

Many people, both black and white, were very impressed by Coleman’s determination. A white businessman helped her buy another plane. By 1926, Coleman was back where she had been before the crash. She wrote to her sister, “I am right on the threshold of opening a school.”

That spring, Bessie Coleman was invited to perform in Jacksonville, Florida. Early on the morning of April 30, 1926, Coleman and another pilot took off for a short flight around the airshow field. At first everything went smoothly. Then a wrench that had been lying loose in the plane slid into the control gears, jamming them. Suddenly, the plane flipped upside down. Coleman had not strapped herself in, and she fell to the ground. Moments later, the plane crashed, killing the other pilot.

At 34, Bessie Coleman was dead, but her dream survived. In 1929, three years after her death, the Bessie Coleman Aero Clubs were formed. The clubs encouraged and trained African American pilots — just as Coleman had hoped to do. In 1931, the clubs sponsored the first all African-American airshow. Bessie Coleman would have been proud.

options.label: Coleman helped other African American women overcome unfair treatment by opening her own pilot school.
Coleman was prevented from taking flying lesson in America, despite her desire to help during World War I as a pilot.
Coleman was treated unfairly because of her race as she attempted to fulfill her dream of becoming a pilot.
Coleman was treated unfairly in the United States for her gender and had to go to France to learn how to fly.

articles.title: Allergic to Lunch
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: This article explains the science behind food allergies and how people with allergies can stay safe.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains why some people are allergic to certain foods, or become ill when they eat them.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the ways people with food allergies stay safe.
articles.permissions: "Allergic to Lunch" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Do you have nut-free classrooms in your school? Or nut-free tables at lunch? You might have friends who can’t be around certain foods. You may have an allergy yourself. But what does having a food allergy really mean? Why do people who have one need to be so careful?

A food allergy is an immune system mistake. An immune system is a network of cells, tissues and body organs that work together to protect the body. Your immune system is always on the lookout for germs that could harm you. When a germ enters your body, your immune system springs into action to stop it. Antibodies are made by white blood cells. These antibodies fight germs. They also remember what a germ looks like, in case it tries to come back. The immune system does a great job, but sometimes it might get confused. That’s how food allergies happen.

Your body makes five main kinds of antibodies. One is called IgE. In some people, IgE mistakes certain foods as threats. So, if a person eats that food, the antibody acts as if the body is under attack by germs. It signals cells to release histamines, chemicals that cause parts of the body to react. In most food allergies, histamines are released in the skin, mouth, throat, and lungs.

Histamines signal nerves to itch. They make small blood vessels swell, so skin becomes red and puffy. They may also cause the nose and lungs to pump out mucus. That triggers coughing and sneezing. The stomach may be upset. In some people, these reactions are just annoying. For people with very bad allergies, they’re life-threatening.

Some allergies cause the immune system to work overtime. It floods the body with a large amount of histamines and other chemicals. Airways tighten and the throat swells, so the person has trouble breathing. Their heart might beat faster than normal. They could pass out. This reaction is an emergency. Without treatment, the person could die.

People with these kinds of food allergies often carry a special kind of shot, or leave one with a teacher or the school nurse. Many people call this shot an Epi-pen, which is the name of a company who are well known for making the shot. This shot delivers a medicine that helps open-up airways so the person can breathe. It gives them time to be taken to the hospital for more help. People with milder allergies may take a medicine called an antihistamine. It blocks the histamines that cause itching, sneezing, and other reactions.

Scientists think that about one out of thirteen kids has a food allergy. Nobody knows why some people are allergic to some foods but others aren’t. If you have a family member who’s allergic, it increases your chances of having a food allergy. But you might never have one. Or you could be allergic to a different food than your family member. You could also be the only one in your family with an allergy. Kids are most often allergic to peanuts, eggs, milk, wheat, and soy. Some grow out of their allergies. Others don’t.

Avoiding the food completely is the best treatment for people with allergies. They read labels on food packages to see how it was made. In a restaurant, they check the menu and ask questions. Schools set up nut-free classrooms and special lunch tables to help keep them safe.

Some people are so allergic that they don’t have to eat the food to have a reaction. Even touching or smelling it can cause them problems. So, while you might want to eat peanut butter crackers in your nut-free classroom, leaving them at home could save someone’s life.

options.label: Some people have food allergies because their body thinks germs are attacking it.
All adults should carry an Epi-pen since everyone has at least one food allergy.
People with food allergies have to learn and eat in different classrooms.
Scientists do not know a lot about how to treat food allergies.

questions.prompt: What is one main idea of the text?

options.label: The author tells a story about a time they had a food allergy and what happened after they ate the wrong food.
The author explains what a food allergy is and then gives details about how the body reacts to a food allergy.
The author explains why food allergies are a problem for people and different ways to stop food allergies.
The author asks common questions about food allergies and then gives answers to those questions.

articles.title: I Will Be a Chemist: Mario José Molina / Voy a ser Químico: Mario José Molina
articles.author: Alma Flor Ada / por Alma Flor Ada
articles.description: A boy receives an introduction to chemistry from his aunt.
articles.intro: Chemistry is the science that studies the small pieces that make up everything in the world. Mario José Molina is a famous Mexican chemist who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1995, an honor given to someone who makes important findings in chemistry. Molina helped the world to understand how humans' actions were causing pollution, damage to the earth caused by harmful materials. In this poem by Alma Flor Ada, the speaker shares how Molina became interested in chemistry.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the speaker learns from his aunt Esther.
articles.permissions: "I Will Be a Chemist" by Alma Flor Ada. Copyright (c) 2015. All rights reserved. Used with permission by Alma Flor Ada.

blocks.text: Only a drop of water
but looking under the microscope
I see things that move inside that very drop.
My aunt Esther has given me a chemistry set.
She says that everything — water, air, earth,
the trunks of trees and our own skin —
is made of small particles we cannot see.
She explains that even these molecules
are made of chemical elements;
just around a hundred elements
combine to make all that exists.
I have started today
in my simple lab in the old unused bathroom
to study these elements.
I will know the secrets of the universe.
Sólo una gota de agua
pero mirándola con el microscopio
veo cosas moviéndose en ella.
Tía Esther me ha regalado un juego de química.
Dice que todo — el agua, el aire, la tierra,
los troncos de los árboles y hasta nuestra piel —
está hecho de pequeñas partículas que no podemos ver.
Explica que las moléculas
están formadas por elementos químicos.
Apenas unos cien elementos
se combinan para crear todo lo que existe.
He empezado hoy
en mi simple laboratorio en el viejo cuarto de baño
a estudiar estos elementos.
Voy a saber los secretos del universo.

options.label: Everyone should have a chemistry set.
You need a microscope to be important.
Great discoveries can start with small beginnings.
Water is the most important secret of the universe.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?

options.label: "I see things that move inside that very drop." (Line 3)
"My aunt Esther has given me a chemistry set." (Line 4)

questions.prompt: Which line from the poem shows the speaker's feelings about chemistry?

options.label: different types of weather
to be in a comfortable position
to wear different clothes for a show

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this poem, the speaker receives a gift from his aunt that led him to become a chemist. Have you ever received a gift that has led you to explore something new? How can the words or actions of people around us impact what we are interested in?

In the poem, the speaker receives a chemistry set as a gift. What do you know about the different branches of science? Is there a branch of science that you find the most interesting? Why? Why is it important that people become scientists?

In the poem, the speaker decides to study the elements in their "simple lab" (Line 13). According to this poem, how do we come to understand how the world works? How is observing the way things work an important part of understanding the world?

articles.title: Thanksgiving at the Lake
articles.author: Megan Hoyt
articles.description: In this Shakespearean sonnet, a speaker reflects on the important role of fathers.
articles.intro: Shakespearean sonnets are a type of poem made up of 14 lines and are divided into three groups of four lines with a final couplet, or two lines, at the end. In each group, the second and fourth lines rhyme, as well as the lines in the couplet. In this Shakespearean sonnet by Megan Hoyt, the speaker tells about watching ducks at the lake.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker describes fathers.
articles.permissions: "Thanksgiving at the Lake" Copyright (c) 2019 Megan Hoyt. Originally published in Thanku: Poems of Gratitude edited by Miranda Paul and published by Lerner Publishing Group, Millbrook Press. Reprinted by permission of the author.

blocks.text: I walk beside the icy lake and spot
A duckling, camouflaged beneath the reeds,
Along the distant edge, a tiny dot —
His papa — gathers food among the weeds.
I wonder, will he slide on wobbly legs,
With mushed-up worms and crunchy little bugs,
While Mama gently warms her other eggs,
To feed his son and give him duckling hugs?
My daddy’s hauling fishing nets onboard
His breath a cloud, he stands on aching feet.
He swings the sail and grasps the heavy cord,
I watch him, twirling, from the captain’s seat.
A busy father’s work is never done,
from daylight’s birth until the setting sun.

options.label: The speaker wishes their father paid more attention to them.
The speaker watches animals caring for their babies.
The speaker has fun playing at the lake.
The speaker feels chilly at the lake.

questions.prompt: Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the poem?

surprised by the papa duck's actions.
upset that her own father is not around.

Both sets of lines show what the speaker does to help.

articles.title: maggie and milly and molly and may
articles.author: E. E. Cummings
articles.description: Four girls have different experiences playing at the beach in this experimental poem.
articles.intro: E. E. Cummings (1894-1962) was an American poet known for trying new and different things in his poems. He played with the form of poems, or how they look on the page and broke many other "rules" of writing. In this poem about four girls at the beach, he chooses not to use capital letters or use spaces between punctuation marks and the next word.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how maggie and milly and molly and may are different.
articles.permissions: "maggie and milly and molly and may". Copyright © 1956, 1984, 1991 by the Trustees for the E. E. Cummings Trust, from COMPLETE POEMS: 1904-1962 by E. E. Cummings, edited by George J. Firmage. Used by permission of Liveright Publishing Corporation.

blocks.text: maggie and milly and molly and may
went down to the beach(to play one day)
and maggie discovered a shell that sang
so sweetly she couldn’t remember her troubles,and
milly befriended a stranded star
whose rays five languid fingers were;
and molly was chased by a horrible thing
which raced sideways while blowing bubbles:and
may came home with a smooth round stone
as small as a world and as large as alone.
For whatever we lose(like a you or a me)
it’s always ourselves we find in the sea

options.label: It is important to play nicely with others.
When someone is left out, it is hard for them to have fun.
Being a good friend means spending time making memories with your playmates.
Even when we are doing the same thing as others, we can all experience it in different ways.

All of the names are words that people think of when they think of the beach.

to weave together.

articles.title: Hello! I'm an Earthquake!
articles.author: Marcia Amidon Lusted
articles.description: An earthquake teaches about its causes and impacts.
articles.intro: In this text, an earthquake introduces itself to the reader.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how an earthquake is “born.”
articles.permissions: "Hello! I'm an Earthquake!" By Marcia Amidon Lusted. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: You don’t know me. But in a little while, you may feel me shake your house and maybe even send your books crashing to the floor. I will set off car alarms with a single shudder and send the wildest of animals scrambling for shelter. I’m not boasting, I’m just saying my power can be so great that sometimes, if you live on land where my type is often found, you may have to learn in school what steps to take if I come crawling along. Yeah, yeah, you’ve probably guessed by now. I’m an earthquake!

You may have seen reports of earthquakes like me on the news. It’s true, we can be rough. We can cause damage to buildings, highways, and bridges, and if we’re strong enough, we can destroy entire towns and cities. We create tsunamis, which are very long and high sea waves that can flood and flatten entire coastal areas. We are often responsible for landslides, which is when huge amounts of rock or earth come tumbling down a slope, and avalanches too, which is similar to a landslide, except we’re now talking snow and ice falling from a hill or mountain. You see, the thing is…a lot of people know all about the destruction that we can cause, but not as many know how I am born, you know? Like, how I get started and do the things I do.
So, let me tell you my story.

What exactly am I? Well, people would describe me as a sudden and violent shaking and rolling of the ground. But there’s more to me than that. This shaking is caused by a very specific movement inside the earth. You might think that the surface of the earth is solid. But it’s actually like a big jigsaw puzzle, with 20 pieces that float on the crust and mantle of the earth, which are like different layers between the surface of the earth and its core. These puzzle pieces on the surface are called tectonic plates . The cracks, or boundaries, between the plates are called faults . The tectonic plates are always slowly moving (yes, you heard me correctly, there’s nothing really stable about the land you are living on) and sometimes these plates bump against each other and get stuck. The pressure builds up at these faults because neither plate can move. Finally, something has to give, and the sudden release of all that energy creates an earthquake like me.

If the plates are forced so that one moves above the other, it is called a reverse fault . If one plate is forced to drop below the other, it is a normal fault . If they suddenly slip sideways by each other, it is a strike-slip fault . I get going when two tectonic plates meet each other at a strike-slip fault. The pressure between them builds up until they finally move by each other. This is the focus, or origin, and it’s where I am born! I am so excited that I send seismic waves of energy in all directions, including up to the earth’s surface. Seismic waves are an energy wave or vibration that moves through the earth. The area right above the focus is what’s known as my epicenter or, to you, “ the earthquake’s epicenter.”

The epicenter is the place on earth’s surface where my seismic waves begin. My first seismic waves travel through the body of the earth itself and move really fast, 20 times faster than the speed of sound! They are the first of my waves that scientists can measure, using a very delicate instrument called a seismograph . Other types of waves follow, and people begin to feel me. They run out of their houses because they are afraid. Sure, I rattle glass windows, and even crack some of them. I knock dishes off shelves and onto the floor, and those may break too. But, really, I’m not a very big earthquake. There are worse earthquakes than me! How can people tell? Well, scientists measure me with the Moment Magnitude Scale , or MMS (which used to be called the Richter Scale). I am a 6.0: big enough to do some minor damage, but not big enough to destroy buildings and bridges. I’m okay with that!

And even after I get started, very quickly I feel myself running out of steam, and growing tired and weak. Most earthquakes only last 10-30 seconds, you see. That said, even after my waves are finished, there will be many others that come after me; smaller earthquakes that will occur over the next weeks or months. These are called aftershocks and are caused by the readjustment of the earth after I am finished.

But even though I’m done being an earthquake, I should warn you I have many relatives all over the world. There are around 20,000 earthquakes every year, or about 55 every day. That’s a lot of earthquakes! Some will be small like me, and a few will be huge and cause severe damage and injuries. I’m sorry about that. But whether we are huge or so small that it just feels like just a big truck driving by your house, it's worth knowing how and why we get going, and that we all begin in the same way.

options.label: How Seismographs Work

articles.title: The Father of Canned Food
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Nicolas Appert's (1749-1841) invention of canning changed the way we store food.
articles.intro: In this text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains how French cook Nicolas Appert (1749-1841) changed food forever.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how preserving food has changed over time.
articles.permissions: "The Father of Canned Food" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Ugh, what smells? Is it the bag of sliced turkey somebody forgot to put back in the refrigerator? Yep, that’s it. The meat has spoiled. It stinks, and it will make you really sick if you eat it. Better throw it out! Keeping food safe to eat is why we have refrigerators, but most homes didn’t have them until the 1940s.

Through much of history, foods were dried, salted, or put in vinegar to preserve them. Those methods could ruin the foods’ taste. Worse, they didn’t always work. Food still went bad, and people still got sick from eating it. In 1790, a French chef named Nicolas Appert set out to invent a better way to store food.

Can you imagine working on a science experiment for nearly fifteen years? That’s what Appert did. At that time, nobody knew exactly why food went bad. The bacteria that make food rot hadn’t been discovered yet. But Appert had a hunch that heat might help, so he ran test after test. He experimented with soups, fruits, jellies, and other foods. All that hard work finally paid off. Appert discovered a way that worked to store food safely.

First, he put the food in a glass bottle. Then he closed the bottle with a cork. He crossed two wires over the top to hold the cork tight. Finally, he put the bottle in boiling water. After experimenting on his own, he decided to test his new bottled food in the real world. In 1803, Appert gave some preserved food to sailors who were leaving on a three-month-long trip. When they came back, the sailors reported that none of the food had gone bad. Even better, his bottled peas were as fresh and tasty as if they’d just been picked.

Appert’s method worked! Now he needed to spread the word. He entered his bottled fruits and vegetables in a food fair, but he didn’t win a prize. He wrote to a group of famous scientists and asked them to taste his bottled food. Like the sailors, the scientists found many of the foods tasted as good as fresh. Then Appert asked the French government to help fund his bottling business. They said they would — if Appert shared his method with everyone.

Appert agreed, and he wrote a book in 1810. Thousands of copies were printed. The book was also sold in Germany and England. Nicolas Appert’s new product was a hit. Vegetables and fruits picked in the summer could be stored for the winter. Travelers would be able to take the bottles on long trips. Appert’s discovery changed the way food was stored.

In 1814, Appert visited London and found a new kind of container that worked to keep food safe. An inventor there had made an iron can and coated it with tin to keep it from rusting. The new “tin can” could keep food as fresh as Appert’s bottles and didn’t break as easily as glass. Appert switched from glass bottles to tin cans. Others did the same. Soon factories around the world were producing cans of fruits, vegetables, meats, and other foods. (Can openers actually weren’t invented until forty-five years after cans. Until then, people had to open cans with a hammer and chisel.)

Today, we understand that the heat during the canning process kills the bacteria that make food go bad. Sealing the can then keeps more bacteria from getting in. Our food producers also have many other methods to store food safely, such as refrigeration, freezing, and freeze-drying. But we still keep canned food on the shelf. It’s all thanks to Nicolas Appert’s years of experiments.

options.label: Canned food can make people sick unless they have a refrigerator.
Nicolas Appert developed a new way to store food that made it last longer.
No one knew why people heated bottles before canning until Appert wrote a book.
Before Appert's experiment people canned food so they could carry it long distances.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

"Vegetables and fruits picked in the summer could be stored for the winter." (Paragraph 6)

options.label: It shows how we can food today.

discussionQuestions.prompt: What is a canned food that you have eaten? Do you agree with the article that it tasted "as fresh and tasty as if they'd just been picked" (Paragraph 4)? How does learning about the canned food process make you appreciate the canned food you have eaten?

Nicolas Appert tried many different ways to preserve food, even though most failed. In fact, his first brilliant idea for preserving food did not even win a prize at the fair! What did Appert do when he faced these setbacks? What kind of person can you infer that Appert was based on his actions?

Canned food was a life-changing invention, but it is probably one we barely think about these days. What is another invention that was life-changing at its time but we do not think about much today? You may want to do research on the internet with an adult or check out your local library for more ideas. Do you have any ideas for how we could make preserved food even tastier and healthier today?

articles.title: Dropping the Ball on New Year's Eve
articles.author: India James
articles.description: The tradition of the "ball drop" on New Year's Eve has its roots in maritime time-telling.
articles.intro: People all over the world celebrate New Year's Eve on December 31st. In this informational text, India James shares about a New Year's Eve tradition, or a practice that has been repeated over time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view.
articles.permissions: "Dropping the Ball on New Year's Eve" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: On December 31st, people all over the world stay up until midnight to welcome in the new year. When the last seconds of the year arrive, the countdown begins. Ten, nine, eight…

In many places around the world, people watch as a ball drops on New Year’s Eve. One of the most famous places to watch the ball drop is in Times Square in New York City. More than one million people come to Times Square to see the ball drop, and more than one billion people tune in on TV!
But where did this tradition come from? And are balls the only thing dropped to celebrate the new year?
A Long Time Ago

Long ago, it was hard for captains of sailing ships to know where they were out in the ocean. Captains could use the stars to find out how far north or south they were. Finding out how far east or west they were was much harder.

One way they could find their location was by comparing the time when the sun was at its highest point to when it was noon at the port they had just left. It was important for sailors to know when it was noon. Ports started building “time balls” that would drop at the same time every day. Captains could look up from their ships and set their clocks when the ball dropped.
The first time ball was built at the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, England, in 1833. It was so popular that more than 150 time balls were soon built in other ports around the world.
Today, GPS has replaced the old way of navigating with clocks. Most of the time balls have been taken down, but the tradition of marking time with a ball drop still happens on New Year’s Eve.

It’s fun to count down to the end of the year! Watching a ball drop is a great way to know when the new year begins. However, people have started dropping things that are much more unusual than a ball.
Anything but A Ball

People in Traverse City, Michigan, throw a huge New Year’s Eve festival. Streets are closed to traffic, and people enjoy eating and talking outside. But instead of watching a ball drop at midnight, people turn their heads to the sky and watch a giant cherry drop down!

When city organizers first started planning a party for New Year’s Eve, they thought about what makes the area special. “This is a very vibrant, agricultural community,” said Mike Kent. He’s the public relations manager for Traverse City Tourism. He remembers city organizers thinking that many farmers in the area grow cherries. They wanted to make their New Year’s Eve celebration unique to their city.
“A cherry makes sense. Let’s drop a cherry,” Mike said.
Every year, they decorate a sculpture of a cherry made from wire, wood, and lights. The cherry is raised in the air by a tall crane and then slowly dropped as the seconds countdown to midnight.

Many tourists come to Traverse City for winter sports. “There is an energy and excitement at New Year’s in the Traverse City area,” said Mike. “But [the cherry drop] is just as much, if not more, for locals as it is for travelers to the region.” The cherry drop is a celebration of the people of Traverse City.

All over the country, people celebrate their local areas by dropping things they’re famous for. In Folly Beach, South Carolina, people watch as a giant pair of flip flops are dropped. In Boise, Idaho, where they grow a lot of potatoes, a big, glowing potato is dropped as fireworks explode around it.

Sailors first started dropping balls to mark time long ago. These days, we do it just for fun. Whether it’s a ball, a bunch of cherries, a pair of flip flops, or a giant potato, watching an object drop is a great way to ring in a new year!

options.label: Watching a ball drop on New Year's Eve is a fun tradition that marks the passing of time.
People travel far distances to watch objects fall from the sky on New Year's Eve.
The way that people tell time on New Year's Eve has changed over the years.
The way that people celebrate New Year's Eve has improved over time.
