questions.prompt: What does Matt mean when he compares Skeena's ears to "rags" (Paragraph 15)?

options.label: It emphasizes how much Matt and Skeena care about each other.
It reveals that Skeena may not be able to be a lead dog.
It stresses how serious Skeena's injuries are.
It shows that Matt cares more about Skeena being a lead than her health.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 26 contribute to the overall structure of the story?
How does Skeena's injury change Matt? Cite evidence from the text in your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Matt and Skeena are friends. How did their friendship develop? Describe a friendship that you have had with a pet or animal.
How does Matt act bravely when he finds Skeena injured? Why was it important for him to be brave in this moment? Describe a time where your bravery helped someone else.

Matt's uncle tells him that he will be able to choose dogs for his sled team when he is "strong enough." What does having inner strength mean to you? How do you develop it? Explain a time when you used your inner strength.

articles.title: Cheese for Dinner
articles.author: Judy Goldman
articles.description: A coyote corners a rabbit for dinner.
articles.intro: Judy Goldman has written for Highlights . In this retelling of the fable, "Cheese for Dinner," a coyote hunts a rabbit.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the rabbit's actions towards the coyote.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Coyote was hiding behind a boulder near the lake. He licked his chops and stared at Conejo (ko-NEH-ho). The unwary rabbit was gazing at the full moon that lit the cloudless sky.
Coyote lunged at Conejo. The rabbit bounded away, leaping over rocks and around bushes, but Coyote stayed right behind him, snapping at his heels.
Conejo came to a wall of rock. Desperately, he looked for a way to escape. Finding none, he cowered against the wall, thinking of what to do.
Coyote scrambled to a stop and brought his jaws close to Conejo. “Caught you,” he said, flashing his pointy white teeth. “I haven’t eaten for two days, and I’m hungry.”
Conejo’s mind raced. “You don’t want to eat me,” he said. “I’m just a scrawny rabbit.”
“I know you’re not much, but you’ll do,” Coyote said, opening his mouth wide.

“Wait!” Conejo yelled. “I have a better idea. I know where you can get something delicious to eat. Just before you started to chase me, I saw an enormous wheel of cheese resting on the bottom of the lake.”
“Why didn’t you get it?” Coyote asked.
“I wanted to, but I can’t swim. I was thinking about how to fetch it when you surprised me. A big strong coyote like you can probably dive in, pull it out, and eat it all by yourself.”
Coyote’s mouth watered. “Show me the cheese,” he said.
Conejo led the way to the lake, then pointed. “See?”
Coyote saw something round and yellow in the water. “That’s a big wheel of cheese,” he said. “I’m going to get it right now.”
Coyote held his breath and dove in. He swam down, down, down. But when he got to the bottom of the lake, he saw no cheese.
Where is it? he thought as he struggled to hold his breath. Then he swam up, up, up. Gasping for air, he heaved himself out of the water. To his surprise, Conejo was nowhere to be found.

Coyote stared at the cheese in the lake. How can I get it? he wondered. Coyote lifted his head to howl in frustration. At that moment, he saw the full moon. He looked again at the lake and saw the moon’s round reflection.
“That sly Conejo tricked me!” Coyote snarled.
For hours, Coyote searched high and low for Conejo. Not finding him, Coyote went to bed hungry.
As for Conejo, he was safe in his cozy burrow.

options.label: Those with great strengths will always be successful.
You can achieve more with kindness than with threats.
It's better to be happy with what you have than to attempt to get more.
Making judgments about other people or animals can be dangerous.

questions.prompt: PART A: What is the theme of "Cheese for Dinner"?

options.label: "Conejo's mind raced. 'You don't want to eat me,' he said. 'I'm just a scrawny rabbit.'" (Paragraph 5)
"'A big strong coyote like you can probably dive in, pull it out, and eat it all by yourself.'" (Paragraph 9)
"'That sly Conejo tricked me!' Coyote snarled." (Paragraph 16)
"'For hours, Coyote searched high and low for Conejo. Not finding him, Coyote went to bed hungry.'" (Paragraph 17)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the story best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: Conejo is terrified of Coyote.
Conejo wishes to befriend Coyote.
Conejo thinks Coyote can be easily fooled.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best describes what Conejo thinks about Coyote?

options.label: "Conejo's mind raced. 'You don't want to eat me,' he said. 'I'm just a scrawny rabbit.'" (Paragraph 5)

"'A big strong coyote like you can probably dive in, pull it out, and eat it all by yourself.'" (Paragraph 9)
"Gasping for air, he heaved himself out of the water. To his surprise, Conejo was nowhere to be found." (Paragraph 14)

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the fable, Coyote decides that he wants to eat the cheese over Conejo which results in him having nothing at all. How could Coyote have handled the situation differently? Describe a time when you tried to get more than you already had.
In the fable, Conejo is originally trapped by Coyote. What does he need to do in order to survive? What do you think it takes for small animals to survive against the larger animals that hunt them?

articles.title: The Day it Rained Cats and Dogs
articles.author: S. Blais
articles.description: Gavi wonders what it means to "rain cats and dogs" until pets start to fall from the sky.
articles.intro: S. Blais is a published author, blogger, and copywriter who lives outside of Washington D.C. She mis-uses the word "literally" on a regular basis. An idiom is a phrase, or group of words, that cannot be understood from the meaning of each word on its own. In this story, Gavi's Granmom tells him it is about to "rain cats and dogs."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the characters mean when they say it is going to “rain cats and dogs.”
articles.permissions: "The Day It Rained Cats and Dogs: A Literal Acres Tale" by Summer Blais. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Grown-ups say really strange things sometimes, Gavi thought as he walked home in the pouring rain. Right before he left his grandparents’ house, his Granmom had tutted at the clouds and said, “It’s going to rain cats and dogs out there. We better bundle you up.” And with that, she handed him an umbrella, made Pop-Pop dig out an old raincoat that hung to Gavi’s knees, and pushed his feet into a pair of giant fishing boots, stuffed with newspaper so he wouldn’t slip right out of them. He didn’t mind the fuss and extra clothes, even though the rain was warm. It rarely rained cold in the small town of Literal Acres. It was going to rain fast and heavy. Even though these sorts of storms only lasted a few minutes, he’d be soaked to the bone if he walked home without extra layers.

As he walked, he made sure to jump through every puddle he could find, marveling that his socks were staying completely dry inside of the huge rubber boots. He thought about the words his Granmom had used: “rain cats and dogs.” What did that even mean?
Suddenly, he heard a high-pitched yowling. He looked up to see a striped, gray cat fall slowly from the sky, land on its feet — of course it did — and scurry off into the alley between two houses.
Well. That was strange.

Gavi looked around. He was standing at the intersection of Idiom Lane and Axiom Avenue. It was where the two main roads in his little town met, so there were houses and trees all around him. He guessed the cat had leapt from a nearby tree or roof. Maybe from a wooden fence, although it didn’t seem high enough for how far the cat fell.

Out of the corner of his eye, he saw a blur of fur. He turned just in time to see a big, apricot-colored dog with curly hair land in the middle of the street. The dog seemed as surprised as Gavi felt. It looked down at itself, jumped over to the sidewalk, and shook itself off. Trying to dry itself off was completely useless in the rain, and it made Gavi laugh. The dog looked over at Gavi. It seemed to smile at him, its tail wagging, spraying more water everywhere. He trotted over to Gavi, who knelt to say hello. The dog licked Gavi’s face, which had remained dry until that moment thanks to the umbrella and raincoat hood. Gavi laughed again and tousled the wet dog’s fur. He was about to stand back up when he heard a soft bump against his umbrella.

A tiny white ball of fur had bounced off the umbrella and landed in a puddle. It was a kitten with big, blue eyes. It looked at Gavi holding the big umbrella, then at the large curly-haired dog staring back with its tongue sticking out. The kitten then glanced behind it, where the puddle was deeper and darker. It couldn’t seem to decide which choice was the worst. The dog took a few steps towards the kitten. The kitten backed up and tried curling itself into a little ball. The dog gently nuzzled the kitten and picked it up by the scruff of its neck, the way momma cats do. Then, of all things, the dog carried the kitten out of the puddle and began licking it! The kitten seemed more shocked than Gavi did.
Gavi watched the two of them for a few minutes. Then he remembered that these two animals had just shown up out of nowhere. They really fell from the sky!

When he stood back up, he realized the rain had stopped. As expected, the rainstorm had finished just as quickly as it began. Gavi shut the umbrella and lowered the giant hood of his raincoat. He noticed there were a dozen more cats, dogs, kittens, and puppies wandering around. They looked dazed and confused. All of them whining or mewling.

There was a big hound dog and next to it was a sleek Siamese cat on one side of the street. Two matching puppies were play-fighting in the wet grass. A big, orange tom cat was scratching at a tree trunk. Everywhere Gavi looked, he saw a cat or dog. He had no idea what had happened, why it had happened, or what to do. His tiny town didn’t have an animal shelter. He couldn’t just leave them here, but where could all these animals go?

Gavi sat on the curb to think. The apricot dog had decided the kitten was cleaned up enough. It sat down by Gavi, thumped its tail on the street, and licked Gavi’s nose. The feeling made Gavi giggle and want to sneeze at the same time.

Suddenly, Gavi had an idea! He ran back to his Granmom’s house. He grabbed the big, red wagon that she kept on her porch. He pulled the wagon back to the corner where all the animals were. He picked up the hound dog and put it in the wagon. He took the hound over to Old Man Jones, who lived all alone in a house a block away. He put the hound dog on the porch rocker, knocked on the door, and then ran back down the sidewalk.

This time, Gavi picked up the two puppies. They stopped tousling with each other long enough to nip at his fingers. He put them in the wagon. He walked a block in the opposite direction of Old Man Jones to the Wileys’ house. Mrs. Wiley had two ten-year-old twins and always looked tired. She had told Gavi’s mom that the twins were like “non-stop, full throttle, energy machines.” She couldn’t keep up with them! Gavi thought if they each had their own puppy to play with, run after, and train, Mrs. Wiley might get a chance to rest.

After delivering the puppies to the back door of the Wileys’ house with a knock, Gavi ran back to the intersection. It was a bit of a struggle to put the orange tom cat in the wagon, but after a few tries, he did it. He pulled the cat over to the general market. The doorbell jingled as he opened the door and gently placed the cat inside. Joe was the owner of the general store. Gavi had heard Joe complaining about mice getting into the storeroom. He thought the tom cat might be helpful.

He dropped the Siamese cat off at the home of the school librarian. He left a small dog with a pouf of black fur at the mayor’s house. He left a beagle with the baker and a tabby with the town tailor. He ran from one side of town to the other, picking the perfect pets for his friends and neighbors in his community.
The sun had come out, and Gavi was hot from running around. He felt happy that he had managed to find homes for almost all the animals. Only the apricot dog and the fluffy white kitten remained.

Gavi stared at them thoughtfully. They’d been drying off in the sun, sleeping in the grass. The dog had wrapped itself protectively around the kitten. Clearly, they were now friends, so he didn’t like the idea of splitting them up. He had an idea. He gently lifted the white kitten up and placed it carefully in the wagon. The kitten stayed asleep. All of the excitement of falling from the sky had tuckered it out. However, the apricot dog woke up and looked worriedly at Gavi.
“Don’t worry, friend,” Gavi comforted the dog. “You’ll stay in touch.”

Gavi very slowly pulled the wagon up the road to his grandparents’ house. He parked the wagon back on their porch and wrapped the big raincoat around the sleeping kitten. He gave the kitten one last pet.
“We’ll come visit all of the time,” he whispered. The kitten scrunched its tiny nose. It stretched out its paws and yawned before immediately falling back asleep.
Gavin knocked his special knock — the one he used so his grandparents knew it was him at the door — and then ran off. The dog followed.

When Gavi finally got home, he called out to his parents. “Mama? Pops? I have a new friend. Can we keep it?” He looked at the dog. It sat down and wagged its tongue. Gavi put a hand on his chin and thought for a moment before announcing, “I named it Rain.”

options.label: It is always good to be prepared.
Animals can help people with their problems.
Children should listen to the adults in their life.
You should not believe everything that people tell you.

questions.prompt: What is a theme of the story?

options.label: "He didn't mind the fuss and extra clothes, even though the rain was warm." (Paragraph 1)
"The dog licked Gavi's face, which had remained dry until that moment thanks to the umbrella and raincoat hood." (Paragraph 6)
"Gavi thought if they each had their own puppy to play with, run after, and train, Mrs. Wiley might get a chance to rest." (Paragraph 13)
"Gavin knocked his special knock — the one he used so his grandparents knew it was him at the door — and then ran off." (Paragraph 21)

questions.prompt: Which line from the text shows that Gavi is a thoughtful person?

options.label: sees rain.
is magical.
thinks hard.
feels amazed.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 2, the word "marvel" shows that Gavi —

Paragraph 12 explains why Gavi feels like getting rid of all the animals he met in paragraph 10.
Paragraph 12 explains who Gavi gives the animals to after he goes to the animal shelter in paragraph 10.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 10 relate to paragraph 12?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Gavi comes up with a creative solution to the problem of the animals that fell from the sky. Do you think that this was a good solution? How could Gavi's solution help others? What are some problems his solution might create?
Gavi thinks about the problems his community members have and tries to solve them. What is a problem in your community? What are your ideas for solving it?

Gavi thinks that pets could help people who are lonely, kids who need a playmate, and to get rid of pests. What is a problem that you can think of that a pet might help to solve? You can think of a problem you have or a problem a friend or someone in your community may have. How would a pet help solve this problem?

articles.title: Bend Me, Shape Me — Joints At Work
articles.author: India James
articles.description: There are six types of joints in the human body that help us move in different ways.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James teaches readers about a part of the human body called a joint.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the different kinds of joints.
articles.permissions: "Bend Me, Shape Me – Joints At Work" by India James. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: A gymnast raises her arm and takes a deep breath. She takes off running. Her feet pound the floor before her knees flex, jumping onto a springboard. She flies through the air. Her wrists bend as she hits the vault. Pushing hard, her shoulders shrug. Her hips bring her legs to her chest. She points her toes. She lands. Her ankles and knees bend once more. She raises her arms, cheering that she stuck the landing. Twisting, bending, she has made a perfect tumble — all thanks to her joints.

You know that your body can move in many different ways and hopefully, by now, that your bones can’t bend! It’s your joints, where two or more bones come together, that do most of the work. Your head is the only place in your body where joints don’t move. For the rest of your body, joints help you do everything from brush your teeth to sticking a complicated landing.

Sounds straightforward, right? But what you might not know is that your joints have different parts that help them work. Ligaments are stretchy material that connect bones to other bones. Have you ever used a rubber band to keep a set of items together? Ligaments keep joints together, just like rubber bands. When you move, your muscles pull on bones. Then the joint turns in the way it needs to. Both synovial (say sih-NO-vee-uhl) fluid, a thick liquid that acts as a cushion between your joints and cartilage (say CAR-till-edge), a strong flexible tissue material, make it easier for bones to slide past one another.
There are many different types of joints in the body, and a gymnast uses each kind!
When the gymnast begins to sprint down the runway, she bends her knees and her elbows. These are both hinge joints. They work just like a door swinging open and closed.

When she lands with her hands on the vault, her wrists have bent. The joints where the two bones of your lower arm meet are called condyloid (say CON-duh-loyd) joints. This type of joint allows your wrist to make a circular motion along with swinging like a hinge joint.
The gymnast then pushes off the vault and tucks her legs up to her chest. Both her shoulder and hip joints are called ball-and-socket joints . These joints allow for motion in any direction.

She lands and triumphantly straightens her back. The bones making up her spine are called vertebrae (say VER-tuh-bray). These move against each other when she bends and straightens her back. This is an example of gliding joints.
With her hands raised high in a salute, her thumbs are pressed against her other fingers. The thumb joint is an example of a saddle joint. This joint lets her thumb cross over her palm and back.
The gymnast looks from the judges to her coach and team. They’re all cheering her on! When she turned her head, she used a pivot joint at the very top of her neck. Pivot joints allow rotation.
These six types of joints give us the ability to move any way we need. Unfortunately, sometimes joints get hurt.

Have you ever had a sprain? This happens when one or more ligaments are stretched too far. You might get better by following the acronym RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. You might also wear a brace. This will keep your joint from moving. Then the ligaments will have time to heal.

Another type of joint injury is called arthritis (say arth-RYE-tis). One type of arthritis happens when cartilage is worn away through disease or over-use. Cartilage is a thin, very slippery material. When it disappears, bone rubs against bone. This is painful and makes movement difficult. There is no way to replace cartilage. Medication can help to ease pain. Sometimes surgeons will replace an entire joint with an artificial one. This often happens with older people who need hip joint or knee joint replacements.
Old or young, joints let every person move.

options.label: "The World's Best Gymnasts."
"How Broken Bones Heal."
"What Makes You Move"?
"All About Arthritis."

People often talk about muscles being important to sports. This text argues that joints are very important to sports. What are other body parts that do not get as much attention as muscles that are important to playing sports?

articles.title: Making Artificial Limbs
articles.author: India James
articles.description: India James explains how artificial limbs are made and how they have changed over time.
articles.intro: Artificial means something that is either not natural or is made by humans. Artificial limbs are human-made tools that are used to take the place of a missing limb, or body part. In this informational text, India James explains how artificial limbs are made and how they have changed over time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how artificial limbs have changed over time.
articles.permissions: "Making Artificial Limbs" by India James. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Throw the word “pirate” into Google images and you’ll find photos and cartoon illustrations of a man holding a sword, a black hat on his head, and an eye patch drawn across his scarred face. You probably also won’t have to scroll past the first page before you find the image of a pirate with a hook for a hand or a wooden stick for a lower leg.

The pirate’s wooden leg is one of the most famous examples of an artificial limb. These are devices that replace a body part. Sailors long ago often lost limbs during battles at sea. A wooden leg might replace a leg that was too damaged. That way the person could continue being a sailor.

A pirate’s wooden leg might be the most famous artificial limbs, but they weren’t the first. There are records of replacement limbs as far back as 200 BCE. In Ancient Rome, a famous general named Marcus Sergius was said to have a hand made of iron. Unfortunately for the general, his hand couldn’t do much. He wore it as decoration.

Many soldiers lost limbs from fighting in the American Civil War. Scientists and engineers began to design replacement arms and legs to do work. One design helped injured soldiers grasp things with moveable fingers. This artificial arm was made out of wood with hinges at the elbow, wrist, and fingers. Straps held the artificial arm against what was left of the soldier’s arm. Other straps wrapped around a soldier’s back to his good shoulder. By shrugging his good shoulder, a soldier could tighten these straps. As the straps tightened, they closed the wood fingers, like you do when you make a fist. This allowed a person wearing the artificial arm to be able to hold something in their hand. It was one of the first examples of a controllable artificial limb.

The artificial arm from the Civil War had a little bit of control. A wood leg was enough to let sailors stand on the deck of their ship. An iron hand looked good coming out of a shirt or coat. But these replacement limbs were uncomfortable and often caused injury on their own. Luckily, artificial limbs have come a long way since then.

There are many reasons someone might need an artificial limb today. Perhaps they were born without an arm or leg. Perhaps they were injured and had to have their arm or leg removed in surgery. Other people need an artificial limb after their original limbs become diseased. Scientists and engineers who design artificial limbs today are concerned with making them comfortable for the person and making it act like the original limb as much as possible.

One of the most important parts of making an artificial limb comfortable is making sure it fits well to the person’s body. The socket is the part that connects the artificial limb to a person’s body. If it doesn’t fit well, it may cause blisters, sores, and injury where it rubbed against skin. Today’s sockets are specially built for each person. A comfortable liner that looks something like a sock sits between a person’s skin and the artificial limb to act as a cushion. Suction is often used to hold the limb onto a person’s body rather than straps. This makes a person wearing an artificial limb more comfortable, too.

There are many different types of artificial limbs today for many different purposes. They can be steel arms, legs molded to look like a person’s original legs, or even blades that runners use to compete in races! Artificial limbs today also weigh much less than they have in centuries before. Modern artificial limbs may be built with carbon fiber. This material is stronger than steel while also being very light. Plastics are also an important part of the limb’s construction material. These types of materials are also lightweight while being strong.

The most advanced artificial limbs today work with a wearer’s body and brain to imitate how a normal limb works. Advanced artificial limbs are able to receive signals from nerves in the person’s body. This lets the person control the limb with their brain, just like they would with any other part of their body.
Thankfully, artificial limbs have improved a lot since the days of pirates and their wooden legs. We can all say “aye!” to that!

options.label: Artificial limbs are better than natural limbs at receiving signals from a person's brain.
Artificial limbs are useful for people who are missing an arm but not for those missing a leg.
Artificial limbs were developed a long time ago and have since been made better with technology.
Artificial limbs were more often used by pirates and sailors on board ships than by soldiers fighting a battle.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The invention of artificial limbs have helped people in many ways. Can you think of another invention that has made people's lives easier or better? What is it and how has it been helpful?

Artificial limbs are an example of technology that has had a positive impact on people's lives yet some technology can be viewed as being both helpful and harmful. Can you think of any examples of technology that has both a cost and a benefit? What is it and what makes it so controversial?

articles.title: Calling Dr. Tree!
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: The pain-relieving qualities of willow trees helped scientists invent aspirin.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink tells the story of a widely used medicine.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Edward Stone studied willow trees.
articles.permissions: "Calling Dr. Tree!" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Have you seen your parents complain about a headache, then reach for a bottle of pain-killing pills (adults only!)? Or maybe your grandparents suffer from joint pain and do the same? We use these medicines when we’re hurting, but one might not exist if a man hadn’t chewed on a tree centuries ago.
It’s true!

In 1758, Edward Stone took a walk one warm, summer morning through the busy town where he lived in England. He kept going until he reached some land he owned. He stopped to rest under a willow tree. While he was there, he decided to chew on a bit of willow bark (although he never explained why). The bark’s bitter taste reminded him of a medicine made from the cinchona tree.

Stone wasn’t a doctor. He worked for the church, but he was also interested in science. He knew cinchona trees grew far away in South America. The medicine that was made from them was expensive. But willow trees grew all around England. Stone wondered if the willow tree could also be used to make medicine.

He gathered up twigs and cut some bark from the tree. He dried them for several weeks on top of an oven. Then Stone ground up the twigs and bark and mixed them in a drink. He tried it himself, and he gave some to people who had aches and pains. Stone tested his willow bark mixture on 50 people over 5 years. It helped them all feel better. Stone wrote about his discovery.

Edward Stone wasn’t the first to learn how to use willow bark. People around the world had been using it for thousands of years. Willow trees grow in Africa, Europe, Asia, North America and South America. The ancient Egyptians, Greeks and Romans used medicine made from willow bark to ease pain. So did Indigenous peoples from South Africa to North America. Willow bark had also been used in England, but Stone was the first person to test it using scientific methods and write about it.

Years passed before willow bark was studied again. In the 1800s, scientists around the world were curious about how medicines worked, especially those made from plants. They had better tools than when Stone did his experiments. Some decided to study why willow bark helped people. It took years, but they figured out which part of willow bark blocks pain. The next step was to find a way to make it in a lab. They wanted a way that would be easier and cheaper than grinding up tree bark.

In 1888, the Bayer company in Germany was looking for new medicines to sell. The company hired a team of scientists. They studied how to use chemicals like the ones in willow bark. They worked on a medicine for nearly 10 years. Finally, on August 10, 1897, they figured it out. The company named the new medicine “aspirin.”

Soon the whole world was taking aspirin to help pain. It was easy to make in a lab, so it wasn’t expensive. And the medicine worked. Aspirin may be the most-used pain reliever in history. And it all started with a tree.

options.label: Aspirin in Ancient Africa.
What Are Trees Made Of?
Look Out! Poisonous Plants.
How Trees Help Our Health.

articles.title: Cobra Kite
articles.author: Mahani Zubaidy Gunnell
articles.description: Mahani Zubaidy Gunnell describes watching a kite fight as a girl.
articles.intro: Mahani Zubaidy Gunnell has written for Highlights . In this memoir piece, Gunnell describes watching kite fighting as a young girl.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the narrator likes about Cobra Kite.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: In parts of rural Malaysia, the string used to fly a kite is coated with ground glass. When a kite is flown, it is inviting others to a kite fight. The fliers usually do not know who owns the other kites. They do know that if they lose the fight, they lose their kites.

Depending on the strength and sharpness of the string, the skill of the flier, and the quality of the kite, one or more kites may be cut off in midair and fall to the ground. When this happens, folks young and old dash pell-mell to claim the fallen kites.

I was throwing paddy to the chickens when I saw the kites: Cobra Kite and Moon Kite. They said Cobra Kite had a cobra’s head on it, but you couldn’t see it from the ground. No other kite was black and had a tail so long. So when Cobra was up, you would recognize it, no problem.

Moon Kite looked like two half moons, one behind the other. My father said that was a traditional design. When he was a little boy, every house in the village had a moon kite, and after the rice harvest the kites filled the sky.

This moon kite was probably pretty in its own way. Its flier seldom showed any tricks, though some people said he was an old hand and knew everything there was to know about kites. When the wind changed, Moon Kite danced a little, but mostly it stayed quite still in the sky. To me it looked as if Moon Kite’s owner had tied it to a pole and watched it while he drank tea.
Since Cobra Kite was zig-zagging up and up, I was surprised Moon Kite didn’t leave the sky. Cobra Kite had cut three kites in that week alone.

I stopped feeding the chickens. Sure enough, Cobra was on the attack. You could tell when it flew near the other kite and waited, its tail wriggling as if it were picking energy from the sky and bringing it to the head before it attacked. Then, with a swoop, Cobra lunged toward Moon Kite, but nothing happened.
Cobra struck again. This time both kites dropped and floated.

“Yea, Cobra!” I clapped my hands and danced around the chickens. Then I saw that Moon Kite was soaring straight and steady like a rocket while Cobra was falling down, down, down like a sad, giant leaf.

I couldn’t believe my eyes. The king of kites had been beaten. I stood and stared at the sky with my mouth open. Luckily my legs took over. I bounded out of the yard and raced down the field between the row of Chinese shop houses and the village.
Cobra was over the field and falling fast. I ran faster. I prayed that the others would be slow; that whoever had seen the kite fight was far away and would not get to Cobra before I did.
As Cobra dropped, I cut into the village. Right then my trouble started. All I could see were tree-tops, roofs, and sky — no Cobra. Just a flash of its wonderful tail was all I needed.
“Please,” I prayed, “don’t let Cobra get stuck in a tree.”

Next thing I knew I was by the village headman’s poultry yard. His geese honked and hissed. They stretched their necks and shook their heads. Good thing they were in the pen. But their din was nothing compared to the shouts I began to hear. The others were close!
The first shout seemed far, but the ones that followed came from different directions and were very loud. My knees went soft. Why did I even think I could get that kite?

Suddenly, with a crack Cobra nose-dived into the headman’s pen. The gander and the three other geese flapped their wings and honked even louder. I heard someone shout “There!” and I stopped thinking. I lifted the pen’s wire gate and marched straight for Cobra. One of the geese followed me and pinched my calf. I told myself it didn’t hurt.

I grabbed Cobra and looped the bridle line around my arm until it was tight. The glassed string stung and itched. Sweat and dye made green patches on my arms and fingers. I was the happiest girl in the world.
I was surrounded by people as I left the pen.
“What is this? A child’s got the kite,” said a man who was not from our village. “What are you going to do with it? Decorate the house?”
Everyone laughed. Someone said, “The kite is as big as she is.” By then even some who had not been chasing Cobra had arrived because of the noise. A woman said, “Wah, the Cobra’s head is well drawn.”
“Yes, yes,” said another, “done by a master.”
“Come, little girl,” said a deep voice. “I’ll give you two wau kecil , and you give me the Cobra.” Later he offered three of those kites.
I held tight. I could make wau kecil kites myself, but not one like Cobra. I could not afford to buy one as big and strong either. Not even for ten wau kecil would I give up my Cobra.
Then I heard the headman’s voice. “Enough, enough,” he said. “She got it; it is hers. End of story.”
The people in the crowd slowly moved away. Some of the village children followed me home. Adil, the headman’s grandson, helped hold Cobra’s tail. His little sister ran and skipped by my side.
I took big steps and held Cobra to the side to make walking easier, but inside I was skipping and jumping and dancing all the way home.

options.label: Even the most powerful can't go undefeated forever.
Hard work and determination can lead to personal rewards.
While appearance is important, strength and size matter most.
Some things are too beautiful to consider selling.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, people aren't happy that the narrator is the one who found Cobra Kite. Do you think some of this anger has to do with the narrator's young age? How do people underestimate the capabilities of kids? Have you ever been underestimated because of your age?

In the text, the narrator is happy to have found Cobra Kite first. Why does finding Cobra Kite mean so much to the narrator? Why do other people want Cobra Kite for themselves? What is something that you own that brings you happiness? Why?

In the text, the narrator works hard to be the first to get to Cobra Kite. Do you think her success is part of her happiness in the end? Why or why not? Do you have something that is important to you because you worked hard for it?

articles.title: Let's Take a White House Tour
articles.author: John Riley and Barbara Burt
articles.description: John Riley and Barbara Burt take readers on a tour of the White House.
articles.intro: The White House is an important building in the United States. It's where the president lives. You can take a tour there!
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what a person will experience on a tour of the White House.
articles.permissions: "Let's Take a White House Tour" by John Riley and Barbara Burt, Appleseeds , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: A bit of history before we begin...
President John Adams and his wife, Abigail, moved into the White House on November 1, 1800. That makes the year 2000 the 200th anniversary of the White House.
Back when the Adamses moved in, the building was called the President’s House.

It was the largest house in America. Abigail Adams said it was twice as big as her church back home in Massachusetts. It was also cold and drafty, and some of the rooms and stairs were not finished. But the Adams family believed that the house would stand for a long time, and it has. In 200 years, 40 presidents have lived and worked in the White House at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue in Washington, D.C. It has become a famous symbol of American leadership.
Start the tour!

Today, some rooms of the White House are open to the public. More than one million people visit each year. Other rooms are private, set aside for the personal use of the president’s family and their guests. The wallpaper, furniture, and art have changed over the years. First families try to make the White House a comfortable home for as long as they live there.

Although the inside of the White House has changed, the outside has stayed the same. Offices have been built in wings on both sides, but the main part of the White House looks much as it did when President Adams arrived 200 years ago on a chilly November day.

To take a tour, you’ll need tickets. You can pick them up at the White House Visitor Center. The tours take place in the morning. After waiting in line, you’ll enter the East Wing and walk down the East Colonnade. A colonnade (a long hallway with windows or columns) extends from both sides of the main building.

On the ground floor, you can look into some rooms as you walk by. The Red Room has walls and furniture covered in apple-red fabric. The Green Room contains furniture that was made in America 200 years ago! Many people think that the Blue Room, with gold wallpaper and a blue and gold rug, is the most beautiful room in the White House. The Green Room, Blue Room, and Red Room are often used for receptions.

Upstairs, the grand East Room is often used for large gatherings. The grand piano can be used to entertain guests. The East Room can also be set for large dinners and even rock concerts! Presidents often hold their press conferences there.
When the morning tours are over, the ropes are removed and the rugs are put back into place.
The private White House
The White House belongs to all the citizens of the United States, but it’s also the home of the president’s family. Only guests of the first family get to see the private rooms on the second floor.
If you were an overnight guest, your bedroom would be on the second or third floor. You might watch a movie in the family movie theater, or bowl a game in the underground bowling alley!
The White House outdoors

The public tour doesn’t include the big “backyard” of the White House. But if you were a White House guest, you could visit the Children’s Garden, play tennis, swim in the pool, go for a jog on the jogging path, and admire the flowers and trees.
Visiting the president at work

The president’s offices are located in the West Wing. You’ll need a special appointment to visit. The president’s office is called the Oval Office because of its shape. Each president can decorate the office any way they like, but one thing never changes: the Oval Office is very impressive. Imagine how many world leaders have met with U.S. presidents here. Perhaps, someday, you’ll be the person sitting behind the desk!

options.label: People can see the private and public areas of the White House on a tour.

People learn a lot about the White House on public tours, but do not see a lot it.
People are not allowed to tour the White House when important people are staying there.

articles.title: A Way With Words
articles.subtitle: Former First Lady Michelle Obama speaks with poet Amanda Gorman.
articles.author: Michelle Obama
articles.description: Michelle Obama interviews Amanda Gorman about her poem "The Hill We Climb."
articles.intro: A poet laureate is a poet chosen by the government to write poems for special events. In this interview, former First Lady Michelle Obama talks to Amanda Gorman, America's first National Youth Poet Laureate, about her poetry.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Gorman feels about performing.
articles.permissions: "A Way With Words" by Michelle Obama, TIME for Kids Grade 3/4 , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Amanda Gorman is the country’s first National Youth Poet Laureate. She read her poem “The Hill We Climb” at the presidential inauguration. That was on January 20. Soon after, it was announced that she’d be reading at the Super Bowl. Former First Lady Michelle Obama heard Gorman speak at the inauguration. Here, she interviews the 22-year-old poet.
I was moved as I watched you read your poem. How did you prepare yourself for that moment?
Everything was moving so quickly. I didn’t get to really sit down with the text until the night before. There was a lot of the night-before performing in the mirror.
You have a mantra you recite to yourself before performing. Can you share it?

It’s inspired by Lin-Manuel Miranda’s lyrics in Moana , in “Song of the Ancestors.” That song goes: “I’m the daughter of the village chief / We’re descended from voyagers who made their way across the world.” Something like that. I wanted something that I could repeat because I get so terrified whenever I perform. So my mantra is: “I’m the daughter of Black writers who are descended from freedom fighters who broke their chains and changed the world. They call me.”
“The Hill We Climb” mentions your being a descendant of slaves. What role does poetry have to play in helping you make sense of our history?

Poetry is the lens we use to interrogate the history we stand on and the future we stand for. It’s no coincidence that at the base of the Statue of Liberty, there is a poem. Our instinct is to turn to poetry when we’re looking to communicate a spirit that is larger than ourselves.
What was your experience of the inauguration? Were you able to listen? Or were you just focused on what you came to do?

I was living in two spheres of my mind. There was the “Wow. Joe Biden’s speech was amazing. Lady Gaga just killed it.” But at the same time, 66% of my brain was dedicated to questions: “How am I going to get up to the podium without tripping? My hands are cold. Am I going to be able to flip these pages because my fingers are going numb?”
People know you as something of a prodigy — a poet who set the country on fire.

I want to highlight that this took a lifetime. Something I haven’t told anyone else is, for the past six years, whenever I’ve written a poem that I knew was going to be public or performed, I told myself, “Write the inauguration poem.” And what that meant for me is: Write a poem that is worthy of a new chapter in the country. Write something that is brave enough to be hopeful.
As you know, I have two daughters around your age. What does sisterhood mean to you and your twin?
As twins, we’re pretty dissimilar. But what bonds us isn’t our personalities. It’s our values. We’ve been raised like you, by a strong Black woman who taught us to value our ideas and our voices.
Do you have any advice for young girls who earn their way into the spotlight?

I would say to anyone who finds themselves suddenly visible and suddenly famous: Think about the big picture. You have to crown yourself with the belief that what I’m about and what I’m here for is way beyond this moment. I’m learning that I am not lightning that strikes once. I am the hurricane that comes every single year. And you can expect to see me again soon.

Gorman was only 22 when she performed at the presidential inauguration. In the context of this text, why should we value our youth? What new ideas and perspectives do youth bring that older people cannot?

Gorman shares her personal mantra and how she came up with it. Do you have a personal mantra that you tell yourself before dealing with a difficult situation? If so, what is it? If not, take some time to think about a quote or powerful line you could tell yourself when you are struggling.

Gorman shares that before performing at the Inauguration, "66%" of her brain was focused on small things like not tripping on the stage. In the context of this text, what does it mean to be brave? When we are faced with a big presentation or moment in our lives, what can we do to stay confident?

articles.title: Bound for a New Life
articles.author: Ruth Spencer Johnson
articles.description: Two children work in Jamestown, an English colony in America.
articles.intro: Jamestown in Virginia was the first permanent English colony in North America. In this short story, two children describe their experiences living and working in Jamestown.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the similarities of Robert’s and Sarah’s experiences in Jamestown.
articles.permissions: "Bound for a New Life" by Ruth Spencer Johnson, AppleSeeds , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: In 1607, 104 English men and boys arrived in North America to start a settlement. The settlement went through many struggles but continued to grow. More laborers were needed to work on the farms and in town, so the Virginia Company brought more people from England. Some went willingly to Virginia. Others were children who were taken against their will—kidnapped or tricked into service. Around 1620, the Lord Mayor of London began sending orphans from London’s streets to work in Virginia. Here are the tales two of those children might have told.
Robert’s story

By the time I was 12, both my parents were dead. Soon I was living on the streets of London, begging for food. One terrible night, two men grabbed me and told me I was going to Virginia, like it or not.

The men said that children like me were a disgrace to London, that our city was too full of orphans, begging and stealing on the streets. The planters in Virginia needed workers, so we were being rounded up and sent to Jamestown to work for the Virginia Company. That meant that I would be bound to work without pay until I turned 21. His words sent fear through my heart!
The other man explained that I would work in the tobacco fields or learn a trade. Then, after I’d served out my term, I’d be free. He said that hard work would keep me out of trouble!

When I arrived in Jamestown, I worked for the Virginia Company. “If you work hard and serve us well,” they said, “we will treat you fairly. But if you are lazy or dishonest, or you try to run away, your life will not be easy.”

Life in Virginia was even harder than I had imagined. I worked from dawn to dark, planting and weeding tobacco. My clothes became ragged; my belly was never full. I existed mostly on corn gruel, peas, and a little bread. Many other servants died from heat and disease, but somehow I survived.

Today I turn 21. At last, I’m a free man! The company has given me the 50 acres of land they promised me. I am luckier than many. The company worked me hard, but I also learned woodworking skills. Carpenters are in great demand here, so I’m sure to find employment. After nine long years, I finally have hope for my future!
Sarah’s story

In London my family was poor. One day in 1622, when I was 14, a man offered me a chance to sail to America. “America is the land of opportunity for a poor girl like you,” he claimed. “Just work a few years in Virginia to pay for your passage on the ship, and then you’ll be free to make your own life.”

I listened, thought, and finally decided to sign the papers. The agreement was called an indenture. It meant that I was bound to work for seven years in exchange for food and shelter. I was ready for a new life in Virginia.

Some opportunity! I had signed up for a chance to work my fingers to the bone in Jamestown. For seven years, I cleaned and cooked, washed and mended. I tended my master’s children and weeded his vegetable garden. His wife was a mean-spirited woman who beat me for the smallest mistake.
Now, at last, my term of service is over. I have married a man who was also indentured. Now we have our own farm near Jamestown. I guess America is a land of opportunity after all!

options.label: Many children who went to work in America tried to return to their home country.
Children were better paid for their services in American than in their home countries.
Some children had to work hard for no money before they were free to live in America.
Children did not find the opportunities they were promised when they came to America.

articles.title: Mo of the Mudflat
articles.author: Nora Nickum
articles.description: Disappointed after moving to a new home, a girl learns to appreciate the new environment's beauty.
articles.intro: A mudflat is a piece of land by the shore that is covered by water at high tide and uncovered at low tide. In this story, a girl named Salma has just moved to a new home on the mudflat.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how meeting Mo changes Salma’s feelings about her new home.
articles.permissions: "Mo of the Mudflat" by Nora Nickum, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Salma picked her way along the shore, holding her nose against its rotten-egg smell. Her flip-flops made a loud squelching sound. As her foot slid on slimy green goo, she flailed her arms to steady herself, but landed with a splat.

With a groan, Salma cupped her dirty hands around her face, making sure not to get mud in her hair. She squinted until the scene almost resembled a paradise — like the one she pictured when her mom said they would get to live by the water. A paradise with rippling waves and sand. Seashells and dolphins. A magical place to help counteract the sadness of leaving her friends and her home in the city.
But when she dropped her hands, she again saw the gloomy warehouses and factories surrounding the bay.
She also saw someone coming.
Someone who was hopping easily along the wet rocks. Someone who was hollering at Salma.
“Do you see the sea otter?”
Salma peered at the water. She had seen a sea otter in an aquarium once, somersaulting and rubbing its fur. It was much fuzzier and cuter than anything she could see here.
“You mean that thing?” Salma asked, pointing. “That’s just an old log.”
“That’s what I used to think.” The kid shrugged and skipped past, not seeming to notice the muck or the stench.
Salma wished her old friends were here. They would have grumbled with Salma about the ugly beach and the mud between their toes, and then made her laugh.

She watched the kid bound away down the shore, sometimes stopping to peer at the muddy stones and squeal with glee. Salma couldn't imagine getting that excited about anything in this place. She trudged back home, ready to change into clean clothes and spend the rest of the day on the couch.

The next morning Salma’s mom left early for work. Salma found a scribbled note with breakfast ideas on the kitchen counter and cooked herself a cheese omelet. Then she looked around at everything that still needed to be unpacked and sighed. She rinsed yesterday’s muck off her flip-flops and grabbed her mom’s binoculars from one of the moving boxes. Then she walked to the shore.

The same kid was already there, hopping around and whistling. Salma paused and considered returning home. She wasn’t sure she could handle that happy, positive energy today. But the kid spotted her on the next twirl and waved.
“Check out the octopus. Did you know they can change color?”
Salma rolled her eyes. “All I see is mud and weeds. I don’t think much can actually live here.”
“You just need to spend more time. And really look. Then you'll see like me — Mudflat Mo.”
“Well, you can call me Skeptical Salma.”

Salma raised the binoculars to her eyes and scanned the water. Definitely no octopuses in sight. Didn’t octopuses live down in caves, anyway? That’s what the “Octopus's Garden” song said. Her mom used to sing that when Salma was little.
“Did you see that?” Mo said, suddenly standing by her elbow. “A seal just popped its head up. I think it wants to know why you showed up this week. We’ve never seen you here before.”
“There’s no seal,” insisted Salma. “Isn’t that called a buoy?”
“Suit yourself,” said Mo. “Why are you here, anyway?”
“My mom got a job,” Salma mumbled. “In one of those factories.”
“Makes sense. My dad works in a warehouse over there, too,” said Mo with a shrug.
Salma felt her chest relax, just a little. So her mom wasn’t the only one who worked in those ugly buildings. Maybe they’d fit in, eventually.
“Hey, you should come earlier tomorrow,” said Mo. “There’s even more to see at low tide.”
More grass and goop, Salma figured. But she nodded. Worth a try.
“Eight o’clock,” said Mo. “Don’t be late.”
The next morning Salma pushed aside her mom’s note with fancy breakfast ideas and grabbed a banana for the walk to the shore. When she arrived, Mo handed her a pair of old boots.
“Dress like you live here,” said Mo. “You do now, right?”
Salma gave a half-smile. “I guess so. Don’t have much choice.” She kicked off her flip-flops and tugged on the boots. They were enormous, but they would work. “Now what?”
“The crabs are easiest to find,” said Mo, lifting a rock. Mo caught one and dropped it in Salma’s hand. The tiny crab, no bigger than her fingernail, skittered sideways across her wrist.
“It tickles!” Salma shrieked. She dropped her hand to the mud and let the crab scurry back under a rock.
Salma followed as Mo looked in cracks and puddles. Mo pointed out a shy hermit crab, a little fish, and pointy hat-shaped shells called limpets.

Salma wandered to find something herself. There were rocks with barnacles, closed up until the tide came back in. There was slick green stuff that Mo said was algae — probably the same stuff that Salma had slipped on when she went splat in the mud the first day. And a bright blue bottle cap. Salma picked it up between two fingers and showed it to Mo.
“Yeah, I’m afraid there’s litter here, too,” said Mo. “It’s not all cute critters.”
Salma raised her eyebrows. Crabs and barnacles were kind of cool. But cute?
She pulled an old snack bag out of her pocket and stashed the bottle cap inside. Mo reached for it and added a broken plastic spoon and a bit of nylon rope.

Stuffing the bag back into her pocket, Salma straightened. Her back was stiff after so much time hunching over to look at the mud. She glanced toward the water, which was rippling lightly in the breeze. “We haven’t seen anything big,” she said. “No otters, no octopuses, no seals. You made those up, right? I knew it.”
Mo smiled and started tromping down the beach. Salma hurried after. When she caught up, Mo pointed. “Look there.”
“I see some lumps of gray rock,” said Salma. “Nothing that’s alive.”
“Look again. Take your time.”
Salma’s eyes widened. “Wait. One of those rocks moved!”
Mo laughed. “Now you’ve met a seal pup.”
Salma’s heart leaped. There were baby seals here!
“We can’t get too close,” Mo said. “But we could check on them again tomorrow. Come on, let’s head back.”
On the way, Salma kept stopping to scan the waves and the rocks. What else had she missed?
In a glint of reflected sunlight, a tiny fin popped up out of the waves, then slid back under as another came up right behind.
“Oh!” Salma shouted. She squinted to make sure she was right. The fins appeared again. “Are those dolphins?”
“Close — they’re porpoises. Nice find!” said Mo. “I might have to start calling you Sharp-Eyed Salma.”
Salma giggled and returned Mo's high-five. “That’s not the worst nickname ever. Better than Skeptical Salma, at least.”
They watched the porpoises until they disappeared. Then Mo rinsed her hands in a pool of salt water and headed for the road. “A solid morning of exploring. Going home?”
Salma pursed her lips. “I think I’ll stick around until I find the otter and the octopus.” She paused. “And a whale!”
“That would be unreal,” said Mo. “I’ve never seen a whale here.”

“It’s about time, then,” said Salma. She stared down at her boots. Then she yanked them off. She dug her bare toes into the muck and squealed. It was slimy and cool and wet — disgusting and somehow satisfying at the same time.
Mo grinned and snatched the boots. “You can wear your own flip-flops when you’re done there.”
Salma smiled. She took a deep breath. The air smelled like rotten eggs, and salt, and magic.
“Come on, Mudflat Mo. There’s got to be a whale around here somewhere. What do you say we spend a bit more time, and really look?”

options.label: When we closely look at nature we can find surprises we never thought possible.
Before judging someone you should understand what they have been through.
Keeping in touch with old friends makes moving to a new place easier.
Helping to care for animals can help us take care of ourselves.

discussionQuestions.prompt: At first, Salma does not like her new home. Have you ever moved to a new place? How did you feel about moving? What, if anything, helped you adjust to your new home?

At the end of the story, Salma realizes that there is a lot to be discovered in the mudflat if she "really looks." What is a place or thing that you find boring or gross? How could really looking at it closely help you discover something beautiful or interesting about it?

Mo helps Salma start to enjoy her new home. Thinking about this text, what makes a good friend? What do you like to do with your good friends? How do you know when you have met someone who could be a good friend? Have you ever helped a friend think about something in a new and positive way?

articles.title: No Peanut Butter in France
articles.author: Joan Strauss
articles.description: American twins who have just moved to France go on a search for their favorite food.
articles.intro: France is a country in Europe that is known for its beauty and food. In this story, two American twins, Lauren and Lee, move to France with their family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the details that explain the setting.
articles.permissions: "No Peanut Butter in France" by Joan Strauss, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Lauren and Lee knew living in France was going to be different. They just didn’t know it was going to be that different.
The twins had learned French so they could speak with the French people, but everybody spoke so fast!

The food was different, too. Their mom was excited to try cooking some new French recipes. One night she made a cheese and egg pie, called quiche , for dinner. Lauren and Lee learned to pronounce it “keesh,” But they weren’t sure they liked it.
“What’s the use of living in France if we don’t try the food?” their dad asked.
“I’ll try it,” Lee promised, “but I miss peanut butter sandwiches in my school lunch.”
“I’ll be glad to make peanut butter sandwiches if I can buy peanut butter,” their mom said, “but I haven’t seen any in the stores.”

The next time their mom went grocery shopping at the supermarché , the supermarket, Lauren and Lee went with her, but they could not find peanut butter. Lee went to the manager. “ Avez-vous . . .” he started to say in his most careful French. He had no idea what peanut butter would be in French, so he finished up in English.

“ Ah, oui .” The manager smiled. He led the twins to a shelf full of jars of something brown. It didn’t look like peanut butter. Lauren showed a jar to her mom. “It’s not peanut butter, it’s hazelnut butter,” her mom said. “Let’s give it a try.”

It was good, but it didn’t taste like peanut butter. At lunch period, Lee gave a bite of his sandwich to his new friend, Jacques. “I know something you’ll like better than that,” Jacques said. “Ask your mother if you and Lauren can go with me after school tomorrow.”

Jacques took the twins to a bakery shop around the corner from school. When they opened the door, a wonderful fragrance of freshly baked bread and cake came out to meet them. Warm, yeasty, and sugary — so good, Lauren thought she could almost bite into the smell alone.
“This is the boulangerie , the bakery, where I get my after-school snack,” Jacques explained.
Past the baskets of crusty baguettes and rolls, the cases of eclairs and fruit tarts and decorated cakes, they followed Jacques to the counter. “Three pains au chocolat , if you please,” he asked.
The lady behind the counter handed a round, flaky pastry filled with chocolate to each of them. Lee took a bite.
“It’s great,” he agreed. “I wonder if they could make it with peanut butter.”
Pointing to his pastry, he asked the lady, “Peanut butter?”
“ Mais, non! ” She laughed. “No peanut buttair! Chocolat ! No peanut buttair in France.”
Could that be true? No peanut butter in France?
Lauren and Lee went with their father to the epicerie , the little grocery store, to buy lettuce for a salad. “Do you sell peanut butter?” Lauren asked.
“No peanut butter,” the store owner replied.
Lee stopped at the boucherie to look for peanut butter. “That’s silly!” Lauren said. “You won’t find peanut butter at a butcher shop!”
She went to the poisonnerie to see if, by chance, they might have peanut butter.
“Now who’s silly?” Lee demanded. “Who ever heard of a fish shop that sold peanut butter?”
One of the twins’ favorite places was the crêperie . The crêperie served crêpes , delicious French pancakes. But crêpes weren’t served with peanut butter either.

When they had been in France a month, their parents took them out to dinner at a big restaurant. Lee read the menu hopefully. He saw nothing that looked like peanut butter, but he asked the waiter, anyway.
“ Non, Monsieur , but we have a fine ragout .” Ragout, Lee discovered, was a very good mixture of vegetables and meat. He even learned to say it, “ragoo.”
One day, Lauren and Lee were walking with their mom through the grand magasin , the big department store. And there, in the section for foreign foods, was peanut butter.
“Peanut butter is not a foreign food!” Lee protested. “It is in France,” Lauren pointed out. “It’s an American food.”
“Then let’s buy a jar for Jacques, too,” Lee suggested. “I hope he likes it as much as we like pains au chocolat .”
“And as much as we like crêpes,” Lauren added.
“And ragout and baguettes,” Lee said. “But I still love peanut butter. Maybe Mom should buy three jars — one for Jacques, and two for us!”

discussionQuestions.prompt: Lee and Lauren have to make a lot of changes when they move to France: there is a new language, new foods, and even the stores are different! If you had to move to a new country, what do you think would be the hardest change for you? Why?

Lee and Lauren miss peanut butter when they are in France because it reminds them of America. What is a food that is special to you? How would you feel if you could not find or eat this food anymore? Why?
Imagine that you had to move far away from the place that you live now. If you could bring one thing with you that reminded you of home, what would it be? Why? How does it remind you of home?
There are many different cuisines, or types of foods: French, American, Mexican, Ethiopian, etc. Which is your favorite? Why?

articles.title: Henrietta Applebaum is Upside Down
articles.author: Hannah Hoffman
articles.description: A young girl learns that some days are upside-down days and that tomorrow will be better.
articles.intro: In this story, a young girl named Henrietta Applebaum wakes up with a very big problem.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Henrietta’s thoughts about being upside down.
articles.permissions: "Henrietta Applebaum is Upside Down" by Hannah Hoffman, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: One morning, Henrietta Applebaum woke up and found herself on the ceiling.
"Are we dreaming?" she asked her doll Abigail. Abigail said nothing.
Over her head, Henrietta could see her bed and nightstand and bookshelf and toys. Everything looked normal, except she was on the ceiling.
She jumped and jumped, but every time she landed right back on the ceiling. She could skip and dance and cartwheel. But she couldn't get down.
"Get ready, Abigail!" Henrietta cried as she launched the doll over her head. But Abigail bumped the floor above and then plopped right back into her lap.
"Mom!" Henrietta yelled. "Mom?"
But her mother had already left for work for the day.

Henrietta tucked Abigail into the waistband of her pajamas and climbed up her bookshelf. Once she reached the top, Henrietta could see dust bunnies hiding in the corners by the back legs of the shelf. But she was still upside down. She climbed back down to the ceiling and sighed.
Henrietta's little brother, Oscar, popped his head in the door. "Dad said breakfast is ready."
"I can't come," Henrietta said.
Oscar looked around the room in confusion. "Where are you?"
"We're up here," she replied, waving Abigail like a flag.
Oscar lifted his head and blinked. "Get down from there!"
"I can't!" Henrietta insisted.
Oscar rolled his eyes. "You're always playing around. You are such a show-off. I'm telling Dad."
"I'm not a show-off! I'm stuck up here!"
Henrietta wasn't a show-off.
Henrietta's father came to the door. "Get down from there," he demanded.
"I can't!" Henrietta insisted.
Her father spoke low and slow. "Henrietta, stop acting like a silly goose, and come down from there."
Henrietta stomped her foot. "I'm not a goose! I'm stuck up here!"
Henrietta wasn't a goose.
"I don't have time for this today," ​​her father muttered and left.
Henrietta looked at Abigail. Abigail said nothing.
After a while, Henrietta stepped through her bedroom door into the hall. She was careful not to trip over the hall light's glass globe. In the kitchen, Grandma cleaned up the breakfast dishes.
"Grandma?" Henrietta said. "Can I have a muffin?"
Grandma spoke without looking at Henrietta. "Get down from there."
"I can't!" Henrietta insisted.
Grandma looked up and shook a sudsy wooden spoon at Henrietta. "No breakfast until you stop being so difficult."
Henrietta frowned. "I'm not difficult. I'm stuck up here!"
Henrietta wasn't difficult.
Henrietta walked through the house, exploring every room, except Daddy's office. Henrietta spied her favorite noisy game at the top of the TV cabinet in the living room, where Grandma had hidden it.
"Ha! I can reach it now," Henrietta said. She looked out through the living room windows into the backyard. If she was on the ceiling and she stepped outside, would she fall up into the sky?
"Better not risk it," she told Abigail. Abigail said nothing.
When it was lunchtime, Oscar came to the playroom with two paper bags. He tossed one up toward the ceiling, and Henrietta caught it.
"It's a pack lunch from Grandma," he said.
Henrietta bit into her baloney sandwich. The crumbs landed in Oscar's hair.
"Hey!" he yelled.
Henrietta apologized. She was much more careful with her bottle of juice.
It was nearly dinnertime when Henrietta's mother came home. Henrietta was worried about what her mother would say. Would she think Henrietta was being a show-off, or silly, or difficult?
Henrietta's mother walked into her bedroom and looked around. "My whole day has been upside down!" she said.
"You too?" Henrietta asked.

That night, Henrietta's mother climbed a ladder to tuck her in for the night. She climbed to the very top to kiss Henrietta on the head." Some days are just upside-down days," her mother said. Henrietta looked at Abigail. Abigail said nothing.
"We'll dream downside-up dreams!" Henrietta said and kissed Abigail on the head. "Tomorrow will be a better day."

options.label: Parents should be more caring towards their children.
Some days do not go as planned and that is okay.
You must try hard to fix a difficult problem.
It is not funny to play tricks on your family.

articles.title: Sybil Ludington
articles.author: Jesse Sullivan
articles.description: Sybil Ludington, a sixteen year old girl, alerted Continental troops during the American Revolution.
articles.intro: Jesse Sullivan is the author of the children's book Spectacular Stories for Curious Kids: A Fascinating Collection of True Stories to Inspire & Amaze Young Readers . During the American Revolution, the 13 American colonies fought a war with Great Britain to win their independence. In this excerpt from the book, Sullivan shares the story of Sybil Ludington, a girl who helped her community during the American Revolution.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s point of view about Sybil Ludington.
articles.permissions: "Sybil Ludington" originally titled as "The Girl 'Paul Revere" from Spectacular Stories for Curious Kids by Jesse Sullivan. Copyright (c) August 20, 2021, Rascal Face Media LLC. Used with permission by Rascal Face Media LLC.

blocks.text: You’ve no doubt heard of Paul Revere heroically riding at midnight to let everyone know that the British were coming. But have you heard of the 16-year-old girl who was also riding at night? Sybil Ludington made her own heroic night ride, only she rode sidesaddle and rode much much further than Revere did.

Young Sybil lived with her family in Dutchess County, New York and was the oldest of twelve children. Her father was Colonel Henry Ludington who had fought in the French Indian War and was a highly respected military man. He was commander of the 7th Regiment that belonged to the Dutchess County Militia. This was a volunteer group of about 400 local men who had banded together to fight during the days of the Revolutionary War.

On April 25, 1777, British Commander General William Tryon landed with about 2000 men. They began to take over nearby Danbury, Connecticut, searching for the Continental Army supply storage and burning stores and houses. A messenger rider was sent to dispatch the message of the British attack to nearby towns. He arrived at the Ludington house around 9pm, too exhausted to go any further.

Colonel Ludington needed to gather his troops to prepare an attack. The problem was that most of his soldiers had already returned to their nearby homes to assist with spring planting. With the Colonel needing to plan his strategy, young Sybil dutifully took on the mission of rallying the troops and warning nearby neighbors.

Sixteen year old Sybil on top of Star (her horse), set out into the dark, rainy night. They rode through nearby farms and many dark desolate roads. She had a stick that she used to urge her horse forward and to tap on doors and windows.

“The British are burning Danbury. Muster at Ludington’s at daybreak!” she was heard shouting on her all-night ride. When she arrived home at sun-up that next morning, Sybil had covered over 40 miles. Nearly all of the 400 men had arrived at her father’s farm and were prepared to march.
Although they were too late to save Danbury, the 7th Regiment was responsible for helping to drive the British troops back south towards the Long Island Sound where their ships were waiting.

Sybil was praised by her friends and neighbors as well as by the one and only, General George Washington. That was a great honor. But for the most part, few people know about the 16-year-old’s heroic ride. The Daughters of the American Revolution funded a statue of Sybil and Star in 1961 in Carmel, New York.

options.label: The Girl Who Saved George Washington

articles.title: Sleeping with the Baby
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker describes their baby's difficulty sleeping through the night.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson is a writer of books for both children and adults. In this poem, a speaker describes their baby and its troubles sleeping through the night.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker feels about their baby.
articles.permissions: From Black Stars in a White Night Sky by JonArno Lawson. Copyright © 2008 by JonArno Lawson. Published by WordSong, an imprint of Boyds Mills Press. Used by permission.

blocks.text: I’m sleeping with the baby in the day
because the baby will not sleep at night.
He’s still too small to smile, or to play,
he’s still too small to hold his head upright.
He purses up his lips in little o’s,
he nurses bit by bit, and then he goes
and grows and grows and grows and grows and grows.
How big he’ll finally be, nobody knows.
For sore eyes he’s a very lovely sight.
His brows are worried, but his face is bright.
I love him and his toothless hungry bite,
although he keeps us up, night after night.
Night after night, oh God, night after night.

options.label: It is both exciting and exhausting to have a new baby.
Parents are easily frustrated with their new babies when they won't sleep.
New parents find that babies don't take as much work as they originally believed.
Babies do not need as much sleep as their parents do.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes a theme of the poem?

"For sore eyes he's a very lovely sight. / His brows are worried, but his face is bright." (Lines 9-10)
"I love him and his toothless hungry bite, / although he keeps us up, night after night." (Lines 11-12)

It shows how much the speaker wants a break from the baby.
