articles.title: Stray
articles.author: Cynthia Rylant
articles.description: In this short story, an abandoned dog is discovered by a young girl.
articles.intro: In this short story, Doris finds a stray dog and tries to convince her family to keep it.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the lessons that we can learn from Doris and the stray dog she finds.
articles.permissions: "Stray" from EVERY LIVING THING by Cynthia Rylant. Text copyright © 1985 by Cynthia Rylant. Reprinted with the permission of Atheneum Books for Young Readers, an imprint of Simon & Schuster Children's Publishing Division. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: In January, a puppy wandered onto the property of Mr. Amos Lacey and his wife, Mamie, and their daughter, Doris. Icicles hung three feet or more from the eaves of houses, snowdrifts swallowed up automobiles and the birds were so fluffed up they looked comic.
The puppy had been abandoned, and it made its way down the road toward the Laceys’ small house, its ears tucked, its tail between its legs, shivering.
Doris, whose school had been called off because of the snow, was out shoveling the cinderblock front steps when she spotted the pup on the road. She set down the shovel.
“Hey! Come on!” she called.
The puppy stopped in the road, wagging its tail timidly, trembling with shyness and cold. Doris trudged through the yard, went up the shoveled drive and met the dog.
“Come on, Pooch.”
“Where did that come from?” Mrs. Lacey asked as soon as Doris put the dog down in the kitchen.
Mr. Lacey was at the table, cleaning his fingernails with his pocketknife. The snow was keeping him home from his job at the warehouse.
“I don’t know where it came from,” he said mildly, “but I know for sure where it’s going.” Doris hugged the puppy hard against her. She said nothing.

Because the roads would be too bad for travel for many days, Mr. Lacey couldn’t get out to take the puppy to the pound in the city right away. He agreed to let it sleep in the basement while Mrs. Lacey grudgingly let Doris feed it table scraps. The woman was sensitive about throwing out food. By the looks of it, Doris figured the puppy was about six months old, and on its way to being a big dog. She thought it might have some shepherd in it.

Four days passed and the puppy did not complain. It never cried in the night or howled at the wind. It didn’t tear up everything in the basement. It wouldn’t even follow Doris up the basement steps unless it was invited.
It was a good dog.

Several times Doris had opened the door in the kitchen that led to the basement and the puppy had been there, all stretched out, on the top step. Doris knew it had wanted some company and that it had lain against the door, listening to the talk in the kitchen, smelling the food, being a part of things. It always wagged its tail, eyes all sleepy, when she found it there.

Even after a week had gone by, Doris didn’t name the dog. She knew her parents wouldn’t let her keep it, that her father made so little money any pets were out of the question, and that the pup would definitely go to the pound when the weather cleared.
Still, she tried talking to them about the dog at dinner one night.
“She’s a good dog, isn’t she?” Doris said, hoping one of them would agree with her.
Her parents glanced at each other and went on eating.
“She’s not much trouble,” Doris added. “I like her.” She smiled at them, but they continued to ignore her.
“I figure she’s real smart,” Doris said to her mother. “I could teach her things.”
Mrs. Lacey just shook her head and stuffed a forkful of sweet potato in her mouth. Doris fell silent, praying the weather would never clear.
But on Saturday, nine days after the dog had arrived, the sun was shining and the roads were plowed. Mr. Lacey opened up the trunk of his car and came into the house.

Doris was sitting alone in the living room, hugging a pillow and rocking back and forth on the edge of a chair. She was trying not to cry but she was not strong enough. Her face was wet and red, her eyes full of distress.
Mrs. Lacey looked into the room from the doorway.
“Mama,” Doris said in a small voice. “Please.”
Mrs. Lacey shook her head, “You know we can’t afford a dog, Doris. You try to act more grownup about this."
Doris pressed her face into the pillow.
Outside, she heard the trunk of the car slam shut, one of the doors open and close. The old engine cough and choke and finally start up.
“Daddy,” she whispered. “Please.”

She heard the car travel down the road, and, though it was early afternoon, she could do nothing but go to her bed. She cried herself to sleep, and her dreams were full of searching and searching for things lost.
It was nearly night when she finally woke up. Lying there, like stone, still exhausted, she wondered if she would ever in her life have anything. She stared at the wall for a while.

But she started feeling hungry, and she knew she’d have to make herself get out of bed and eat some dinner. She wanted not to go into the kitchen, past the basement door. She wanted not to face her parents.
But she rose up heavily.
Her parents were sitting at the table, dinner over, drinking coffee. They looked at her when she came in, but she kept her head down. No one spoke.
Doris made herself a glass of powdered milk and drank it all down. Then she picked up a cold biscuit and started out of the room.
“You’d better feed that mutt before it dies of starvation,” Mr. Lacey said.
Doris turned around.
“What?”
“I said, you’d better feed your dog. I figure it’s looking for you.”
Doris put her hand to her mouth.
“You didn’t take her?” she asked.

“Oh, I took her all right.” her father answered. “Worst looking place I’ve ever seen. Ten dogs to a cage. Smell was enough to knock you down. And they give an animal six days to live. Then they kill it with some kind of a shot.”
Doris stared at her father.
“l wouldn’t leave an ant in that place,” he said. “So I brought the dog back.”
Mrs. Lacey was smiling at him and shaking her head as if she would never, ever, understand him.
Mr. Lacey sipped his coffee.
“Well,” he said, “are you going to feed it or not?”

options.label: Dogs can bring people closer together.

Doris is afraid of the stray dog she finds, but her parents decide that they want the dog.
Doris finds a dog in her yard, but is unable to convince her family that she should keep it.

articles.title: Looking Ahead
articles.author: Kathiann M. Kowalski
articles.description: Kathiann M. Kowalski interviews a Washington, D.C. city planner.
articles.intro: Washington, D.C. is the capital of the United States. In this text, Kathiann M. Kowalski interviews a city planner for the nation's capital.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what a Washington, D.C. city planner does.
articles.permissions: "Looking Ahead" by Kathiann M. Kowalski, Cobblestone , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Washington, D.C., is not just the capital of the United States. The city is also home to about 650,000 people. Those people need homes, transportation, police protection, water, sewer services, and more. People who work in and visit Washington use many city services, too. Those things don’t happen by themselves. They take planning. To learn more, we talked with Ellen McCarthy, acting director of the Office of Planning for Washington, D.C.
For starters, what do city planners do?
City planners look to the future and what kinds of changes are happening. [They work] to make those changes be positive for the people who live there, work there, or visit there.
What’s special about planning for Washington, D.C.?

We have the normal local planning issues — new people moving in, how to keep housing affordable, how to manage the traffic, how to provide the right kinds of parks and open spaces, [and] making sure we have enough schools. But on top of that, we have special responsibilities that come from being the nation’s capital.
How can the federal government affect your ability to plan?
The federal government can end up making rules such as how tall our buildings are — decisions that normally are made by local governments.
Congress also has to approve our budget — even the funds that are raised from local tax dollars. We can’t spend the money until Congress appropriates it.
How would you rate Washington as a capital city?

I think that we always compare ourselves to world capitals like London and Paris. We may not quite be there yet. But we have really come such a long way in the last 20 years. When people visit Washington now, they see a downtown that is bustling with people and with museums, art galleries, theaters, restaurants, and sidewalk cafes. And the public space is well cared for.
Is Washington’s population growing?
We are gaining 1,100 residents a month. People actually want to come here now — not just to be tourists visiting the White House and the Capitol — but to come and live here.
Many newcomers are between 25 and 34 years old. What draws them to D.C.?
They’re young people out of college. They find Washington to be a place with lots of interesting jobs, lots of other people their age, and lots of things to do.
Are children’s numbers growing?

We’ve got a baby boom going on. We’ve had more births in the last few years than we had for decades before that. We are one of the only cities that offer free pre-K to 3- and 4-year old children guaranteed.
What are Washington’s big challenges for the next 10 or 20 years?
Transportation and affordable housing are probably our two biggest challenges. And, of course, economic development, so that we can provide jobs for everyone in the city as well.
How is Washington adding homes?

We’ve been concerned about trying not to displace people. So we encouraged building downtown and along the Anacostia waterfront and an area north of downtown. All of these places had a lot of surface parking lots and were under-used. We’ve been able to build thousands and thousands of new housing units. A lot of young professionals are going there and to neighborhoods around our Metro stations where we’ve been able to build apartment buildings that fit more people.
Why is public transportation important?
People can come and live, and they don’t have to have a car. They don’t have to add to traffic congestion and pollution. They can just hop onto the Metro, our subway system.
What might Washington’s founding fathers think if they saw the city today?

Maybe Pierre L’Enfant, who planned the city in 1791, wouldn’t be surprised. L’Enfant had a great vision for D.C. But I think Washington and Jefferson would be surprised now. They’d be surprised by how Washington grew, [yet] basic elements of the L’Enfant plan have been retained.
Does Washington aim to keep that plan?
We have a strong historic preservation law. If someone wants to close a street that had been laid out on the L’Enfant plan, that’s generally not allowed.
We’ve stayed true to the spirit and the framework that was set out by the L’Enfant plan. But we’ve been able to grow and have a population beyond what they ever thought we would have.
What else should young people know?

Every planning department in every city wants to have participation from their citizens. Read the newspaper. See what things are suggested. That’s really how your city is going to look over the next 20 or 30 years. And think about careers in planning. Be a direct part of making all these things happen.

options.label: City planners are making plans for Washington, D.C.'s future based mainly on citizens' input.

City planners consider how Washington, D.C. is changing and growing to better plan for the future.
City planners in Washington, D.C. have little to do because not many changes can be made to the historic city.

articles.title: Two Destinies
articles.author: B.C. Bond
articles.description: A young prince, who would later become the Buddha, faces a decision about what to do with his life.
articles.intro: A destiny is what will happen to someone or something in the future because of luck or a power beyond human control. This legend tells the story of Buddha when he was still a young prince named Siddhartha. Siddhartha must decide between the two destinies before him.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on King Suddhodana’s words.
articles.permissions: "Two Destinies" by B.C. Bond, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Moonlight glittered on the palace pond like silver carp. Hearing his father approach, twelve-year-old Siddhartha realized the hour was late. The moon had reached its zenith. Tossing a rock into the pond, he shattered its glassy surface. Dark ripples spread across the water, ending the evening's spell.
“Why are you up, my son?” King Suddhodana recognized the troubled look in his son's dark, unwavering eyes.
“Father, why can't I explore outside the palace?” Frustration was unmistakable in Siddhartha's reply.
The king frowned, knowing his son was questioning traditional rules that had long guided court life.
“If I'm to be a good king, I should see how our subjects live,” Siddhartha said.

“You're too young to venture into the harshness of the world,” the king replied. He pointed toward the garden. “For now, enjoy the beauty within our palace walls. See how everything is ready for tomorrow's celebration? There will be singers, royal musicians, and a magician to entertain you.”
“Why do you give me three of everything? I have a winter palace, one for summer, and another for rainy season. I have three servants. Even three lotus ponds in my garden.”

“Asita, our soothsayer, predicted three pillars would support your life path. The first pillar is devotion to duty. The second, wisdom. And third, love, shared by all you serve. Three symbolizes your destiny. Now, isn't the garden magnificent?”

Siddhartha remained silent. The rainbow streamers decorating the garden reminded him of his father's wish to distract him from the outside world. Marvelous smells drifting from the palace kitchen — of chicken stewing in coconut milk, along with endless breads, cakes, and sweet pickled fish — teased his appetite. Tomorrow's celebration would be perfect, yet Siddhartha felt uneasy.

King Suddhodana gazed with worry at his strange son. Other children would be thrilled to live a prince's life, but not Siddhartha. With a sigh the king clapped his hands and commanded three lemon-robed servants to assist Siddhartha to bed.
“May I go to the plowing ceremony in the village?” Siddhartha begged.
“Children are not allowed,” the king said, shaking his head firmly. “Not even a prince.” He failed to hear his son's sigh as they parted.
After the servants withdrew, a shadow slipped into Siddhartha's room. “My prince, I have need of you,” a familiar voice whispered.
“Channa, what is it?” Siddhartha leaped from his bed.
“It's been weeks since you visited the stables. Kanthaka is sick.”
“Why wasn't I told?”
“It is the king's wish that your eyes not gaze upon sickness or suffering.” Channa swallowed nervously. “If His Majesty discovers I...”
“My father has no right to hide Kanthaka's illness from me.” Siddhartha's face turned scarlet. “Take me to the stables at once, Channa.”

“If we are quiet, we can get past the guards,” Channa whispered, opening the door a crack. Three guards in red and blue kasi cloth sat in a corner, arguing over a game of dice. Hugging the shadows, Channa and Siddhartha slipped away unnoticed.
At Kanthaka's stall, the prince knelt beside the black stallion. The great horse lay still, eyes closed, breathing shallowly. His nostrils quivered at Siddhartha's smell.
“Can you do nothing, Channa?”
“Only you can cure Kanthaka, my prince.”
“What can I do that the greatest horse master in our kingdom can't do?”
“There are stronger medicines than man-mixed herbs.”
“What medicine is that?” Siddhartha wondered aloud.
“Discover it for yourself, my prince,” Channa said, leaving Siddhartha's side.

“Forgive me, Kanthaka,” Siddhartha whispered, as he gently stroked the horse's neck. Recognizing his master's touch, the horse nickered weakly. “While daydreaming about the world outside, I have neglected my closest companion. How could I do this?”
Siddhartha remained at the horse's side through the night, until sounds of morning stirred in the palace.
“Remember our rides in the park? We will go again. Forgive me,” Siddhartha sobbed. “I promise I'll never again forget my duty to you.”
Kanthaka's eyes fluttered. Snorting softly, Kanthaka kicked twice, shook himself, then stumbled to his feet.
“Channa, come! Look! Kanthaka is standing.”
“It seems even animals need more than food and shelter to live, my prince,” Channa said when he returned.
“I know it now,” Siddhartha said, lowering his head in guilt. “If only Father understood the same is true of me.”
“Perhaps I do,” a deep voice spoke from the stable shadows. “You have learned an important lesson, my son. And I have been reminded of one.”
Channa bowed as King Suddhodana stepped into the morning light.
“Father!” Siddhartha exclaimed.
“I found you missing from your room. I might never have discovered your feelings otherwise. But, knowing Channa, I expected to find you here.”
Siddhartha winked at his faithful servant.
“You are more man now than when we spoke earlier, my son. You have learned you are no greater than the least of your subjects. I have learned I can't protect you from your destiny.”
“My destiny as king?” Siddhartha asked.
“As king or sage,” his father said. “Asita prophesized that you would choose between two great destinies. That is why we named you Siddhartha, ‘One Whose Aim Is Accomplished.’”
“Sages dress in rags, begging alms with a wooden bowl, and speak riddles no one understands,” Siddhartha laughed. “Only great kings can help the people.”
“Perhaps,” his father said. “I hope you choose to be king. But to be a good king, you must meet your subjects.”
“Then I can go outside the palace?”
“Channa will guide you, as will I. But today you may take part in the plowing ceremony. After all — I am king. Who else can decide such things?”

Many years later, at age twenty-nine, Prince Siddhartha Gautama discovered his true destiny. Leaving palace and riches behind to journey the world, he became a teacher. People called him Shakyamuni, “Sage of the Shakya,” the clan of his father. We know him as Buddha, meaning “the Enlightened One.”

options.label: Channa is a servant who takes care of Siddhartha's horse.
King Suddhodana wants Siddhartha to grow up to be a sage.
Siddhartha is a young prince who is not allowed to go outside of his palace.
King Suddhodana gives Siddhartha three of everything because of a prophecy.

questions.prompt: Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of "Two Destinies"?

options.label: He feels grateful because he knows the food will be delicious.
He feels curious because he has never seen a magician before.
He feels troubled because he wants to attend the plowing ceremony.
He feels angry because he thinks villagers should come to the celebration.

questions.prompt: How does Siddhartha feel about the celebration his father plans for him at the beginning of the story?

options.label: Siddhartha loves the horse very much.
Siddhartha knows the recipe for a special medicine.
Siddhartha is rich and can pay someone to cure the horse.
Siddhartha needs to learn a lesson about not being selfish.

questions.prompt: Channa tells Siddhartha that he is the only one who can help his horse, because –

options.label: He felt neglected by his friends after they forgot his birthday.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses "neglect" in the same way as it is used in paragraph 27?
Why does King Suddhodana change his mind about allowing Siddhartha to go to the plowing ceremony?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Siddhartha seems unhappy even though he lives in a palace with everything he wants or needs. Why do you think this is? Do you think it's possible to be rich and unhappy? Why or why not?
Why do you think Siddhartha chose to give up his palace at the end of the story to become a sage? If you had to make the choice between being a king and a sage, which would you choose? Why?

Siddhartha becomes the Buddha, the person who started the religion of Buddhism. What do you think this story can teach us about what is important in the religion of Buddhism? How can learning about different religions help us better understand the world around us?

The story shares that Siddhartha had "two destinies" — to either become a king or a sage. Do you believe in destiny or fate, the idea that the things that happen are pre- decided and based on luck? Why or why not?

articles.title: Miami by Fujian
articles.author: David Kwee
articles.description: A fifth-grader revisits her birthplace in China and learns to appreciate her multicultural identity.
articles.intro: To adopt means to take in as one's own. Some parents adopt children, which means to make them legally a part of their family. David Kwee is a Chinese American pediatrician who has cared for many adopted children in China. It was once common for many children in China to wait for adoption in orphanages — places where children without parents live and are cared for. In this story, fifth-grader Mariel revisits her birthplace in China with her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Mariel feels about herself.
articles.permissions: "Miami by Fujian" by David Kwee​​, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Sometimes, I wonder who I really am.

You're thinking, Hey, Mariel, you're a short, skinny fifth-grade girl with long, black hair and almond eyes. You live in Miami. You love arroz salteado . Your abuela scolds you for eating too much, then gives you a huge hug since she made it.

But some of my classmates think they know about me just because I look the way I do. They think I play violin and study all the time. I play volleyball. I make good grades, but I hang out with my friends, too.
Some think I can't speak Spanish. “ Hecho en China ” — Made in China — I once heard someone whisper. “ ¿Que tiene ella? No es una de nosotros. ” Who do I think I am? I'm not one of you? Not entirely.

I know where my family comes from. My abuelos talk about Cuba: the beaches, the silky jazz bands, and the gorgeous people of all colors. “Even some who look like you, cielita . But none as beautiful,” my abuela says.
So when my mom told me we were going to visit where I came from, for a moment I imagined a village in Cuba.
“No, mi vida . We're going to China. To the place where we adopted you. It's important that you see where you were born.” Her voice trembled.
“Mami, don't worry. You will always be the only mother I have.”
My abuela added, “Not the only mother you have. You have to put up with me, too.”
When it was bedtime, my mom kissed me. “We don't have to go if you don't want to, but we want you to feel sure of who you are....”
“OK, Mami. I want to go. But there's one thing I am worried about.”
Mami's concerned look made me smile.
“I really hate Chinese food.”
She smiled. “Don't worry, the Chinese food there is better than here.”
I couldn't sleep. I kept wondering what my birthplace looked like, what kind of people lived there, and how I would feel about it all.

The flight lasted twenty hours! As we flew over Hong Kong at night, the city lights looked like stars that had fallen onto the hillsides. The ride to the hotel amazed me. Cars and motor scooters darted everywhere. How could so many people crowd onto the sidewalks? Everybody had black hair and dark almond eyes like mine.

We walked to the markets in Kowloon. At the Temple Street Market, my father bought me a jade coin necklace. I put it on next to the little charm necklace I always wore. My abuela had put the charm around my neck when my parents first brought me home.

Then I had my fortune told. When the fortune teller saw me, she tried to speak Chinese, but soon gave up and spoke in English. She coaxed a little bird out of its cage with a nut. It hopped to a row of bamboo tubes with tiny scrolls inside them and pulled one out.
My fortune was that I would be happy with myself the way I am.
We ate at a dim sum restaurant. Carts wheeled everywhere. A low buzz and pleasant smells filled the air. I liked Chinese food after all, if hecha en China .

On the flight to Fuzhou, I looked down at mountains different from any I had ever seen. They rose from misty river valleys, some egg shaped, others like tall mushrooms or green-topped haystacks. Maybe fairies from these mountains left me at the orphanage.
After we landed, the car ride to the village took all day. Mami smiled while she cried. She was remembering when they had first come to the orphanage to get me.
Papi explained, “ Princesa , we were going crazy because we couldn't be together. We had waited so long. The paperwork took forever. All we had was a little photograph of you.”

My parents had shown me pictures from the first time they met me. I thought I was a cute baby. But not cute enough for my birth parents to keep me. The orphanage workers didn't tell my parents why I had been put up for adoption. Papi guessed that my birth parents could not afford to care for me.

We drove past farms as we climbed into the mountains. After we left the asphalt behind, the children were poorer, but still played hard in their shabby clothes. In one village, a truck broke down ahead of us. While we were waiting, I looked beside the road. A couple and their teenage daughter were raking a huge pile of rice. When our driver honked his horn, they glanced up at me.
They could be my parents and my older sister.
I thought I might recognize the orphanage — I'd lived there until I was nearly two years old — but nothing looked familiar. We were met by the director, Ms. Guo, who fussed over me.
“Min Zhi! I wondered would we ever see you again. You were a baby when you left. But Xing Hua has not forgotten you.”

The nursery's white walls announced “institute.” The floor was covered with soft foam puzzle tiles. A row of wheeled bassinets lined a wall, where an older woman rocked a crying baby. When she saw us, she spoke excitedly.
Ms. Guo translated. “Xing Hua can't believe how big you are. She remembers when you were smaller than this baby. Only you would never cry like that.”
Mami laughed. “She made up for that after we got her.”
Ms. Guo continued. “You were always quiet. Except when another baby laughed. Then you would, too. That's why we named you Min. It means gentle and sweet.”
Xing Hua scolded Ms. Guo.
Ms. Guo added. “Xing Hua says she named you. I wanted to call you Xing Cha, ‘lucky tea.’ We found you in a basket full of tea.”
My mom asked, “Why would her parents leave her in a basket of tea?”
“They gave us a gift to help pay for taking care of the baby. They probably felt shame they could not.”
I thought to myself, I'm lucky. My birth parents were probably dirt-poor farmers. Instead of hard work, crummy clothes, and not enough food, I lived in a nice house with plenty in the fridge.

Ms. Guo led us into a classroom jammed with kids. One boy had crooked, thin legs. His split mouth hung open, and his crossed eyes didn't focus. Kids with Down syndrome played on the floor. A blind girl sat on a sofa with a Braille book. But other kids looked completely healthy. They paid attention as the teacher wrote on the blackboard, in English.
Ms. Guo said, “Many will be adopted here in China, but a few will go to America.”

The lesson finished, and after a few friendly stares, the kids raced to the playground. A girl about my age hung back. She was shorter than me, and her black hair was tied up in ponytails. She was totally cute and smiled shyly. Why had I been adopted and not her?
I asked, “What's your name?”
“Mei Jing.”
“Are you the oldest here?”
“Yes. Ten years old.”
“How long have you been here?”
Her polite smile showed that she didn't understand — or didn't want to answer.
I had a million questions, but I didn't know where to start. She hesitated, then asked, “Your school fun?”
“Yes.”
“You like?”
“Sure. Do you like your school?”
“Yes. But I want go school in city.”
“Why?”
“More children ten years old. More learning.”
I felt for Mei Jing. She was stuck. I asked her, “Do you want to be adopted?”
She shrugged her shoulders. “Too old go America. Better stay China. If here parents want me, I am happy.”
I hoped she would find parents like mine.
A ball rolled by, and I picked it up. “Want to play?”

She nodded. I tossed the ball up then tapped it to her with my palm, like a volleyball. She caught it and tried to imitate me. At first she struggled, laughing. But she was a natural, and soon everyone was watching us bounce the ball between us.
After recess, the kids went to the garden. Mei Jing showed me how to harvest some vegetables for lunch. I didn't know what all of them were, but back at the kitchen she taught me how to cook them.
Then Ms. Guo gathered everyone together. The kids were very curious when she introduced my family, speaking first in English, then Chinese.
“This is your sister Min Zhi and her parents. They came a long way to visit. I hope she will remember us. She will always be welcome to return.”
Ms. Guo asked us. “Do you want to speak?”
My parents shook their heads, but I sat down in front of the kids. “I will answer any questions they want to ask.”

Ms. Guo translated, and I did my best to answer. Some of the questions were funny. Somebody even asked if I was friends with the president. Ms. Guo was about to end the session when I noticed Mei Jing had not raised her hand.
“Do you want to ask anything, Mei Jing?”
“In America, you happy?”
“Yes. I'm really happy.”
She simply said, “Good.”

Next morning before we left, Ms. Guo was waiting to say goodbye. We all hugged. I was about to get into the car when Mei Jing ran toward us. She handed me a piece of paper. Scrawled on it in English was the recipe for the vegetable dish.
“Cook this. Remember me.”

I almost cried. I didn't know enough Chinese to tell her all the good things I wished for her. I couldn't tell her that I was sure she would be adopted. Instead, I took my charm necklace off and fastened it around her neck. Neither one of us said anything more. We hugged each other, then I left.

Back home, my abuela took me to a Chinese market. She liked the bai cai I cooked. They tasted great with arroz salteado . Mami is a hopeless Mandarin practice partner, so I'm glad I like the boy in my language class at the cultural center.
My fortune has come true, by the way. I am happy the way I am — a Florida chica china by way of Fujian.

options.label: Adoption can be hard, but it always works out fine in the end.
Traveling to other countries is exciting and gives people courage.
Knowing about where you came from can help you like yourself as you are.
If you give away a special gift from your grandmother, you may miss it later.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Mei Jing has lived in the orphanage for her whole life and believes that she will not be adopted because she is too old. Reread paragraphs 40-55 in the text and think about the things that Mei Jing says that she would like to have, if only things were different. With a partner or in a small group, work together to write a different ending for Mei Jing. If she could get her "happy ending" after Mariel returns to Miami, how would you write it?

Mariel is close with her entire family, but she seems to have a very special relationship with her grandmother. Is there anyone in your family with whom you have a very close relationship? What do you like so much about this family member? What is it that makes the two of you get along so well?

In paragraph 73, Mariel says, "my fortune has come true, by the way. I am happy the way I am." Do you think most people can say that they are happy the way that they are? Why, or why not? Who do you think is more likely to feel happy the way they are – kids, or adults? Why? What are some things that people could do to feel happier and more secure in who they are?

articles.title: The Tiger Son: A Tale from China
articles.author: Retold by Sue Cowing
articles.description: In this retelling of a popular folktale, a widow loses her son and a tiger seeks to make amends.
articles.intro: "The Tiger Son" is a popular story in China that the writer Pu Songling (1640–1715) included in his book Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio . In this retelling, a widow —a woman whose husband has died — warns her only son to watch out for the tiger in the forest.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the widow’s feelings about the tiger.
articles.permissions: "The Tiger Son: A Tale from China" by Sue Cowing, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Hundreds of years ago, tigers roamed the forests of China. Almost everyone had seen one sometime in his or her life, and everyone knew someone who knew someone who had come to harm in a tiger’s jaws.

In the village of Zhaocheng, a man who was too poor to marry lived with his widowed mother. He took good care of her, making repairs on her house and providing her food. But to hunt for their meat and firewood, he had to go into the forest beyond their village, and in that forest he had often seen a large tiger hunting and swimming in the stream.
The widow warned her son to be careful.
He laughed. “Don’t worry, Mother,” he said. “Wasn’t I born in the Year of the Tiger? I will always return.”
But one day as she waited for him, dusk fell around her. Her son did not come home all night.
The next morning, some neighbors came running to tell her the terrible news. The tiger had pounced on her son and eaten him. All that was left of him were his bloody clothes.

The old widow’s grief was very great, but so was her anger. She went straight to the local magistrate and beat on the drum at his gate with her cane, demanding that the tiger be arrested and put to death.
“I deeply respect your loss, Old Mother,” said the magistrate, “but a tiger cannot be held responsible for what he does by nature. Shall we arrest bees for stinging?”
But the old woman would not leave until something was done. At last the magistrate asked his court, “Who will go and arrest this tiger?”
A deputy named Li Neng, who had had too much wine to drink, said that he would, and the old widow went home.
Of course Li Neng was just saying this to quiet the old lady, and he did nothing about the tiger. But the magistrate scolded him.
“You have given your solemn word. As a matter of honor, you must go get the tiger and bring him here.”

For weeks Li Neng searched for the tiger, but could not find him, much less catch him. The magistrate had the deputy beaten for his failure. Desperate, Li Neng went to the village shrine and threw himself on the ground. What was he to do?
Suddenly a large tiger appeared in the doorway of the shrine. This is surely the end of me, Li Neng said to himself. But I might as well die, since I cannot fulfill my duty.
The tiger did not attack. Instead, he sat quietly, showing neither teeth nor claws.
Li Neng took a deep breath and said, “If... if you are the tiger who killed the widow’s son, you must come with me. You are under arrest.”
To his surprise, the tiger lowered his head so Li Neng could put a loop of rope around his neck.
The people of Zhaocheng were amazed to see the deputy returning to the village with the tiger walking behind him.
The magistrate quickly set up court and said to the tiger, “Did you eat the widow’s son?”
The tiger nodded.
“How did you expect her to live after you killed her only son?”
The tiger closed his eyes.
“If you take a life, your own must be taken. That is the law...”
The tiger nodded again.
To tell the truth, the magistrate was impressed by the animal’s show of remorse.
“...but the law does not say how you must give your life. If you are willing to serve the old woman as her son, I will spare you.”
The tiger nodded once more, solemnly.
So they released him and sent him on his way.
When the old widow learned of this, she was furious. “Now I have no son and no justice either,” she said.
But the next morning she awoke to discover a freshly killed deer at her gate. She kept some of the meat for her supper and traded the hide and the rest of the meat for things she needed.
The next day she found a fresh wild pig in the same place.

Day after day the tiger brought fresh meat to the widow. It was always more than she herself could eat, so she always had some to trade or sell. Before long she was living very comfortably, and she was grateful to the tiger. She began letting him into her garden in the mornings to rest in the shade. Soon no one in the village was afraid of him, in spite of his fierce appearance.

After several years, the old widow died. The tiger came to her graveside and bellowed piteously for days. Then he left and was not seen again in those parts. The people of the village built a shrine to honor him, “The Shrine of the Ethical Tiger,” because he had been like a son to the old woman.

options.label: We cannot change someone or something's true nature.
We often fear what we do not understand.
When we punish others, it often backfires.
When we admit mistakes, it is never too late to do the right thing.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The magistrate tells the old woman: "a tiger cannot be held responsible for what he does by nature. Shall we arrest bees for stinging?" (Paragraph 8). In your opinion, should the tiger be punished at all for his actions? Do you think that the magistrate is right to say that we cannot punish animals for acting according to their nature? Why?

"The Tiger Son" is a famous and popular story from China. What does this story show about what character traits and actions Chinese people think of as important? Think about your own culture and family. How are these character traits and actions similar to and different from what people from your culture and family think of as important?
In your opinion, do the actions of the tiger towards the old widow make up for eating her son? Would you be able to forgive the tiger if you were the widow? Why or why not?

articles.title: Be Careful What You Fish For
articles.subtitle: (You Might Get It)
articles.author: Pamela Love
articles.description: A prince must prove he is the real prince after catching a copy of himself while fishing.
articles.intro: Artists often paint portraits, or pictures of what a person looks like. To paint these portraits, artists often ask the people they are painting to pose, or stand very still in one place, for a long time. In this story, a young prince is posing for his portrait.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the characters feel about the portrait.
articles.permissions: "Be Careful What You Fish For" by Pamela Love, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Cautiously, Prince Bernard lifted his saddle from the wall. Just a buckle's jingle or the straw crackling beneath his boots might give him away.
"Good afternoon, Your Highness. Are you taking Bright Star for a ride?"
Would he never escape? Suppressing a groan, the prince said, "Shh! Yes, but please don't tell anyone you saw me."

Paul Lebrun had been working in the royal stable since the king was Bernard's age and had taught the young prince all he knew about riding and caring for horses. Chuckling, he reached for Bright Star's bridle. "Let me guess. Are you hiding from Signor Romano?"

Placing the saddle on his horse, the prince rolled his eyes as he adjusted its girth. "Four hours I spent standing as still as a statue for that artist. 'Your Highness must stop fidgeting! You cannot expect me to create a perfect likeness if you will not cooperate. And stop frowning. No, don't grin like some clown. Now you're frowning again!' Nothing satisfied him.

At last, Signor Romano declared he was finished. It was all I could do not to turn cartwheels and handsprings. I headed to my room so I could take off these ridiculous clothes." The prince wore a purple velvet doublet, black satin breeches, and a hat with an ostrich feather twice the size of his head. "Then the artist changed his mind! And Father seems to have lost his, because he agreed to make me pose for hours more so my portrait can be a masterpiece."
The sound of voices calling Bernard's name came from the castle. "No doubt they're looking for you," Lebrun said. He quickly slipped the bridle over Bright Star's head. "You'd better be going."

Bernard mounted. "Thanks so much, Paul." He pulled the reins and trotted slowly out of the stables, trying not to look suspicious. Then, urging Bright Star into a canter, the prince put miles between himself and the castle until his horse began to breathe hard.
Dismounting, Bernard led Bright Star on foot, not much caring where he went as long as he kept moving. An hour later, he'd reached Looking Glass Lake, named for its mirror-clear waters.

Bernard looped Bright Star's reins around a nearby willow. He knelt to scoop up some water. As he leaned forward, the thread holding the feather on his hat snapped. The feather fluttered down and Bernard grabbed for it, lost his balance, and tumbled into the lake.
Spluttering, he stood up and felt a tug on the feather. A silver fish the length of his arm had the other end in its mouth.
"Release the feather!" It was a royal command, but not the prince's.
Bernard blinked. Standing before him was a water sprite, covered with silver scales and wearing a matching crown. "Release the feather!" repeated the sprite. "My pet wants it."

The prince shook his head. "I'll get in trouble with Signor Romana if I change even the smallest detail of my appearance." Then he had second thoughts. After all, he was already in trouble. And he'd heard enough tales of enchanted beings to know how foolish it was to offend a magical spirit unnecessarily. "Oh, very well. Take it."

With a flick of its tail, the fish swam off with its prize. Laughing, the sprite said, "Such generosity, Prince Bernard! Here is something you will like far better in return." Then it vanished in a spray of water.

In Bernard's open hand, a fishing rod had taken the feather's place. With a shrug, he decided to try out the sprite's gift. He climbed back onto the bank and flicked his wrist. The hook settled lightly on the lake's surface. Instantly, he felt it snag on something.

The prince liked surprises, unless they meant having to pose for hours longer to please a fussy artist. Closing his eyes, he reeled in his catch. Instead of jumping and twisting like a fish, it seemed to peel off the lake. Bernard's eyes opened wide, and his jaw dropped.
He'd hooked his own reflection! Once out of the water, it became three-dimensional. In every way, the two princes were identical to the last eyelash.

Bernard grinned as he tugged out the hook from his reflection's hat. "What a wonderful gift! Now you can pose for that artist." Turning, he shouted his thanks to the lake sprite. There was a brief sound of laughter, then silence.

From behind him came the sound of running feet. Bernard glanced back to see his reflection untying Bright Star and leaping into the saddle. Bernard dashed back, shouting, "No, don't go yet! Wait for me! I command you!" But it did no good. Horse and reflection galloped away.

Wet and angry, the prince followed. As he walked, the prince began to worry. What would his reflection do when it reached the castle? Was it even going there? As his nervousness grew, the prince began to run.
Out of breath, he at last arrived at the castle. It was well past sundown. The drawbridge was up. "Guard, let me in! It's Prince Bernard!" he bellowed up to the tower.
But the guard saw only a dim shape below in the twilight. "Shame on you! Imitating the prince's voice won't fool me. His Highness returned hours ago. Be off with you, rascal!"

Exhausted, his stomach growling with hunger, and even more afraid, Bernard sank down under an oak tree. He spent the night huddling on the castle lawn. When it began to sprinkle, he gritted his teeth. "This is just for a night. In the morning, Father will recognize me. Then everything will be all right," he told himself over and over.
When the drawbridge was lowered the next morning, Prince Bernard walked boldly into the castle's throne room. "Father, it is I!"

Beside King Mark stood the prince's reflection. It said, "See, Father! That's the lake reflection I told you about, the one I caught with the fishing rod a sprite gave me. Now it's treacherously trying to take my place."
"No, Father, that's the reflection!" shouted Bernard. "I'm the real Bernard."
The king slowly looked from one to the other. "How is it possible to tell? You're so much alike…”
The reflection said smugly, "Ah, but that thing is dripping wet. What does that tell you?"

Bernard yelled back, "Of course I'm wet. The guard wouldn't let me in. I spent the night outside in the rain and never dried off from falling in the lake." He pointed at his reflection. "That thing stole my horse and got back first."
"A likely story," the reflection sneered in return.

Bernard's heart began to pound even harder than after he'd run back to the castle. He could tell his father was beginning to believe the reflection. Why shouldn't he? After all, Bernard was the wet one. Other than that, they were identical, so there was no way to prove his identity. Then Bernard remembered one tiny difference he'd spotted the day before.
He lunged toward the reflection. It dodged, but not before the prince yanked off its hat. Bernard tossed it to the king. "Look, Father! My fishhook made a hole!"
King Mark felt the small rip in the velvet for a moment, before the hat and the rest of the reflection turned into mist.
Father and son embraced each other. Then the king said, "I'll send Signor Romano home. Perhaps a perfect likeness of you isn't as necessary as I'd thought."

articles.title: Heavy Cream
articles.author: Sheila Quinn
articles.description: Three clever kids find a way to have a great adventure during the Great Depression.
articles.intro: During the 1930s, the world experienced the Great Depression, a time when many people had a hard time finding work, food, and housing. Farmers in the United States also experienced droughts, or long periods without rain, that made it hard to grow crops. In this true story based on the author's grandmother's childhood in eastern New Mexico, three clever kids find a way to have a great adventure during the Great Depression.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how other characters help Maxine and Cleo.
articles.permissions: "Heavy Cream" by Sheila Quinn, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: We had just sat down to our cornbread and sweet milk when we heard the buzzing. Daddy headed out the door, and Momma followed with my brothers and me on her heels. We bent our heads to the sky, squinting in the direction of the sound.

Of course, in those days, we had all heard about Charles Lindbergh and his flight over the ocean. My best friend, Cleo, carried a picture of him that she had swiped from her daddy's collection of news clippings. The fifth-grade girls would pass it around, sighing and giggling during the lunch recess, and Cleo would try out the things she might say if she ever met the famous aviator.

The whirring, oversized bird had us all in a spell as it passed over our farm. We stared until the plane was out of sight, and I thought my eyes might never stop burning. I reached up and grabbed my daddy's hand, needing something to bring me back to earth. "That sure was something," he whispered. That was all any of us could say.

Weeks later, Miss May announced that a pilot would be making a stop near the Rogers School the next afternoon. For a dollar each he would give rides to those students who wanted to go up in his plane.
"Maxine, we have to get on that airplane!" Cleo said to me on the bus ride home.
"Sure, but . . ." My voice trailed off. Families of dirt farmers like us didn't have a penny to spare in those Depression days.
My best friend was not discouraged. "Maybe the pilot will look like Mr. Lindbergh," she said. I rolled my eyes.

When we got off the bus, I noticed my little brother Claude’s lips tilted into a crooked grin. He had been sitting behind us while we talked, and he didn’t say anything, but I knew that look. “What are you up to?” I asked.
“I know how you can both go up in that airplane.”
I knew it.
“Well?” Cleo asked.
I could see she was playing right into his hands.
“You’ll each get your dollar for the airplane.” He paused. “And you’ll owe me a dollar for the idea.”

By the time we reached the road to Cleo’s place, Claude had closed the deal, and we had our plan. When we got close to our house, I ran out to the cotton field where Daddy was weeding. I shared the news and our plan in a series of panting breaths. He leaned against his hoe, took off his hat, and wiped his brow as though he had just noticed me.
“Well, I’ll be,” he said.
Though he hadn’t actually answered my question, I threw my arms around him. He smiled, replaced his hat, and went back to work.

I found our old neighbor, Mr. Fletcher, in the next field and asked if he could help us. We were counting on the high butterfat of his Jersey cows for our plan to work. Once he agreed, I rushed to the house to get my lessons and chores finished.

At suppertime, I didn’t even taste my cornbread, though I did soak it in my milk to make up for the lack of butter. Cleo walked over, and we sat in our barn milking for an hour. When we finished, we walked over to the Fletcher place and each milked one of his cows. We carried our pails home one careful step at a time. Daddy was waiting for us outside the barn. He handed me the lantern, then kissed me on the forehead and went back inside. We poured the milk into empty cans, secured the lids, and worked together to lift them into the cold water of the stock tank.

The next morning’s chores came early. After a quick breakfast, I cleaned the dishes, swept the floor, and took the scraps to the chickens. Claude met me outside the barn with an empty five-gallon bucket in each hand. We saw Cleo coming across the field, and she joined us under the windmill. Claude helped us lift the milk cans out of the tank. I removed the lids and began skimming the cream off the top of each one. “Do you think it’s going to be enough?” Cleo asked.

We staggered to the bus stop with our half-filled buckets. The other children had already boarded by the time we arrived. The driver tilted his head and narrowed his eyes as he watched us struggle to get our cargo on board. I can’t be sure, but I wonder if he didn’t try to hit every pothole and rocky corner he could find that morning.

Rather than cross the road to the schoolhouse, we headed down the block. By the time we made it to Mr. McCormick’s store, my hands were blistered from the bucket handles. Mr. McCormick looked up from the counter he was wiping. “What are you kids up to?” he asked.
“We were hoping you might like to buy five gallons of cream.”
Mr. McCormick walked around the counter. “Did your daddy send you with all this? I’ve never known him to have so much at once.”
“Yes, sir,” I said. “That’s right. We usually keep it for our own butter, but he let us do all the milking last night and get the cream this morning. And Mr. Fletcher let us have some of his, too.”
“But what’ll you do for butter?”
“I’ll have to go without for a while. It’s part of the deal we made.”
“You see,” Cleo interrupted, “there’s a plane coming to the school today, and we each need a dollar to ride.”
“And another dollar for my brother,” I added.
“Oh, did he help, too?”
“Well, sort of,” I said. I was hoping I wouldn’t need to explain.

With a businessman’s tone and a grandfather’s smile, Mr. McCormick put out his hand and said, “As a matter of fact, I could use an extra stock of cream today.” The girls exchanged victorious smiles. “Say, you don’t suppose that pilot would give a ride to an old man, do you?”
“Sure!” Cleo said.
“I’m only teasing. I think I’d best keep my feet on the ground at my age and leave flying to you young ones. Besides, I just gave away my last dollar.”

Every minute of school that morning felt like forever. It was impossible to keep my attention on ordinary facts and figures knowing that something amazing was on the horizon. When Miss May finally dismissed us for our lunch recess, I was exhausted from the effort.
Suddenly, lunch was interrupted by a buzz like a million bees, and every child in the yard took off after it. Miss May called for us to wait, then she lifted her skirt and ran with us.

The plane went past the schoolyard, circled, and returned. It flew low enough that we could have jumped up and touched it, but we came to our senses and got out of the way. We flinched when the wheels bounced off the ground with a spray of dust and then let out a cheer when the bouncing gave way to a bumping roll.

When the plane finally stopped, a short, round figure stumbled out of the cockpit and peeled off his helmet to show tired eyes under bushy gray eyebrows. A cigarette clung to the corner of his mouth. “I guess not all pilots are Lindbergh,” Cleo muttered.
“My name is Lester Boyd, ma’am,” the pilot shouted at Miss May even though she was standing right next to him. “Anyone riding with me today?”
Our classmates’ faces dropped. Chasing the plane was the only thrill they could afford.

I stepped forward holding Cleo’s hand. Claude stepped beside us. I heard the murmur of the other children. The pilot looked skeptical. He hitched his thumbs in his front pockets and looked Cleo and me over.
He continued shouting. “You sure about this? I know the boy can handle it, but you girlies might want to think again. Don’t want nobody gettin’ sick in my plane.”
“Oh, yes! We have intestines of steel,” Cleo answered, matching his volume.
“Yes, sir!” I added.
“Well, give me your money and come on!”
We stepped forward holding out our money, and he handed us each a ticket in return. “This here is Miss Molly. She’s a Stinson SM-8A, a Detroiter. We’ve been all over the country together.”

He placed his foot on the step and pulled himself up. He turned back and reached a gloved hand down to help each of us into the plane. We ducked inside and crawled to the bench seat behind him. We watched as the pilot returned the helmet to his head then turned around and gave us a thumbs-up. We all returned the gesture.

When he started the engine, it was slow at first, but I could hear it picking up speed. I couldn’t see the propeller, but I imagined it spinning faster and faster as the engine grew louder. I began to understand why the pilot yelled so much. The plane jerked forward and rolled bumpily across the clearing. When we took off, I was sure my stomach was still on the ground and I hoped I could keep my promise not to get sick.
“Oh me!” I cried.
“Oh my!” Claude echoed.
“Miss Molly!” Cleo yelled.

Once the plane leveled off, I saw the world from a bird’s-eye view. It was both wonderful and sad. Seeing entire farms in one glance and looking at houses from the rooftops was amazing, but where there should have been green fields and lakes, there was nothing but dirt. It was the first time I understood the impact of those long years of drought.

We didn’t say much during the flight, though every once in a while one of us would point to a familiar landmark. We made out the Baptist and Methodist churches by their steeples and the post office by its flag. I hoped we’d fly over our farm so I could wave at my dad, but we were going the wrong direction. It’s hard to say how long we were in the air. Too soon, we were bumping our way back to the ground, testing my stomach again.

Two weeks went by before my biscuits or cornbread were softened with butter, and my oats went without cream even longer. But it was a small price to pay for that grand adventure with Miss Molly. I knew I’d remember it for the rest of my life. And if Cleo ever met Mr. Lindbergh, now they’d have something to talk about.

articles.title: Worms in Danger
articles.author: Elizabeth Kuelbs
articles.description: One short worm comes up big to save his family and friends.
articles.intro: Worms may make some people squirm, but they help all of us by breaking down waste and helping much-needed air and water get into our soil. In this story, a worm called Shorty wants to go on a field trip.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the other worms feel about Shorty.

blocks.text: I was finishing dinner when the alarm sounded. “Worm in danger!”

A rescue operation! I dropped my leaf and slithered out of our tunnel. The Safety Squad raced by me, rushing to the surface to save someone from a hungry robin. “Clear the way,” the captain shouted. “Worm in danger!” I tried to follow them, but Mom caught my tail.
“Where do you think you're going, Shorty?” she asked, tugging me back inside.
“Um, to help?”
“Exactly how would you help the Safety Squad?”

She had me there. I was too small for feather-pulling or pop-up teasing or any other bird defense maneuvers. I wasn't even allowed aboveground without a grown-up. But I was dying to do something exciting for once. “I could cheer them on,” I said.
“Time for homework, you,” Mom said.
“Please?”
“You heard me, Shorty.”
“Oh, fine.” Mom never changes her mind about homework.

Of course homework had to be Apple Boring, my worst subject. It took me forever to bore into an apple, especially the sour kind. I always got stuck on the peel, and when I tried to push loose, I rolled the apple over myself. So I imagined saving a baby worm from a sharp beak while my big brother chewed through his Granny Smith.
“Beat you again!” he said, with apple bits on his face. “Hey, guess who's going to Compost Mountain tomorrow!”
“Ooh. Hard one.” Not. “You?”
“Yep,” he said, grinning. “Scout field trip!”

I'd never been to that delicious mound of turnip green, baked bean, bagel crust yumminess. He knew I'd wanted to go there forever. But you needed full aboveground permission to be in Worm Scouts, and I was, yeah, you got it: too small.
“Want to bring me in your backpack?” I joked.
“Dude, no way. But if you give me your seed collection, I'll bring you back an eggshell.”
“You are an eggshell,” I said.

Mom is my brother's troop leader, so the next day my grandpa came to babysit me while the scouts went off to have fun. When Grandpa opened his newspaper, I crawled up to the ceiling, bit onto a root, and tried to stretch my tail down to the floor.
“What under earth are you doing?” Grandpa asked.
I let go and landed with a puff of dirt. “Um, getting taller?”
Grandpa shook his head. “Shorty, you only need to do two things to grow. One: eat compost for breakfast. Two: never be breakfast.”
I saw my chance. “Speaking of food, could we please take our own field trip? Pretty please? With mulch on top?”
“Well,” he said, putting down his paper, “I could go for some coffee grounds.”
I love my grandpa.
A few minutes later, we crawled topside and slithered off through thick grass and a cool drizzle. Soon we smelled lettuce and pasta and coffee.
I raced ahead. I could not wait to tunnel through compost, yummy compost! But then the ground started rumbling like gigantic robins were dancing on it.
I circled back to Grandpa. “Um, what's happening?”
Grandpa climbed a blade of grass to survey the area. I climbed another beside him.
It wasn't birds. It was an enormous furry thing, scratching at the side of Compost Mountain.
“Burrow, Shorty!” Grandpa yelled. “It's a puppy!” Puppies looked way smaller when we studied them at school.
Then the alarm sounded. Only this time it was my mom's voice shouting, “Worms in danger! Worms in danger!”
The furry thing was about to dig up everyone on my brother's scout trip.
“Shorty! Burrow!” Grandpa yelled. “I'll come back for you!” Then he slid to the ground and crawled straight for that thing, calling, “Here, puppy! Here, puppy, puppy!”
My five hearts pounded like hail. But I couldn't just hide. I had to do something! So I followed Grandpa.
The ground rumbled louder. Chunks of compost flew. Then the enormous paw scratched right in front of us. My whole body shook.
Grandpa stretched up tall and shouted, “Come here, you furry earthquake!”
But that puppy just sniffed at something round and green. An apple...that looked like a ball. Puppies chase balls.
“Worms in danger!” Mom yelled from close by.
I slithered past Grandpa, past the giant nose, and I chomped into that sour Granny Smith and tried to push loose. The apple started rolling downhill. Yes! Only I was still stuck on the peel.
The world spun. Mulch. Clouds. Mulch. Clouds. Huge sharp teeth!
I pried loose with all of my strength and tumbled to the ground just before those teeth munched me. The puppy thundered away with the Granny Smith in its jaws.
When its paw-steps faded, I found everyone tunneled safe inside Compost Mountain.
“Dude!” my brother said. “Awesome apple move!”
“Shorty! Shorty!” his troop cheered.
“That's my grandworm,” Grandpa said.
“Come here, you,” Mom said and hugged me tight.
Breakfast was delicious.
Later, the captain of the Safety Squad stopped by our tunnel to ask if I'd like to help their new Puppy Protection Division design some defense maneuvers.
“Mom, please?”
“Hmm,” she said. “OK. After you finish your homework, of course.”
“YES!”

articles.title: Magic Noodles
articles.subtitle: On a chilly day in 1947, one Japanese inventor dreamed of bringing hot noodles to the whole world. And he did!

blocks.text: Maybe you've seen those square packets of dry, curly Japanese noodles in the supermarket. Maybe you've even eaten them. You can find fast-cooking ramen in grocery stores from the Arctic to the tropics. Where did it come from, and how is it different from other noodles?
No-Wait Noodles

In 1947, the country of Japan was recovering from World War II. Many people were out of work. Food was scarce. One day, a bank manager in Osaka named Momofuku Ando walked past a long line of shivering people. They were waiting at a noodle stand to buy bowls of noodles called ramen .

A big bowl of ramen soup with fish and vegetables was a cheap and tasty meal. Ando never forgot the people standing outside in the cold, just for noodles. Ten years later, when he lost his job, that memory sparked an idea. Could Ando create a new kind of ramen, one that didn't make people wait?

Ando bought some second-hand kitchen gear and set up a workshop in his backyard shed. He made a list of goals: his noodles had to be tasty, cheap, and quick to cook. He also wanted people to be able to store his noodles without a refrigerator. So the ramen had to be dried. That was a problem.

Fresh ramen noodles are made with kansui, a mixture of salts similar to baking soda. The kansui makes ramen noodles feel springy when you bite into them. Most other kinds of noodles don't use kansui. When they are air-dried, then cooked in boiling water, they taste just like fresh noodles. But when ramen is air-dried and then boiled, it clumps together and goes rubbery.

Ando went to his shed and experimented with ramen nearly every day for a whole year. He made noodle after noodle. He tweaked the recipe and dried them in different ways. None had the right texture. But he kept trying.
World of Ramen

Then one day, Ando was in the kitchen when his wife was making tempura, battered vegetables cooked in hot oil. Ando watched the tempura bubble as it hit the oil. He realized the hot oil was driving water out of the batter (making bubbles). Was frying the answer to his ramen problem?

Ando fried some noodles to see. Sure enough, the hot oil boiled the water out of the ramen, “drying” it. When the fried ramen noodles were boiled, they tasted like they were fresh. Ando had found the answer!
Frying noodles had another surprise. It filled the noodles with tiny holes. Those holes made the ramen cook fast with not much water.
Ando seasoned his noodles before frying them. That meant they didn't need to be boiled in a big pot with broth — his ramen cooked in two minutes, just by pouring hot water over it.

On August 25, 1958, Ando introduced his new ramen to Japan. Because it cooked so quickly, with just a little hot water, it was nicknamed “magic ramen.” Ando's noodles were a hit! Trucks lined up at the factory, waiting to be filled.

Instant noodles soon spread to other countries. At first, the soup flavor was dusted directly onto the noodles. Later, Ando switched to powdered soup packets. These could be made in many different flavors.

In 1966, Ando was visiting America when he saw workers put dry ramen noodles in a cup, pour hot water over them, and eat them with a fork. Ando was inspired. After five years of experimenting, he made a new version of ramen that came in its own cup.

Instant ramen has even been to space! At age 91, Ando formed a special team to create "Space Ram." They pressed the ramen into a ball shape and made a thick, syrupy broth. This sticks to the noodles even floating around in space. Astronaut Soichi Noguchi of the Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) tried them out on the Space Shuttle Discovery in 2005. Ando was 95 years old.

Momofuku Ando passed away in 2007, but his company and many others are still busy making noodles. It's estimated that more than 100 billion servings of instant ramen have been eaten since Ando's first batch. That's a lot of noodles for the world.

options.label: Instant ramen was popular when it was first invented, but not as many people buy it today.

articles.title: Excerpt from "Adventures of Isabel"
articles.author: Ogden Nash
articles.description: A young girl meets a bear during her journey.
articles.intro: Ogden Nash (1902-1971) was an American poet who wrote over 500 pieces. Nash was one of the best-known writers of amusing poetry. In this poem, a young girl meets a bear.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the bear wants to do to Isabel and how she responds.
articles.permissions: Copyright © 1936 by Ogden Nash, renewed. Reprinted by permission of Curtis Brown, Ltd.

blocks.text: Isabel met an enormous bear,
Isabel, Isabel, didn’t care.
The bear was hungry, the bear was ravenous,
The bear’s big mouth was cruel and cavernous.
The bear said, Isabel, glad to meet you,
How do, Isabel now I’ll eat you!
Isabel, Isabel, didn’t worry;
Isabel didn’t scream or scurry.
She washed her hands and she straightened her hair up,
Then Isabel quietly ate the bear up.

options.label: Animals aren't as dangerous as people usually believe.
People who act unkindly are always punished.
Bravery can help people face scary challenges.
Staying calm is the best way to handle a stressful situation.

articles.title: Broomball
articles.author: Nora Nickum
articles.description: Jade has trouble adjusting to her new home after her family moves from California to Minnesota.
articles.intro: In this story by Nora Nickum, Jade and her family just moved from California, where the weather is typically warm, to Minnesota, which has much colder winters. Ice skating on outdoor rinks, or areas that have a smooth, frozen surface of ice, is a popular sport in this part of the United States.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Ms. Silva’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Broomball" by Nora Nickum, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The cafeteria conversation was all about hockey again. Jade usually fit in with the sports crowd, but now she was the odd one out. She stared out the window and let her mind drift like the whirling snow to a soccer field in sunny California.

Hazel elbowed her in the side. “When are you going to get out on the ice, Jade?” Hazel turned to the others at the table. “Jade was a soccer star at her old school. I’m lobbying her to take up hockey.”

Back in California, the only ice was in tiny frozen puddles. Here in Minnesota, it seemed everyone skated like a pro by the time they got to kindergarten. Jade didn’t have skates, and she couldn’t make it to open skating time because she had to spend weekday afternoons babysitting Calvin while he toppled block towers and got crumbs everywhere.

She sighed. She’d have to wait months for spring to arrive, bringing new conversation topics and sports she could actually play. But Hazel had been friendly since their first homeroom class — and she wasn’t going to give up on this.
“I’ll stop by to watch this weekend,” Jade told her.
“Perfect! The rink is behind the stadium. We practice on Saturday morning.”

Jade watched the hockey players zip around the outdoor rink, the sounds of scraping skates interspersed with pucks whacking against the boards. The wind was frigid, and she stuck her chin into her scarf and stomped her feet to warm up.
When practice ended, Hazel pulled off her skates and handed them to Jade. “You made it! Give these a try.”

Jade laced them up. Stepping onto the ice, she grabbed the wall and inched her way along. On her second circuit, she let go and did a wobbly glide. A boy who looked barely older than Calvin launched into a twirl inches away. Flinching, Jade lost her balance and fell hard.
Soccer skills might help her with hockey if she ever made it near the goal, but she wasn’t off to a good start with the skating.
“I’ll have serious knee bruises tomorrow,” she said, as Hazel reached over the wall to help her up.

“At least it’s not shorts weather,” Hazel replied with a laugh. “Just practice every day — in a few weeks, you’ll be good enough to start training with us. You can rent skates from Ms. Silva, the facility manager.”
Jade nodded. She didn’t mention that when she finally had free time, in the evenings, the rink was closed. Or that the cost of daily skate rentals would add up fast.

After school on Monday, Jade stopped at the thrift store, checking to make sure none of her new classmates saw her go in. In the sporting goods aisle, there was only one pair of skates, four sizes too big.

Jade dug her hands into her coat pockets and hurried home, retrieving Calvin from the neighbor’s house and finding his loudest musical toy. Maybe it would drown out her nagging thoughts about how the icy winter made her feel as aimless as a soccer ball without a goal.
Mom came home exhausted as usual from her job at the diner. After a quick spaghetti dinner, she put Calvin to bed and fell asleep beside him.
The house was silent except for the scraping rumble of a passing snowplow. Jade tiptoed into the bedroom.
“Mom, can I go for a walk?” she whispered.
Mom sat up and stretched. “I can’t believe I fell asleep again. Sure, honey. Just be home by eight.”
Jade grabbed her winter gear and tugged on her boots. Walking down sidewalks sprinkled with sand, her clouds of breath glowed under the streetlights, and ice crystals formed on her eyelashes.

The rink was deserted and dim in the streetlight. She leaned on the wall. Then she unlatched the gate and stepped in. She slid around in her sneakers, flailing her arms to keep from falling. She felt ridiculous and couldn’t stop giggling.
After dinner the next evening, Mom swept up the crumbs under Calvin’s highchair. Watching her grasping the broom handle, Jade got an idea.
When Mom and Calvin headed to bed, Jade hugged them goodnight and bundled up again. She grabbed the broom and an old tennis ball. She’d try her hand at pretend hockey.

At the rink, she glided around in her shoes, hitting the ball with the broom. She fell a few times, but it didn’t matter — nobody was watching. She managed to tap the ball into the goal once before it was time to head home.
At school on Wednesday, Hazel plunked her lunch tray down next to Jade. “I saw some awesome new skates at Super Sports. You should buy them, so you don’t have to rent used ones.”
Jade took a bite of her sandwich. “I’ll check them out this weekend,” she mumbled. She already knew Mom wouldn't be able to afford them.
Jade made three tennis-ball goals at the rink that evening, imagining the last was a game-winning shot. But her grin disappeared as she saw the facility manager striding over.

“Someone called in an after-hours skater,” Ms. Silva said, hands on her hips. New wrinkles appeared in her forehead as she looked down at Jade’s feet. “But those aren’t skates. Now I know where the dirt came from yesterday.”
“I’m really sorry,” said Jade.
“It’s my job to clean the ice, you know. Takes me longer if people don’t follow the rules.” Ms. Silva paused. “I heard you moved here from California. They have broomball there?”
Jade snorted. “That’s a good name for it.”
“Broomball is a real sport, you know.”
Jade found that hard to believe but questioning Ms. Silva would probably make her grumpier.
“Come back this weekend,” said Ms. Silva with a loud sigh. “You owe me an hour of rink-cleaning.”
On Saturday morning light snow was falling. Ms. Silva nodded when she saw the old sneakers in Jade’s hands. “No dirt on those, they’ll work.”
She handed Jade a shovel. Ms. Silva clearly wasn’t one for making small talk. “First we clear off this new snow before it freezes and makes the surface bumpy.”
Jade warmed up quickly with the shoveling. She hadn’t sweated like that since her last soccer game back home.
“Not bad,” said Ms. Silva. “Want a weekend job?”
Jade’s stomach flipped. Maybe she could earn enough to buy the skates. “Definitely.”
“I’ll teach you to fix a rut. See if you can spot one.”
Jade slid around the ice until she found a big crack. Ms. Silva cleaned it out with a broom.

“I looked up broomball,” Jade said as Ms. Silva swept away bits of ice. She’d read that broomball rules were similar to hockey rules, but players wore shoes and had a bigger net for scoring goals. Most important, broomball players didn't have to know how to skate. “Is there a team in town?”

“Not for the last six or seven years,” said Ms. Silva. “I’ve been meaning to get rid of the blue and orange balls taking up room in storage. Now, pack that rut with snow. Then we’ll dribble water on it and let it freeze.”
Jade finished the patch job.
“That’s good for now,” said Ms. Silva. “The hockey team will arrive soon.”
That meant Hazel. Jade wasn’t sure whether she wanted Hazel to know she had a job. Hazel’s family seemed to have plenty of money to buy her all the hockey equipment she needed.
“I’ll come help again next weekend,” Jade said.

Ms. Silva carried the shovel back to the shed. “Responsible employees sometimes stop by the rink after hours,” she said over her shoulder. “To remove any leaves — or a stray blue ball, for that matter.”
Jade grinned. A broom would come in handy for that.
As she walked home, a car stopped, and Hazel poked her head out the window. “Where are you going? Let’s skate together after hockey practice is over.”
“I have to watch Calvin,” Jade said. Mom had babysitting Calvin covered today, but Jade couldn’t skate with Hazel until she had skates of her own.

All the following week, Jade was a dutiful employee, checking on the rink every night. She shoveled new snow, picked off leaves, and filled ruts. And then she pulled the balls from the bucket that had appeared by the gate — along with a stick with a narrow, broom-shaped rubber head on the end, just like the ones she had seen online — and did forty shots on goal. Realizing that her soccer skills were starting to pay off, she did a careful little dance on the ice to celebrate.
When the weekend arrived, Jade went to the rink early. She helped Ms. Silva scrape away bumps to prepare the ice for hockey practice.
Hazel appeared and Jade waved.
“Are you free to hang out here after practice?” Jade asked.
“Sure.” Hazel grinned.
Practice seemed to take forever. Jade jumped up and down, partly to stay warm and partly because she was nervous Hazel wouldn’t like her idea.
An hour later, Hazel skated over. “Ready? Wait, where are your new skates?”
Jade had planned to tell Hazel the truth, but now the words weren’t coming out. Her throat was dry and tight. “I... forgot them.”
“Run home and get them. I’ll come along.”
Jade cleared her throat. “I mean, I don’t have skates yet.”
“Super Sports didn’t have the ones you wanted? Just put in a special order.”

Squeezing her cold fingers together, Jade took a deep breath. “That’s not it. I don’t have money for skates yet. But I have a weekend job now, so I can buy some in a few weeks.” Hazel hesitated and then shrugged.
“That’s cool. What did you want to do today, then?”
Jade’s heart lifted. She held up a pair of sticks and a blue ball. “If you get your sneakers back on, we can play with these.”
Hazel’s eyes lit up. “Are those for broomball?”
“Yeah. Playing after hockey practice is perfect, because the skates have chopped up the surface. It’ll be less slippery, and easier to run on.”
“How do we play?”
“I’ll show you!”

Hazel waved over a few teammates and introduced them to Jade. They changed into their shoes and headed back onto the ice, running and slipping and laughing and asking Jade lots of questions about broomball — and about California, and soccer, and her other hobbies. There weren’t enough sticks to go around, so for now, they made up their own game — a mix of hockey and soccer and broomball and finally fitting in.
Ms. Silva passed by, her lips twisted into a hint of a smile. “Looks like we may have a broomball team again.”
“I’m nominating Jade for team captain,” hollered Hazel.
Jade laughed and whacked the ball into the goal.
