articles.title: A Fishy Christmas
articles.author: Charlotte Blessing
articles.description: A young boy's family and local community help rescue a fisherman who is lost at sea.
articles.intro: In this short story, a young boy prepares to celebrate his second Christmas in Zanzibar, an island off the coast of Tanzania, a country in East Africa.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Walter’s actions in the story.
articles.permissions: "A Fishy Christmas" by Charlotte Blessing, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Walter’s eyes were fixed on the glow-in-the-dark frogs and lizards stuck to his ceiling. He sighed. If only he could fall asleep! But his mind was on the big box that had arrived that day from his grandmother in America: Christmas presents, he was sure.

This was Walter’s second Christmas on the tropical island of Zanzibar. When his parents had moved the family there over two years ago to study dolphins, Walter had been unprepared for his first tropical Christmas. Sure, it was awesome to go out in the powerboat every day to search for dolphin pods with his parents, but that first holiday had been strange….

Most of the islanders were Muslim, so they didn’t celebrate Christmas. December 25 was just another day to them. There were no holiday parties like the ones at home in America. Even the family’s Christmas tree — a young coconut palm tree decorated with strings of shells and seaweed — had been a shock to Walter.
But that had been the first Christmas. Tomorrow would be his second, and he was looking forward to the different — but still exciting and fun — celebration with his family.

Walter tiptoed over to the open window. It was a moonless night, but a tiny row of lights flickered on the horizon. “It’s the fishermen,” Walter whispered, “searching for the big fish that surface because of the lantern lights. At least someone else is awake besides me….”

The father of Walter’s friend Hamisi was a fisherman. He slept all morning, repaired nets in the afternoon, and, when the sun set, he went out in his fishing boat. He didn’t return until the sun rose again.
Clonk, clonk. Walter woke up to the sound of small rocks hitting the wooden shutters on his window. “Walter! Come to the door!” whispered a voice.
Hamisi? Walter thought. What was he doing here? Hamisi didn’t celebrate Christmas.
“Coming.” Walter yawned as he jumped out of bed and padded to the door.

Hamisi was breathing hard. “My father is not back yet. My mother is so worried. We do not know what has happened to him. My mother told me to run here and ask your fa — ” Hamisi didn’t have the chance to finish his sentence.
“Mom! Dad!” Walter called, racing to their bedroom. “Wake up!” He bumped into his mother as she opened the bedroom door.
“Merry Christmas, Walter,” his mother said, raising her eyebrows. “And Hamisi! How are you?”
“Morning,” Walter’s dad said, coming to the door. “Did you check to see if Santa’s sleigh managed to fly all the way to Zanzibar?”
Walter ignored the question. “Dad, we need your help!”

“Baba Walter,” Hamisi said, “my father is in trouble. His boat didn’t return this morning with the others. Your powerboat is much faster than the fishing boats. My mother wonders if you will help us look for him….” Tears glistened in Hamisi’s eyes.
Walter’s father immediately began getting dressed. “We need people and a strong rope. Hamisi, tell your family and any others you can find to get down to the beach right away.”
Hamisi sprinted off.
“Walter, get the rope from the storeroom. We might need to pull the boat when we find it.”
Walter nodded.

A search-and-rescue team gathered at the beach and was ready when Walter’s family arrived. Last year, a fishing boat had gone missing, too. In the end, the news hadn’t been good. But this time it was Christmas morning, Walter reminded himself. They had to find Hamisi’s father.

The tide was out, so the rescue team had to carry the powerboat until the water reached their knees. No one spoke. Everybody scouted for signs of Hamisi’s father’s ngalawa , the traditional wooden boat with one sail.

Walter’s father started up the engine and headed out to sea. “What are the fishermen catching these days?” he asked Hamisi. The bigger the catch, the farther out the boats would fish. Even Walter had learned that.
“Tuna and red snapper,” Hamisi quickly replied.
The sun burned overhead like fire. They searched for several hours, but there was still no sign of the boat. Walter watched his friend grow quieter and quieter, his forehead bunched up with worry.
Suddenly Hamisi shouted, “Look! That’s our boat! Father!”
Walter’s father slowed the powerboat. As they neared the ngalawa , they could see Hamisi’s father lying down. He was waving one arm.

Walter’s father tied the rope to the ngalawa and leaped aboard. Hamisi’s father was exhausted and was so thirsty he could barely speak, but other than that, he seemed fine. Hamisi climbed into the ngalawa and held his father’s hand as they were slowly and steadily towed back to shore.
It took five grownups to carry Hamisi’s father’s catch onto the beach. Everyone admired the huge fish: a black marlin longer than Walter’s father was tall.

Hamisi’s father sipped some water while he told them his story. “Only small fishes had gotten caught in my net, and I knew they would bring no money for us,” he said, shaking his head. “So I decided to move the boat, just a little, and before I knew it, something big was pulling on my line. The ngalawa leaned over and took in some water.”

Hamisi’s father rubbed his eyebrows, shaking his head again. “I knew this beast would make much money for our family, so I couldn’t let it go. I held the line until my arms screamed in pain… and yet I held on even longer.” He rubbed his arms. “I fought the fish for hours, but I won. With my last bit of strength, I pulled it into the boat. Then I must’ve fallen asleep. I was so tired! It was the sound of your powerboat that woke me up.” Hamisi’s father laughed.

After the rescue, Walter suddenly remembered it was Christmas Day. He still had to empty his Christmas stocking and open the huge box from Grandma. And as for Christmas dinner, Walter and his family were having grilled black marlin fish… and coconut ice cream for dessert.

options.label: You can catch the biggest fish if you fall asleep.

Problems are best solved when people work together.

articles.title: The Memorial Day Mission
articles.author: Debbie A. Taylor
articles.description: A boy faces his fear of public speaking to share his grandfather's experience as a Tuskegee airman.
articles.intro: Debbie A. Taylor is a children's book author. This story is about Boomer and his grandfather, who was one of the first Black pilots in the U.S. Air Force. This group of Black pilots were called the Tuskegee airmen. Pilots call their orders "missions."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Boomer’s grandfather’s actions.
articles.permissions: "The Memorial Day Mission" by Debbie A. Taylor, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “Is that the last one, Poppy?” asked Boomer. His great-grandfather nodded. Every Memorial Day, they placed little flags by the headstones of a dozen pilots. Boomer and Poppy walked out the gates of the cemetery toward home.

Boomer smiled. Poppy was coming to school tomorrow to tell all about his years as a real Tuskegee Airman — one of the first African-American pilots in the world. Who wouldn’t want to hear how he escaped the swarms of enemy airplanes that chased him through the skies above Europe over sixty years ago? Boomer never wanted to speak in front of his class, but there wasn’t a shy bone in Poppy’s body.
They climbed the steps to Boomer’s house. Suddenly, Poppy’s foot slipped. Down he went, right off the porch. “Poppy!” yelled Boomer. Mom and Dad burst through the front door. Mom knelt beside Poppy.
“Don’t move! I’m calling the paramedics,” said Dad.
Before Boomer could blink, Poppy was on a stretcher in the ambulance with Mom holding his hand.
“I’ll call from the hospital,” said Mom as the ambulance doors closed.

Boomer picked up the box of small flags and squeezed it against his chest. While they waited, he and Dad rubbed grease off the barbecue grill without talking. An hour later the phone rang, and Dad snatched it up.
“A sprained ankle? OK. See you then.”
Dad hung up the phone. “Well, Poppy is fine, but he needs to stay in the hospital overnight for some tests. He wants that old suitcase from under his bed.”
Boomer raced to get Poppy’s suitcase. He was quiet during the ride to the hospital.
Poppy sat in bed stirring something green. Mom fretted the way she did when Boomer forgot to wear his hat on a cold day.
“Now, Grandpop, you’ve got to eat,” she said.
“But I need some real food, like spareribs, baked beans, and pie, not these green jiggly-wigglies.”

He grinned when he saw Boomer and Dad. Dad shook Poppy’s hand, but Boomer threw his arms around his great-grandfather’s neck. Dad placed the suitcase on a table. “We’ll be back in a few minutes,” he said. “Mom and I’ll go try to find some snacks.”
“Don’t worry, Boomer,” said Poppy. “I’ll be home soon. But I’m sorry I can’t visit your school tomorrow.”
“Oh, I forgot!” said Boomer.
“Well, we promised to tell about the Tuskegee Airmen. Now you’ll have to speak for both of us.”
“How?” asked Boomer. “Poppy, you know I don’t like talking in class. Last week at Show and Tell my voice cracked, and I dropped my model car. I’m too scared.”

“Scared!” said Poppy. “The first time I climbed in a P-51, my hands shook. But nobody was better trained to intercept enemy planes and protect our pilots than the Tuskegee Airmen. So I took a deep breath and buckled up.”
Boomer shook his head. “But I just can’t.”
“Well, hold on now. Let’s see what’s in my suitcase.” Poppy lifted out an aviator helmet and a leather jacket.
“See this?” asked Poppy. Boomer traced a jagged scar with his finger. “A piece of shrapnel tore through the floor of the cockpit and scratched that sleeve.”
Boomer slipped on the helmet, then squinted through the goggles. “These kept the bugs out of your eyes?”

“Not many bugs flew into the cockpit,” said Poppy. “But I sure was grateful for those goggles the night my whole canopy tore off in mid-air. Between the freezing rain and winds beating against my face, I couldn’t see a thing, but the goggles protected my eyes. Luckily, two other pilots guided me back to base.”
He patted Boomer’s shoulder. “Could you show your classmates that jacket and helmet? And there’s more in this suitcase.”
“I don’t know, Poppy,” said Boomer.
“Visiting hours are over,” said a nurse.
Boomer hugged Poppy. “You decide,” Poppy whispered.

At home, Boomer slipped on the leather jacket. He pulled on the helmet and faced the mirror. “I am… a Tuskegee Airman!” he croaked. “Aww, my head looks like a buckeye.” He stuffed his hands into the jacket pockets.
Something soft as a cat’s tail touched his fingers.
“What’s this?” Boomer pulled out a velvet pouch.
He opened the pouch and drew out a red-tailed model airplane. “The P-51!” He spun the propeller. Then he set the plane on his nightstand.
The next morning, Boomer trudged to school. Poppy’s suitcase felt heavier with each step.

In the classroom, Boomer’s teacher, Mrs. Kane, announced, “Unfortunately, Mr. Harris won’t be joining us, but his great-grandson, Boomer, will tell us about the Tuskegee pilots who served our country so bravely.”
Boomer walked to the front of the room. Most of his classmates looked only half-awake. One of them even yawned.
Suddenly, the suitcase snapped open and slipped from Boomer’s hands. The helmet slid across the floor.
“Uh-oh, I can’t do this,” he whispered as he scrambled to pick up the helmet. Then he thought of Poppy’s proud face. He swallowed and took a deep breath.
First, he held up the leather jacket. “My great-grandfather wore this jacket in the 301st Squadron. Poppy and his friends were the first Black Air Force pilots.”
Then, he untangled the strap on the goggles. “He was really glad he had these when the cover over the cockpit fell right off the plane in midair!”
Boomer’s classmates leaned forward.
Finally, Boomer pulled out the pouch. “This is a model of his P-51.” The other kids craned their necks to see the little airplane.
“This plane,” Boomer said in a strong, clear voice, “flew as fast as lightning!”
Poppy came home the next day. Boomer told him all about the presentation. Then they tucked the jacket and helmet back into the suitcase. But Poppy left the model plane out.
“Boomer, you keep this someplace where you can see it. Maybe on your dresser,” said Poppy.
Boomer smiled. The P-51 gleamed next to his plate all through their dinner of spareribs, baked beans, and strawberry pie.

Before 1940, African Americans were not allowed to pilot airplanes in the United States Air Force. Then in 1941, during World War II, President Franklin D. Roosevelt broke that restriction by commanding that African Americans be trained as pilots at an Army airfield in Tuskegee, Alabama, home of the Tuskegee Institute, a college for Black students. Despite having to eat and sleep in separate barracks and train apart from white pilots, nearly 1,000 men completed the training from 1941-1945, learning to fly red-tailed airplanes and protect other planes from enemy fire. Of these, 450 served overseas. Notably, these dedicated heroes never lost a single plane they escorted!

options.label: A family's support can help one be brave.
It is important for old people to be careful.
Sometimes bravery means remaining silent.
Old people can learn a lot from young people.

articles.title: Bending Sticks into Sculptures
articles.subtitle: Artist Patrick Dougherty has turned his love of nature into a career in art.
articles.author: Highlights for Children
articles.description: "Stick Man" Patrick Dougherty builds giant sculptures out of sticks.
articles.intro: Sculptures are three-dimensional objects made from materials like wood, clay, or metal. In this text, an artist named Patrick Dougherty builds sculptures out of sticks.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Stick Man helps the world around him.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Outside an art museum in Topeka, Kansas, a child peers from the window of what looks like a giant hobbit house. Other kids scamper through the house's woven archway into a twisted twig tower. The house, made entirely of sticks, is a sculpture built by Patrick Dougherty. Most people call him the Stick Man.
Dougherty has created more than 200 of these stick sculptures all over the world. "I design my sculptures to look like they've always been there," he says.
And Dougherty likes people to get "into" his work. If they arrive during the three weeks he takes to build a sculpture, he lets them build, too. "I want my work to help people enjoy nature."
From Forts to Sculpture

As a child, Dougherty learned to love nature while tramping through the North Carolina woods. He'd work for hours bending sticks into forts and hideouts for his brothers and sisters. When he grew up, he studied art and learned to sculpt with clay. But it was too heavy to make the lines and shapes he imagined.

One day, he watched thin, young trees waving in the wind beside his driveway. The saplings reminded him of the sticks he built forts with as a boy. Soon after, he began his first creation made out of sticks.
Birth of a Sculpture

To begin a sculpture, Dougherty digs holes and buries tall saplings in the ground for support. Then he weaves smaller sticks around them. He uses twine to hold and bend the twigs while he's weaving. When he removes the twine, the sticks stay in place. "If you drag a stick behind you," Dougherty says, "you see how it snags other things." The strength of this "grabbing power" keeps the sticks in the sculptures together.

It takes four tons of sticks to build a sculpture, and Dougherty chooses materials that would otherwise be wasted. For example, he often selects finger- to wrist-sized saplings from areas where the young trees would be cleared anyway for new building projects.

He also likes to use wood that grows naturally in the area. He has used bamboo in Japan and strawberry guava in Hawaii. In places like Kansas, he may use maple or willow. "I like to use wood that bends well," he says. But getting native saplings isn't always easy. In one area, biting ants attacked him. In another, he had to watch for alligators and poisonous snakes.
But Dougherty likes solving these problems. "Maybe it snows, or we have to change where we're going to build," he says. "Or we have to check the sticks for bugs or treat them to prevent fires."
Back to the Earth

Dougherty also likes getting to know his helpers. Anyone who wants to help is welcome, and Dougherty chats with the students, passersby, and businesspeople who may volunteer. "My sculptures are friendly. While people work, they tell me their own stick stories."

Dougherty's sculptures last two to four years. But even after that, they serve a purpose. When the wood begins to rot, it is shredded into tiny pieces called mulch. As mulch breaks down, it makes soil rich and helps new plants grow.
So if you happen to see a giant pile of sticks taking shape in a neighborhood near you, it might just be the Stick Man weaving his magic again.

options.label: Sticks that Stay

Volunteering with the Stick Man
Cutting Down Trees to Make Sculptures

articles.title: The Biggest Little Artist in the World
articles.author: LeeAnn Blankenship
articles.description: LeeAnn Blankenship discusses a famous artist who creates amazing tiny sculptures.
articles.intro: In this informational text, LeeAnn Blankenship discusses the success of Willard Wigan, a famous artist who creates sculptures that can only be seen using a microscope.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Willard creates tiny art.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Five-year-old Willard Wigan struggled to tell the difference between an M and a W or a 6 and a 9 . Unfortunately, his schoolteacher knew nothing about dyslexia, a learning disability that can make letters and numbers confusing. She didn’t try to help him.

Not surprisingly, Willard didn’t like school. Usually, his mind drifted — to playing outside, to his dog Maxie, or to the ants that lived near his family’s garden shed. Willard was especially curious about those ants. He felt like them — small and insignificant.
Building Ant Houses
Willard noticed that the ants were coming and going from a hole in the dirt. One ant was carrying a blade of grass, and Willard thought, He’s trying to build a house, so I am going to help him!

Gathering splinters of wood and carefully splitting them with a shard of glass, Willard constructed a little building. Then he sprinkled sugar inside to encourage the ants to move in. When they did, Willard built more houses.

At school, Willard still struggled, but now he knew he could do something special. Maybe he wasn’t a failure after all. If he had trouble with his reading or math, Willard later went home and created tiny furniture for the ant houses. Sometimes, he made ant coats and hats with fabric scraps. He even built an ant school, with teeny swings, ladders, seesaws, and a merry-go-round.
His artistic skill increased, and a love for little things began to grow in his heart.
Carving on Toothpicks
At age nine, Willard began carving faces on toothpicks.

He discovered that his ability improved when he held his breath as he worked. When he showed his mother the carved faces, she said, “This is what you do well. You must strive to be the best in the world.”

With her encouragement, Willard continued. When he quit school at age 15 to help support his family, Willard still spent his spare time carving. His confidence grew as more people appreciated his talent.

Eventually, he quit his factory job to pursue his dream of becoming one of the best artists in the world. Willard’s mother advised him, “The smaller you carve, the bigger your name will become.” So he began to carve even smaller.
The Tiniest Artwork
Now, years later, Willard carves the tiniest artwork in the world! His sculptures are so small that several can fit on a period at the end of a sentence.
Using a powerful microscope, Willard carves grains of sand, flecks of gold, bits of fiber, and even specks of dust.

To make his carving instruments, Willard drills a hole in the point of a needle and inserts a diamond shard for cutting. As he carves, he holds his work with a tiny hook. But static electricity sometimes causes a sculpture to flit away — lost forever.

Willard works best at night when there are few disturbances. He calms his body and, holding his breath, he carves between heartbeats when blood is not pulsing through his fingertips. He often works for months to complete a sculpture. Then he paints it with bits of acrylics. Willard’s favorite paintbrush is one hair from the back of a dead housefly!
For details like the ropes on a ship, he has found that cobwebs or strands of glue are perfect.

To move his sculptures, Willard uses an eyelash. But even that can be tricky. Once, while moving a microscopic Alice in Wonderland, his cell phone rang. Startled, Willard took in a quick breath. When he looked back, Alice was gone. After laboring for weeks to carve her, he’d accidentally inhaled her! Willard had to start all over again. Luckily, the second Alice was even better than the first.
Willard sculpts everything from famous people to fictional characters. His subjects range from dragons and polar bears to the Statue of Liberty.

Because of their beauty and rarity, his sculptures have made Willard a wealthy man. But he says, “Success isn’t about material things like an expensive watch or a costly ring; it’s about persevering and achieving your dreams.”
Willard Wigan, microsculptor, has done just that. The man who felt small as a boy has shown the world that something small can really be BIG.

options.label: Small art requires more talent and creativity than big art.
Willard found something that he loved and worked hard to succeed at it.
Art is an important way for kids to express themselves.
Willard was supported by his teachers and family to pursue art.

articles.title: Pasha's First Yom Kippur
articles.author: Carol Coven Grannick
articles.description: A boy prepares to celebrate his first Yom Kippur and must apologize to a neighbor.
articles.intro: Carol Coven Grannick has written for Highlights Magazine for Kids. In this short story, a boy prepares to celebrate his first Yom Kippur, a Jewish holiday which requires people to ask for forgiveness from others before it begins.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Pasha’s relationship with his grandpa.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “Rooshky, pooshky, gooshky!” Pasha heard David shout from across the lawn. Pasha sat on the front steps, making a list of people he would apologize to before his first Yom Kippur.
Pasha didn’t like David, who had teased him ever since Pasha’s family had arrived from Russia to live outside of Chicago with Dedushka , Grandpa.
“Pasha, listen — I’m talking Russian!”

“Be quiet, David!” Pasha snapped. Not only did David live next door, but he was in Pasha’s grade and went to the same synagogue. In Russia, Pasha had been taught nothing about his Jewish heritage. But here in America, Dedushka taught him many things. David isn’t acting the way Dedushka said people act before Yom Kippur, thought Pasha. David should be making a list, and I should be at the top of it!
When Dedushka had explained Yom Kippur, he’d said, “Before the start of Yom Kippur, we apologize to those we’ve wronged, and we forgive those who apologize to us.
Then, on Yom Kippur, Mama, Papa, and I will not eat. We will spend the day in the synagogue, trying to keep our minds on how to be better in the next year.”
David planted himself in front of Pasha. He stared at Pasha and acted as if Pasha couldn’t hear. “Do... you... understand... English... yet?”
“Stop it!” Pasha yelled.
“Stope eet!” David mimicked, then laughed.
Pasha threw down his list and shoved David backward into a row of bushes. “There! Now you’ll know I understand English!”

Pasha ran into Dedushka’s house. He felt better. He took a deep breath. Well, sort of better. What would Dedushka say if he found out what Pasha had done? Would he , Pasha, have to apologize to David ?

Now that he lived in America, Pasha wanted to be just like Dedushka, who observed Jewish customs and holidays. Still, once in a while, Pasha wanted to be back in his little village with his old friends, where they knew nothing about being Jewish. Especially now, before Yom Kippur, being Jewish seemed to be a lot of trouble.

Pasha looked out the window. David was walking back and forth on the grass between their two houses. David looked at the window and caught Pasha’s eyes. Pasha stared at David for a moment, then turned away. He would not let David tease him anymore.
“Dedushka,” Pasha said at breakfast the next morning. “If someone starts a problem, and then another person reacts in not such a nice way, does first person have to apologize before Yom Kippur?”

“I think, Pasha, that if something is not nice, it is not nice, whether first or second,” Dedushka said. “The second person shouldn’t wait for the first one to apologize, even if that one started the problem. Whoever does wrong, is wrong.”
Pasha sighed. He wished Dedushka had answered differently.
Pasha’s stomach turned this way and that on the way to the bus stop.
“Pasha!” David ran up from behind and pulled at Pasha’s sleeve.
Pasha pulled his arm away and sat far from David on the school bus. At least in school David sat on the opposite side of the room.
Still, during geography, Pasha could not remember where Canada was.
During math, Pasha missed four times three.
And during reading, Pasha thought about Yom Kippur.
When Pasha walked out to recess, he saw David standing by himself.
Would it count if Pasha said he was sorry for pushing but didn’t really feel sorry?

Pasha scrunched up in a corner of the playground and put his head down on his knees. “I’m sort of sorry. I’m maybe sorry. I’m pretty sorry,” he repeated to himself, to see how it sounded. I’m kind of sorry. I’m really sorry. I’m sorry.”
“You are?” a voice said. Pasha looked up. It was David. At first Pasha didn’t speak. Then he said, “Well... yes.”
“That’s funny,” David said.
“Because I was trying to apologize to you , but you didn’t let me.”
“You were?” Pasha looked at David closely.
David kicked the ground, put his hands in his pockets, and coughed. Then he looked at Pasha and said, “Yeah. I’m sorry. I was mean.”
“Yes, you were.” Pasha stood up and turned an angry face toward David. Then he saw that David’s eyes were sad, and he stopped feeling angry. “But I was, too,” Pasha said quietly.
“So you forgive me?” David asked.
“Well, do you forgive me ?” Pasha asked.
David smiled. “If you forgive me.”
“OK.” Pasha laughed.
“Do you want to sit together at Yom Kippur services tomorrow?” David asked. Pasha hesitated.
“OK.” Would he and David be friends? Pasha wasn’t sure. But he was sure that Dedushka would be proud of him, and that he was sure proud of himself. Now he felt ready for his first Yom Kippur.

options.label: Apologizing to someone can help you both move forward.
People who are mean shouldn't get apologies.
Moving to a new country can be scary and lonely.
Getting revenge on someone can make you feel better.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the short story?

articles.title: My Favorite Things
articles.author: Joy Cowley
articles.description: A narrator describes their favorite things to their grandmother.
articles.intro: Joy Cowley is a New Zealand author best known for her children's fiction. In this story, a narrator describes their favorite things to their grandmother.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes what types of things are special to the speaker.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: I said to Grandma, “Do you want to hear about my favorite things?”
“Sure, honey,” she said. “Go ahead and tell me.”
“My favorite color is yellow because it's like the sun and dandelions in summer. Sometimes it smells like lemons and sometimes like warm puppies. It's a happy color, and it makes me hum inside.

“My favorite time of the day is early morning, when the sun isn't up yet, and the grass looks as though it's just had a bath, and the trees smell new. No one is around except the birds and squirrels and rabbits on our lawn and me in my pajamas. The rest of the day is pretty good, but early morning is best.

”My favorite day of the year is my birthday because it is my own day made especially for me, and everyone in the family knows it. Sometimes I have a party with friends and a birthday cake and presents. But what I like best about my birthday is that in just one day I get to be a whole year older. Isn't that amazing?

“My favorite way of traveling is by train with Mom. I can look out the window at the world rushing past. I can walk up and down, pretending I'm the ticket person, or I can just sit with my head against Mom, and shut my eyes to hear the train talk to me. Clickety-clack. Clickety-clack. I guess my second best way of traveling is on the back of Uncle Jack's horse.

“My favorite thing in my bedroom is the patchwork quilt that you made, Grandma. Sometimes it's a country with hills and valleys and farms. Sometimes it's a tent in a desert. Sometimes it's a rabbit's burrow, where the rabbits go to hide from fierce wild animals. It's always a snuggly quilt, and when I pull it around my ears, I feel safe and warm.

“My favorite clothing is my jacket. It's yellow and red, and it's warm. It has six pockets, two on the inside and four on the outside. In the outside pockets I put crayons and money and notes from my teacher and my hands when they're cold. The inside pockets hold my treasures-the pink shell, the fossil stone, the acorn with a face painted on it. When my jacket gets too small, I'll give it away. But I'll keep the treasures.

“My favorite animal is our dog because she is licky at the front and waggy at the back and kind to everyone, and because she follows me anyplace I go. When I sit on the couch to watch TV, she lies with her head on my lap. She doesn't look at the movie. She just watches me all the time with big loving eyes. She is absolutely my favorite animal, but I wouldn't mind having a horse, too.

”My favorite game is country-and-western singing with my friend Jillian. We dress up and stand on the bed with a pretend microphone and Dad's old guitar. We sing real songs, and when we run out of words, we make up our own. I'm showing Jillian how to whistle, and she can nearly get her mouth right. We're both learning how to yodel. Oh-de.layee. Oh-de-layee. Oh-de-layee. Last week, Uncle Jack videotaped us. He says we are good enough for his TV.

“My favorite fruit is grapes. Sometimes I squeeze grapes, and the insides squish into my mouth. Sometimes I just crunch them up, skin and all-but not the seeds. Did you know that I can spit a grape seed from the mailbox clear out to the road?

“My favorite dinner is spaghetti with meatballs and tomato sauce. I pretend I'm eating worms and bugs. I chew up all the bugs, and then I suck up the worms. When they slide into my mouth, they sometimes flick on my nose, leaving a blob of red sauce, and Dad says, 'Stop playing with your food.'

“My favorite drink is lemonade slush. It has this sweet-and-sour taste that makes my tongue curl up at the edges. On a hot day it cools me better than anything, but I can't drink it too fast because the ice makes my head bones hurt. The best lemonade slush is the stuff we make together, Grandma, when you let me juice the lemons and grind up the ice, and we drink it together on the porch swing.
“Well, those are my favorite things,” I said. “Tell me, Grandma, what is your favorite thing in the whole wide world?”
”Why, honey, that is easy,” she said. “My very favorite thing is you.”

options.label: Things are made more special by the memories attached to them.
Children can appreciate the world more than adults.
Adults have a better understanding of familial love than children.
Children tend to find joy in simpler things than adults.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence best describes the theme of the story?

"The inside pockets hold my treasures-the pink shell, the fossil stone, the acorn with a face painted on it." (Paragraph 8)
"The best lemonade slush is the stuff we make together, Grandma, when you let me juice the lemons and grind up the ice, and we drink it together on the porch swing." (Paragraph 13)
"'Tell me, Grandma, what is your favorite thing in the whole wide world?'" (Paragraph 14)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the story best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It shows how much time the narrator spends imagining things.
It reveals how talented of a quilter the grandmother is.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Describe a favorite activity that you enjoy doing with your family. Why is it important to you?

While the narrator describes many favorite things, the grandmother only names one. Why does the author only have the grandmother share one of her favorite things? What does this show about the grandmother?

articles.title: Rock Like an Egyptian – How and Why the Pyramids Were Built
articles.author: Summer Blais
articles.description: Summer Blais explains the extraordinary feats of engineering behind ancient Egyptian pyramids.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Summer Blais explains the history behind ancient Egyptian pyramids which are large triangular structures, or buildings, made of stone.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how it took “clever thinking and hard work” for the ancient Egyptians to build the pyramids.
articles.permissions: Rock Like an Egyptian – How the Pyramids Were Built By Summer Blais. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The ancient Egyptians believed death was the beginning of a journey to another world. People who lived in a kingdom thought their afterlife was dependent on the pharaoh’s journey to the afterlife (a pharaoh was a king or queen in ancient Egypt). Because of this, every kingdom wanted to make sure, long before the pharaoh even died, that their final resting place — their tomb — was built just right, so that their journey to the afterlife was successful.

For more than 1,000 years, pyramids served as a final resting place for pharaohs. The triangle shape of a pyramid was a symbol for new life arising from a mound of earth. The place where the pharaoh would lay inside typically faced the west, because that is where the sun sets. Egyptians believed that the doorway to the afterlife lay where the sun set.
Picture a Pyramid – It’s Probably Bigger Than You Think

Pyramids came in a variety of sizes, but they were never small! The tallest remaining one that we know of today is called the Great Pyramid of Khufu at Giza. It was built in the 26th century B.C. It is 481.4 feet tall. That’s about 45 floors, or half the size of the Eiffel Tower in Paris. It was the tallest structure on Earth for more than 3,800 years. The base of the pyramid — the first floor — is 756 feet wide, which is longer than two football fields.

The rocks that were used to build the pyramids included limestone and granite. They were found in locations known as quarries, which were large rock pits in the desert. The Great Pyramid was built from more than two million limestone blocks and each block weighed around two tons, which is approximately the same weight as 20 baby elephants. Some other pyramids used blocks that were 50 tons each, which is heavier than seven full-sized elephants!
The ancient Egyptians didn’t have any of the construction tools or machines that we use today, so it took a lot of clever thinking and hard work for them to build the pyramids.
Teamwork and Tools to Get the Job Done

So, since pyramids were created before there were cranes or bulldozers — or even wheels or pulleys — how did the ancient Egyptians move these heavy rocks without machines? Well, even though they didn’t have modern tools, ancient Egyptians developed methods to make the work easier.

First, the Egyptians used tools like hammers and spades to dig up the rocks from the quarry. Once the rocks were unearthed, they were transported to the pyramid location. Some historians think the workers developed a way to transport big rocks using wooden planks arranged like train tracks and a wooden sled with rounded rails at the front. A large and heavy rock would be lassoed with a rope onto the sled and a dozen men would pull the sled along this “road.” Other men would constantly pour water on the tracks and on the sand to keep it moving smoothly.

However they managed it, once these huge rocks arrived at the pyramid site, another team used copper chisels, drills, and saws to cut the softer stones — like limestone — down into smaller bricks. The harder stones, like granite, were pounded into shape. The workmen would also break down the rocks by hammering grooves into the stone, inserting wooden wedges into the groves, then soaking the wooden wedges with water. As the water was absorbed, the wedges expanded, and the rocks would naturally break into more workable sizes.
Another team of men would then polish the rocks with rough quartz sand, so they were smooth and even, which made them look nice, but also made them easier to fit together.

And then, yet another team of men would set the blocks in place and secure them with a form of mortar. Mortar is like glue — it is used to stick rocks to other rocks. We still use forms of mortar today for buildings made of stone or brick. The ancient Egyptians would build layer upon layer of rocks and mortar until the pyramid was complete.
Worth the Work

Knowing all this, it shouldn’t be a surprise to learn that pyramid building was hard and dangerous work. It could take up to 20 years and roughly 20,000 workers to build just one pyramid! Therefore, it might blow your mind to learn there are over 100 known pyramids still in Egypt today. But pyramid building was very well regarded and workers were treated very well, so men would volunteer from all over Egypt to join pyramid building projects.

Whatever reasons the ancient Egyptians had for putting so many resources and so much manpower into building the pyramids, we can be grateful they did. These magnificent structures have lasted more than 4,000 years and given us an extraordinary glimpse into Egypt’s rich and glorious past.

options.label: Ancient Egyptian pyramids were amazingly large structures that have lasted thousands of years.
The whole kingdom worked together to build pyramids after a pharaoh died in ancient Egypt.
People who study the past still do not know how the ancient Egyptians built the pyramids.
The pyramids are amazing because nothing close to their size has ever been built today.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: Water helped the workers break the stones apart.

Water helped keep the workers from getting too tired while working. E. Water helped keep the wooden planks wet so the rocks could move easily. F. Water helped the workers polish the stones to make them fit together better.

options.label: to shrink up.

articles.title: The Tastiest Day of the Year: Pi Day
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Some people enjoy eating pie on Pi Day, a holiday that celebrates a special number.
articles.intro: In this informational text, India James writes about a special day called Pi Day.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the connections between math, Pi Day, and pie.
articles.permissions: "The Tastiest Day of the Year: Pi Day" by India James. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Have you ever heard of Pi Day? You might think this celebration is all about eating pie. But that’s only partly right. Pi Day is really about math!

A pie is a dessert with a filling that is baked inside of a crust or two. These sweet treats can come in many forms, ranging from fruit flavors like cherry and apple to crunchy textures like pecan. Sometimes, pies can even be a main course when they’re filled with meat or savory ingredients.
Any day is a great day to eat pie, but March 14 is a special day. It’s known around the world as Pi Day.
Pi Versus Pie
Pi Day started as a way to celebrate a special number.

Pi is both a letter in the Greek alphabet (π) and a number. The number pi is the result of dividing the circumference of a circle by its diameter. The circumference of a circle is the length of the line that makes up the circle. The diameter of a circle is the straight-line distance from one side of the circle to the other through the center. No matter how big or small of a circle you have, the result of dividing the circumference by the diameter will always be the same — the number pi!
Pi is one of a kind in mathematics. This number goes on for many digits. In fact, it goes on forever! And the numbers never repeat in a permanent pattern.
Because it is so long, many people shorten the number pi to its first three digits. The first three digits of pi are 3.14.
March is the third month of the year. The fourteenth day of March — or 3/14 — is known as Pi Day because the date matches with the first three digits of pi.
Pi Day is the perfect excuse to eat some pie!
Celebrating Pi Day
Brian Phillips is the owner of Mehaffies Pies in Dayton, Ohio. He’s been baking pies for over 30 years! Every year, he’ll make about 5,000 pies to help people celebrate Pi Day.

He says there’s a lot of math that goes into baking pies, which makes them a good match for Pi Day. “You have to get the right mixture and weight of flour and water,” says Phillips. “It needs to be done the same way every time.”

Measuring and understanding how to add the ingredients in the right amounts is crucial in making pies taste great. “It’s important to do the math right and the measurements correct in baking,” Phillips adds.
Pi and pie don’t just sound alike — math is important to both.
More Than Just Math
The idea of pi has been around since Babylon and ancient Egypt. That’s more than 4,000 years ago!

Celebrating Pi Day is a much more recent event. People have been eating pie on Pi Day since the 1980s according to Phillips. He first heard about the event from engineers who use math a lot in their jobs. “Engineers like to eat!” Phillips says. “So why not celebrate Pi Day with a piece of pie?”
These days, it’s not just engineers who look forward to March 14th. Pi Day has become so popular that Phillips says he has a customer that even wants to celebrate June 28th as Double Pi Day.
Baking pies and math go hand-in-hand. Pi Day is a great reason to eat a tasty treat!

options.label: Pi Day is a day to celebrate a special number by eating pies.
Pi Day was created by Brian Phillips as a way to sell more pies.
Pi Day is a holiday that has been around for more than 4,000 years.
Pi Day honors the day that the circumference of a circle was first discovered.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: the math that bakers use when making delicious pies
the day in March that is exactly halfway between winter and summer
the word that ancient Greek people used for the food we call pie today
the number you get by dividing the length around a circle by the length across a circle

questions.prompt: Which best describes what pi is?

options.label: They explain the order of months in a year.

options.label: It started as a celebration in Egypt and is now celebrated all over the world.

articles.title: More Than He Looked: Steph Curry
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Basketball star Steph Curry proved others wrong and became one of the most successful NBA players.
articles.intro: Stephen Curry is one of the greatest basketball players of all time. In this informational text, India James discusses Curry's early life and how he found success as a basketball player.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Steph Curry’s actions.
articles.permissions: "More Than He Looked: Steph Curry" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The Queensway Christian College boys’ basketball team hadn’t lost a game. They were so good that coach James Lackey had begun arranging games with big high schools in the area. The Queensway team beat them all.
But one day they played against a team from Toronto. The Queensway team was down by seven points with one minute left. Coach James called a timeout.
“Hey, let's be good sports whether we win or lose and shake hands and keep your heads up,” said Coach James.
He was going to keep speaking and telling them to learn from this experience. Except one boy interrupted him.
“We are not going to lose this game. Give me the ball and we will win,” the boy said.
That boy was Steph Curry. At 5 feet 2 inches, he didn’t look like much. He was skinny and almost a full head shorter than many of the other boys on his team.
But Coach James agreed. Their play would be to get Steph the ball.
Steph began sinking shots. It seemed like he would shoot from anywhere on the court, and he didn’t miss. When the buzzer rang, the Queensway team had won by seven points.
It was just the start for the small boy who would grow up to be a basketball legend.
Basketball beginnings
Steph Curry was born Wardell Stephen Curry II. He had basketball in his blood. His dad, Dell Curry, played for the NBA.

Steph grew up going to his father’s games. He and his brother, Seth, often played on the team’s practice court while his father’s team played on the main court. Steph and his brother liked to run across to the main court to see a few plays, then they rushed back to the practice court to imitate what they saw. All that time watching and practicing paid off. It was obvious to anyone who saw Steph handle a basketball that he had skills.
It was a good thing Steph had talent, because he didn’t look like a regular basketball player. He was short and skinny. Most people couldn’t believe that he was a star player when they first saw him.
But Steph learned to play to his strengths. He was fast, and he practiced and practiced until he became one of the fastest shooters.
“I want to practice to the point where it’s almost uncomfortable how fast you shoot, so that in the game things kind of slow down,” Steph said.

However, even after playing well in high school, many teams didn’t want him to play for them in college. Steph had wanted to play for Virginia Tech, like his dad did. But all Virginia Tech offered him was a chance to play as a walk-on. That meant that he wouldn’t receive an athletic scholarship.
Steph decided to play for Davidson College. Their basketball team wasn’t as famous, but the coach at Davidson saw a spark in Steph.
“Wait until you see Stephen Curry,” the Davidson coach told everyone else.
He knew that Steph was going to be a star.
Keeping playing

Steph averaged 21.5 points per game in 2006-2007, his first year at Davidson. That was more than any other first-year player in the country! In his second year, he and his team made the quarterfinals of the NCAA March Madness basketball championship tournament. The next year, he was the top scorer in the entire country.

Steph was selected by the Golden State Warriors in the 2009 NBA draft. Five years later, he was voted the Most Valuable Player out of all the players on all the teams in the NBA. That same year, Steph helped his team win their first NBA championship in 40 years.

Since then, Steph has won the MVP award one more time, helped his team win the championships three more times, and been selected to the NBA All-Star team nine times. Steph is recognized as the best shooter in the history of the game. No one has scored as many three-point shots as him. Basketball today is played with more of a focus on three-point shots because of Steph.
The short, skinny boy practiced and practiced, and he used his speed and shooting skills to become one of the greatest players of all time.

options.label: Curry became famous for being one of the tallest and fastest players in the NBA.
Curry used the success of his father to help him become a famous basketball player.
Curry's many mistakes in high school and college almost ended his basketball career.
Curry's hard work and natural talent helped him prove to others that he could be a basketball star.

"'We are not going to lose this game. Give me the ball and we will win,' the boy said." (Paragraph 5)
"Steph grew up going to his father's games." (Paragraph 11)

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the context of the text, how did Steph Curry become a star in basketball? What do you think made Steph succeed? What do you think was Steph's biggest achievement? Why?

Steph Curry faced criticism about his appearance in his effort to become a professional basketball player. What do you think would have happened if Curry had believed the negative comments people were making about him? Why do you think it is important for people to believe in themselves, even if no one else does?

Steph Curry credits his success to the people who supported him, like his family and coaches. Think of a time when a family member, friend, or coach helped you feel successful or supported you through a difficult time. What piece of advice did they give you that helped you work through a difficult time and how could their advice help others? As an extension activity, have students write a note of gratitude to the person who helped them through a difficult time in their life.

Steph Curry had big dreams for himself as a young athlete. What is a dream that you hope to succeed in making true one day? What can you learn from Curry's experiences that can help you accomplish this?

articles.title: Were Dinosaurs Good Parents?
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler explains that some dinosaurs stayed with their offspring after laying eggs.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes what scientists have learned about dinosaurs' parenting skills.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how dinosaurs cared for their young.
articles.permissions: Were Dinosaurs Good Parents? By T. J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Reptiles aren’t known for their parenting skills. Most species lay their eggs and then just leave. Sometimes the eggs are laid in a nicely prepared spot, sometimes not. After the babies hatch, they’re usually on their own.

Sure, there are exceptions: Some snakes wrap themselves around a clutch of eggs to keep the eggs warm. Crocodile mothers guard their eggs, help the babies out of their eggshells, and hang around for months until the little crocs are ready to face the world on their own. A crocodile mom might even give her babies a ride to nearby water — safely inside her gigantic mouth!
Still, compared to mammals and birds — which feed, bathe, protect and care for their babies for a really long time — reptiles don’t win any parenting prizes.

So when scientists first discovered dinosaurs and identified them as reptiles, they assumed that dinos fell into the “lay them and leave them” style of parenting. In fact, when scientists in the 1920s found a fossil of a small dinosaur near a nest of eggs, they named it Oviraptor , which means “egg thief.” They figured the little dino was feasting on another dinosaur’s eggs.

They were wrong. Later research (and more fossil finds) showed that Oviraptor was actually a devoted parent, according to the University of California Museum of Paleontology. It wasn’t stealing other dinosaurs’ eggs; it was taking care of its own! Paleontologists — the scientists who study life long ago — have since found Oviraptor skeletons on top of nests, protecting and warming their eggs just like modern birds do.

In the late 1970s, the famous paleontologist Jack Horner and his colleagues found evidence showing how devoted some dinosaurs were to their offspring. They studied a place where fossils of a duck-billed dinosaur were found in all stages of development: eggs, hatchlings, juveniles, and adults. Year after year, the dinosaurs returned to that place — their nesting site — to lay their eggs and raise their young. By studying all the fossils, the scientists realized that the babies were born too weak to survive on their own. So the parents brought food back to the nest and cared for the hatchlings until they were big enough to move along with the herd. Horner chose the perfect name for the dinosaur: Maiasaura , which means “good mother lizard.”

Since the discovery of Maiasaura , paleontologists have discovered several other good-parent dinosaurs. Though some were as small as modern-day pheasants or turkeys, others were huge. One dino could weigh as much as three horses. That raised an important question: How could a dinosaur sit on a clutch of eggs to keep them warm without crushing them? Dinosaurs came up with a brilliant solution: They laid their eggs in a ring, leaving a bare spot in the middle, so most of the dino’s weight rested on the bare ground. They could then spread their arms, which were probably feathered, around the eggs.

So were all dinosaurs good parents? Hard to say. Scientists are still looking for more clues about some dinosaurs’ parenting style. Some dinos — such as the huge, long-necked sauropods — may have been too big to care for their tiny hatchlings. They wouldn’t want to risk stepping on them! Other dinosaur species probably laid their eggs and left. Fortunately, in those species, the dinosaur babies — like baby sea turtles today — hatched from their eggs ready to run and take care of themselves. And, of course, some dinosaurs were devoted parents. They took care of eggs and hatchlings that could not fend for themselves.

It wasn’t only the mothers that took care of their babies. In some dinosaur species, such as Oviraptor and Troodon , the daddy dinos probably did a lot of the parenting work: guarding the nest, keeping the eggs warm, and feeding the hatchlings. They did this so that the mother’s total energy could be focused on producing and laying the eggs. When the fathers did a lot of the parenting, the mothers laid more eggs — maybe as many as 20 to 30 at a time. That was a good way to make sure more of their offspring survived.

As they discover new fossils, scientists are learning more about dinosaurs’ lives and parenting styles. As paleontologist Michael J. Benton has noted, one thing is clear: Dinosaurs lived lives and behaved in ways that were more complicated than we originally thought. When it came to taking care of their young, they were much more like modern birds than the “lay them and leave them” reptiles that people first thought them to be.

options.label: The mother dinosaurs did most of the parenting, and the fathers left once the eggs hatched.
All dinosaurs laid eggs and cared for their young, and some even stayed together as a family.
Scientists have discovered that some dinosaurs stayed with their eggs and took care of their young.
Dinosaurs had a difficult time finding a good spot to lay eggs, so many of them left their eggs behind.

articles.title: Beyond Beautiful: Ancient Egyptian Cosmetics
articles.author: Summer Blais
articles.description: Many of the beauty and hygiene practices used today come from Ancient Egypt.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Summer Blais explains the Ancient Egyptians' use of cosmetics, or beauty products made to clean or change the face, skin, hair, nails, and teeth.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how cosmetics used by Ancient Egyptians shaped today’s cosmetics.
articles.permissions: Beyond Beautiful: Ancient Egyptian Cosmetics By Summer Blais. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: If you have seen a movie, television program, or even a cartoon featuring the Egyptian Queen Cleopatra, I bet she’s probably wearing a lot of makeup. Cleopatra is typically portrayed with bright red lips, heavy blue or green eyeshadow that covers her entire eyelid, dark brows, and even blush on her cheeks. All of that’s not just for show — I mean, it is, but it’s also historically accurate. Cosmetics were a common part of Ancient Egypt’s culture, and date back as early as the Predynastic period (c. 6000 - c. 3150 BCE). Makeup was routinely worn by both men and women during this period. It was not only used to enhance beauty, but it also had a practical 7 purpose as well. The Egyptian word "Tutankhamun Artifact" by Robert Thiemann is for “makeup palette” derives 8 from their word licensed under CC0. for “to protect.” Ancient Egyptians lined their eyes with smoky kohl — like modern-day eyeliner — to protect their eyes from the sun’s rays. Kohl is a powdery substance made from an ore called galena and lining their eyes with it had 1. the queen of Egypt from 51-30 BCE 2. to show in a picture 3. showing something as it actually was in the past 4. arts, entertainment, and fashion that is popular with many people during a time period 5. regularly; normally 6. to add to the quality or beauty of something 7. Practical(adjective)useful; ordinary 8. to come from 9. Ore(noun)a rock or mineral the same effect as the black under-eye protectant that many athletes use today. They also used blue and green eyeshadow, but that was usually ornamental and saved for special occasions. Hello Cleopatra! In addition to all this eyework, Ancient Egyptians paid just as much attention to what was below the nose too, and applied tinted ointments to their lips — like lipstick and lip gloss. This not only added color to their mouths, but also served to protect and heal sun- chapped lips. Did you know there are many beauty and hygiene practices that we use today that derive from ancient practices? Today, you can get products made of grainy salt to rub onto your skin to help remove dead skin cells. This process is called exfoliation and it makes your skin smoother and fresher. Ancient Egyptians had a similar process using Dead Sea salts to exfoliate their skin. They would then take baths in milk to soften their skin. Take a trip to your local beauty store and you’ll find a variety of face lotions and masks to help your skin look younger and feel softer. Ancient Egyptians would make similar face masks by mixing milk and honey, as well as using honey and sugar to wax off excess hair. They used a variety of oils to help soften their skin, fade scars, and prevent wrinkling — like modern day face creams. These oils were practical, too, as they protected them from sunburn and warded off sand flies and other insects. The oils usually smelled good, because they would come from plants with strong odors, such as lavender, peppermint, thyme, cedar, rose, and almonds. With these scented oils, they also created variations of deodorant, perfume, breath mints, and toothpaste. The Egyptians took their hygiene very seriously. Everyone wore make-up in Egypt during these ancient times. It didn’t matter if you were a king or queen, or just a regular, everyday person. However, royal families would typically have their own makeup artists and manicurists. The Egyptian term for makeup artist derives from the word for “write or engrave,” which suggests that being a makeup artist back then required a great deal of skill. These makeup artists — or servants — would mix the components to make the cosmetics, and then apply it using various brushes. Henna was also used too. It is a dye made from a plant known as a henna tree. There is evidence that Egyptians used henna to add highlights to their hair. It can also act as a temporary tattoo, and they would use henna to decorate their skin with elaborate designs.

Religion was also another reason why Ancient Egyptians wore makeup. The Book of Dead, a collection of texts that includes spells and instructions to help a citizen’s journey from life to the underworld and afterlife, claimed that a person must be well presented when entering the 10. used only for decoration 11. Hygiene(noun)the practice of keeping clean to stay healthy 12. Variety(noun)a collection; a number of different things in a group 13. more than what is needed 14. slightly different forms of something 15. a person who gives manicures, or cuts and paints others’ nails, as a job 16. parts of something afterlife. As death could come at any time, people were expected to look their finest whenever possible. Cosmetic items were frequently placed in tombs to accompany people on their journey into the afterlife. It’s worth noting that although the makeup Egyptians used seemed very similar to what we use today, the ingredients were very different. The eyeliners they used were made of ore, carbon, lead, and sulfite. The eyeshadows came from minerals like malachite. The red lipsticks were made of ochre mixed with animal fat or vegetable oil. It’s said that Cleopatra would crush beetles to get a deeper red for her lips. They also made lipsticks that mixed the dye from iodine and bromine mannite – which was poisonous. Perhaps that’s where we got the phrase “kiss of death.”

options.label: Only wealthy and royal people in Ancient Egypt wore cosmetics.

Ancient Egyptians used cosmetics for sun protection and to look beautiful.

articles.title: A Sweet Invention
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: This text tells the story of how Ruth Wakefield invented the chocolate chip cookie.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains how a popular treat came to be.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people learned about Ruth’s cookies.
articles.permissions: "A Sweet Invention" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Ruth Wakefield wanted a cookie, but not just any cookie. Ruth wanted something new. Something different. And she was just the person to create it.

In the 1930s, Ruth and her husband owned the Toll House restaurant in Whitman, Massachusetts. Toll House was a well-known stop on the way between Boston and Cape Cod. Actors, singers, and other celebrities of the time all ate there. Ruth was in charge of the restaurant’s great food.

Ruth had studied cooking in college, and she was good at it. People said that all Ruth had to do was taste a dish, and then she could make it without even looking at a recipe. She was always learning and trying new things. Every January, Ruth and her husband took a trip overseas to try foods in different countries, such as France and Egypt.

Toll House was especially known for its desserts. Ruth made lemon meringue pie, Boston cream pie, and gingerbread. The restaurant served a thin butterscotch cookie, but Ruth wanted to create something brand-new. On the way home from one of their trips overseas, she planned out a new recipe.

At that time, chocolate was used in desserts, but it was always melted and mixed in. What if Ruth could use chocolate a different way? She thought back to a college class on cooking where she learned how foods work together. She also recalled her past experiments with chocolate.

Back home, Ruth ordered bars of semi-sweet chocolate from the grocery store. Then she used an ice pick to break the chocolate bars into pieces and added the bits to cookie dough. She tried batch after batch until she had one she liked. Ruth named her new cookie the Chocolate Crunch Cookie.

Chocolate Crunch Cookies were a hit! Customers at her restaurant loved them so much they asked her to make them at home. They shared the cookies with their friends. People wrote to Ruth and asked her for the recipe. She was happy to give it to them.

Ruth’s cookies grew even more famous after she was invited on a radio show to talk about them. Then the recipe was printed in the newspaper. The cookies were so popular that the chocolate bar company saw their sales go up 500% in the region where Ruth lived! In 1939, the company asked Ruth if they could use her recipe and the Toll House name on their chocolate bars. Ruth agreed.

Everyone who made the cookies from Ruth’s recipe had to break up the chocolate bars the way Ruth did. It wasn’t always easy — Ruth’s daughter remembered her mother’s hands turning red and sore from chopping up chocolate when making a big batch of cookies for the restaurant. Then, in 1940, the chocolate bar company made chocolate chips for the first time and put them on sale with Ruth’s recipe on the bag.

Nobody had seen anything like chocolate chip cookies. Other companies began making their own chocolate chips and cookies. Chocolate chip cookie mix appeared on store shelves. Ruth’s cookies became popular and stayed that way. Chocolate chip cookies are still named as one of people’s favorite types of cookies.

Ruth retired in 1967 and sold her restaurant. Today, the Toll House name and Ruth’s recipe still appear on bags of chocolate chips and on chocolate chip cookie products. Her skill at cooking and experimenting with new tastes gave us one of our favorite treats. The next time you enjoy a chocolate chip cookie, thank Ruth Wakefield.

options.label: Chocolate chip cookies started out as a healthy snack.
There are many different kinds of cookies that people like.
Ruth Wakefield was a cook who invented the first chocolate chip cookie.
The Chocolate Crunch Cookie was the first cookie served by the Toll House restaurant.

articles.title: Was T. Rex Scaly — or Fluffy?
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler explains why paleontologists have changed their minds about what T.Rex looked like.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes what scientists have learned about the Tyrannosaurus rex .
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how paleontologists’s ideas about T.Rex have changed over time.
articles.permissions: "Was T. Rex Scaly — or Fluffy?" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Any dino lover can picture a Tyrannosaurus rex . After all, the massive meat-eater is one of the most popular dinosaurs — and the star of blockbuster movies. T. rex was a scaly, brownish-gray giant whose loud roar could rumble the Earth and drown out an entire pride of lions, right?
Probably not.

The “king of the tyrant lizards” — that’s what “Tyrannosaurus rex” means — more likely had a mullet of fluffy, maybe even colorful feathers on its head, neck and tail. And those loud roars? Forget about them. T. rex probably made deep booming, honking or grunting sounds like a modern-day emu.

How could we have been so wrong for so long about what T. rex was like? The short answer is: We didn’t know any better. Up through the 1980s, T. rex skeletons were pretty rare finds, so we didn’t have much to go on. When there’s not much evidence, scientists have to figure things out based on what they already know.

Until the 1840s, nobody knew that any dinosaurs had ever existed. So a huge bone discovered in the 1670s was believed to have come from a man — a giant man! Bird-like tracks found in the early 1800s were called the “tracks of Noah’s raven,” referring to the biblical account of Noah sending out birds in search of dry land. And large tooth-shaped fossils discovered in 1822 were identified as coming from something like a super-sized iguana. Wrong, wrong, wrong.

By 1841, British scientist Richard Owen realized that these fossils had come from extinct animals the likes of which we’d never seen before. He named them “Dinosauria,” which means “terrible lizards.”

Figuring out exactly how these ancient animals looked or moved around was difficult. Paleontologists, the scientists who study life long ago, learn a lot by studying fossils and comparing their anatomy to other, living animals with similar features. But through the mid-1900s, they had few fossils to study. Based on the bits of discovered dinosaur skeletons and their knowledge of reptiles, early paleontologists believed dinosaurs plodded along with their tails dragging behind them. In fact, dinosaurs seemed so ill-suited to life on Earth that scientists initially believed they died out on their own. (Now we know a massive asteroid impact led to their extinction.)

Early depictions of T. rex —even in famous natural science museums — fit this image. In art and exhibits, the dinosaurs were posed standing upright with their tails dragging on the ground like monstrous kangaroos. (And before a complete T. re x skull was found, their heads and mouths were drawn ridiculously small, too.)

In the 1960s and ’70s, some paleontologists began to realize that they had gotten T. rex really wrong. The king of the tyrant lizards would not be able to hold itself upright without dislocating its hips and maybe even breaking some tail bones. Scientists began to think that, instead of being colossal, cold-blooded, lumbering lizards, dinosaurs probably were warm-blooded and more bird-like: agile and lively — but way more ferocious. They realized that T. rex moved in a stalking posture with its body nearly parallel to the ground and its long tail suspended in air to balance its huge, fearsome head. Museums everywhere had to change their exhibits.

Since the early 1990s, paleontologists have discovered many more dinosaur fossils, including another dozen T. rex skeletons and several other tyrannosaur relatives. They also gained a better understanding of evolution and comparative anatomy — knowing how joints can and cannot move in any animal — and what life was like during the Mesozoic Era when dinosaurs lived. Scientists also developed new tools to study dinosaurs. They made 3D scans of fossil bones and used computers to learn more about how the dinosaurs were put together and might be able to move.

Still, for more than a century, scientists had little evidence suggesting that dinosaurs looked much different from their scaly reptilian relatives on the outside. Soft tissues, including skin, decompose quickly after an animal dies and rarely get preserved in fossils. But in the 1990s, paleontologists in China found a treasure trove of amazingly well preserved fossils that changed our entire understanding of dinosaurs.

Many dinosaurs had feathers! Paleontologists found feathers not just on the little avian dinosaurs that evolved into today’s birds but even on ferocious tyrannosaurs — though nobody found a T. rex fossil with feathers (yet). Not that they haven’t tried. Some paleontologists examined fossilized impressions of an adult T. rex’s skin from parts of its stomach, chest, neck, pelvis, and tail. It was scaly. Period. No traces of feathers.
Does that mean that T. rex was not one of our feathered friends? Nope.

Scientists believe that a T. rex baby, which was about the size of a small turkey, had an entire coat of downy feathers to keep it warm. But as it grew bigger — which it did at an alarmingly fast rate — the little fluff ball lost most of its feathers. By the time a T. rex was fully grown, it was mainly scaly.

To figure this out, scientists relied not just on the fossils they found but also on their understanding of evolution and how animals adapt to their environments. T. rex evolved to be much bigger than its predecessors, and — like other big animals — it undoubtedly got hot when it dashed to catch its next dinner. Having a full coat of feathers would have been a disadvantage, because it would not have let the T. rex cool down.

But T. rex was hardly bald. It still sported patches of feathers on its head, neck, tail and maybe back. Those feathers were just for show. Like modern birds—which are considered “avian dinosaurs”—many dinosaurs long ago used displays to attract their mates. They showed off their flashy feathers and their best dance moves. (And they made sounds much more like bird-like booms and grunts than lion roars.)
T. rex was probably no exception. It likely flaunted its feathers by waving its tail and even prancing around like a bird. That’s quite a different depiction from the lumbering lizard of the 1800s!

options.label: The first people who found a dinosaur fossil thought it was from a scary and dangerous animal.
The way people imagine T.Rex from movies is not what they really looked and sounded like.
Museums all over the world are still teaching kids the wrong information about T.Rex.
Scientists think dinosaurs were very different from modern day birds.
